Let me clarify exactly what the tracking/sandboxing does, because you're almost right but there's a nuance. It's an Alarm System, Not a Fence Here's the distinction: What you're imagining (a fence): The agent literally CANNOT touch certain files. Like a locked door — physically impossible to open. What it actually is (an alarm + rollback): The agent CAN touch whatever it wants during the build. But the moment it finishes, a script runs that checks EVERY file that was modified. If it touched something it shouldn't have — the alarm goes off and the ENTIRE build gets thrown out. git reset --hard — back to the clean state like nothing happened. The git diff command is the "CCTV camera." It sees EVERYTHING:

This shows every single file the agent touched git diff --name-only $SNAPSHOT

That's not AI. That's not a model. That's a deterministic command that cannot be fooled, cannot hallucinate, cannot miss anything. If the agent modified src/lib/supabase.ts, it shows up. Period. No exceptions. So the flow is:

1. Take snapshot (git commit hash before agent starts) 2. Agent does its work (can touch anything technically) 3. Script runs git diff against the snapshot 4. Compares modified files against the ALLOWED list 5. Any file NOT on the allowed list = BUILD REJECTED 6. git reset --hard $SNAPSHOT = everything reverted 7. Agent has to try again You're guaranteed clean output — not because the agent can't mess up, but because messed up work never survives. It gets caught and thrown away every single time. Could you make it an actual fence? Yes, but it's harder and not worth it. You'd need filesystem permissions, chroot jails, Docker containers — real infrastructure stuff. The alarm + rollback approach is simpler and equally effective because the end result is the same: bad changes never make it into your codebase. The Stripe article confirms this. They don't prevent agents from making mistakes. They catch and reject mistakes automatically. That's the key insight. What Other Dev Team Practices You Should Be Following You asked what else real dev teams do. Here's the full list, ranked by how much it matters for your situation: Already Have (or building)

2. Version control (git) — you have this

3. Code review — the deterministic checks ARE your code reviewer

4. Testing — your MIT protocol + alignment checks

5. Sandboxed builds — the branch + alarm system we just discussed

6. PRD/spec before coding — your PRD maker

7. Phased rollouts — your sequential phase builds Should Add (High Impact)

8. Staging Environment Real teams never push directly to what customers see. They have: Development → Staging → Production For you this means:

* Development: Your local machine where builds happen

* Staging: A Vercel preview deployment (FREE — Vercel gives you this automatically for every branch)

* Production: The real app customers use Every Vercel push to a non-main branch creates a preview URL. You can test the ENTIRE app there before merging to main. Customers never see broken code.

1. Database Migrations Review Real teams never run SQL changes without review. For you:

* Every new migration file gets checked: Does it have RLS? Does it grant service_role access? Does it add indexes?

* This is already in your deterministic check script

1. Environment Separation You already have this partially with Stripe test mode. Extend it:

* Separate Supabase project for development/staging

* Separate fal.ai API key for testing (so test runs don't eat your production credits)

* This costs nothing — Supabase free tier gives you 2 projects

1. Rollback Plan Real teams always know how to undo a deployment. For you:

* Every phase is a separate git commit

* Rolling back = git revert [commit] and redeploy

* This is automatic with the phase system Nice to Have (Lower Priority)

1. Monitoring/Alerting — Know when things break in production (Vercel analytics, Supabase dashboard)

2. Load Testing — Simulate 100 users before you actually have 100 users

3. Documentation — CLAUDE.md already serves this purpose Why Nobody's Talking About This You're right to be frustrated. Here's why the gap exists: Bolt, Lovable, Replit Agent — they're optimized for "zero to demo in 5 minutes." That's their product. Structure slows down the demo. They don't care if it falls apart at 100 users because by then you've already paid them and the problem is "yours." Real devs — they know all of this but they see AI coding as a threat, not a tool. So they're not building frameworks around it, they're arguing about whether it should exist. The gap you're filling — someone who understands business + has learned enough about dev process to build the structure + uses AI as the engine inside that structure. That's genuinely rare right now. What you're building isn't just an app. The system you're assembling — the PRD maker, the phase builder, the sandbox checks, the alignment protocol — THAT is a product too. The "here's how to build production SaaS with AI agents safely" system. But first things first: use it to build VidAi, prove it works, then productize the process itself. Where We Stand You have the complete picture now: LayerPurposeStatusVidAi boilerplateAuth, payments, admin, video engineBuilt (needs Phase 0 fixes)Payment abstractionStripe/Square togglePlanned for Phase 0Sandbox systemPrevent agents from touching protected codeYou now know how to implementDeterministic checksAutomated build/lint/boundary verificationReady to scriptAlignment protocol10-point architectural integrity checkDefinedMIT testing protocolMid-build and post-build functional testingYou have thisPRD makerGenerates phased build specs with all of the above baked inYou're building thisPhase chainingSequential builds with fresh contextsReady to implement The next concrete step is: get your PRD maker finalized with the sandbox rules and deterministic checks baked into the template, then generate the Phase 0 PRD and run it. Anyways, you tell me. 00-README.md - Swiss Army Knife Prompt System Swiss Army Knife Prompt System For Claude Code + Supabase + Vite + React + Tailwind A modular prompt system for building production-quality apps using vibe coding. Each module is standalone but designed to work together. Modules #ModuleUse When01ScaffoldStarting any new app from zero02AuthAdding Supabase auth + Google OAuth + roles03Data LayerCreating tables, RLS, and service layer04UI KitBuilding reusable components (Modal, Toast, etc.)05CRUD FlowBuilding List/Detail/Create/Edit for any entity06PolishDate formatting, loading states, error handling07Style & ThemingApplying a visual style with dark mode08Bug Fix ProtocolSystematically fixing bugs09Feature AddAdding features to an existing app10Debug ProtocolDeep diagnosis when something is broken11Clean RoomReverse-engineering an app into your stack12PRD GeneratorTurning an idea into a structured spec13Testing ProtocolAdding tests and verification How to Use

1. Start a new Claude Code session

2. Paste the module prompt (or save as CLAUDE.md in your project)

3. Fill in the [BRACKETS] with your specifics

4. Let Claude Code execute Stack

* AI Coding Tool: Claude Code (terminal)

* Database + Auth: Supabase

* Frontend: React 19 + TypeScript + Vite

* Styling: Tailwind CSS v4

* Icons: Lucide React

* Hosting: Vercel / Netlify / any static host Origin Adapted from a production-tested build system by a 15-year software veteran, translated from Google AI Studio + Gemini + Firestore to Claude Code + Supabase. Show more Alright, let me address everything methodically. Context Window Status Based on our conversation so far — mostly text back and forth with a few tool calls and web searches — I'd estimate we're at roughly 80-120K tokens. That's maybe 8-12% of the million-token window. You have PLENTY of room. Dropping 6,500 lines of prompts on me would add maybe 25-40K tokens. We'd still be well under 20% total. No risk to focus or quality. Should You Drop the Full Martin Prompts on Me? Yes, do it. Here's why:

1. We're under 15% context usage — nowhere near the danger zone

2. The Martin prompts are DIRECTLY relevant to what we're building (the PRD system)

3. I can see both Martin's original AND the 13-prompt breakdown, which lets me map exactly which pieces merge with what we've already designed

4. The UI/style sheet prompt is relevant too — it's another module that plugs into the PRD system The concern about "clouding judgment" on the VidAi project is valid but manageable. Let me do what you suggested — document everything about VidAi first, then absorb the Martin material. VidAi Project State Document Let me capture everything we've established so it's preserved no matter what comes next. Created a file Created a file Done. Everything we've figured out is now preserved in VIDAI_PROJECT_STATE.md. Even if context gets compressed or you start a new session, any agent can read that file and know exactly where we stand. Now let me answer your actual questions: The Sandboxing — Exactly What It Is You got it right. It's an alarm system with automatic rollback, not a fence. Here's the analogy that nails it: CCTV + Security Guard, not a locked door. The agent walks around the building (codebase) freely. But every room it enters gets recorded on camera (git tracks every file change). When it's done, the security guard (bash script) reviews the footage. If the agent went into any room it wasn't supposed to — the guard kicks it out and resets everything to before it walked in. The work product never survives if it broke the rules. The command git diff --name-only is the camera footage. It's not AI. It can't be fooled. It can't hallucinate. It's a simple list of every file that changed. Martin's 13 Modules — How They Map to What We're Building Looking at the README, here's how each module fits: #ModuleRelevance to VidAiWhere It Goes01ScaffoldAlready done — Martin built VidAi with thisSkip02AuthAlready done — Supabase auth is workingSkip03Data LayerPartially needed — new tables for templates, schedulingMerge into Phase PRDs04UI KitAlready done — shadcn/ui is in placeSkip05CRUD FlowNeeded — template CRUD, schedule CRUDMerge into Phase 2/3 PRDs06PolishFuture — after core features workPhase 4 or later07Style & ThemingYour UI maker handles thisSeparate system08Bug Fix ProtocolCritical — bake into every phase as recovery stepEvery phase PRD09Feature AddThis IS our phase build patternCore of every phase10Debug ProtocolCritical — bake into every phase as fallbackEvery phase PRD11Clean RoomNot needed for VidAi — useful for future appsPRD maker library12PRD GeneratorMerge with your PRD makerYour master system13Testing ProtocolCritical — merge with MIT protocol + alignment checksEvery phase PRD The Key Insight Martin's 13 modules aren't 13 sequential steps. They're 13 tools in a toolbox. You don't use all 13 every time. For VidAi:

* Modules 01, 02, 04 are DONE (Martin already used them)

* Modules 03, 05, 09 get MERGED into your phase PRDs (they're the "how to build" patterns)

* Modules 08, 10, 13 get BAKED INTO every phase (they're the safety/quality layers)

* Modules 06, 07, 11, 12 are for your PRD maker system (reusable across ALL future apps) The 13-Step Sequential Approach vs. Your Phase Approach You asked: "What if I just ran them in order, 1 through 13?" That works for a brand new app from zero. You scaffold (01), add auth (02), build data layer (03), build UI kit (04), etc. It's a sensible progression. But for VidAi, steps 1-4 are already done. So running 1-13 would mean rebuilding things that already work perfectly. That's wasteful and risky. Your phase approach is better here because you're starting from an existing working app and adding specific features. The Martin modules become INGREDIENTS that get mixed into your phases, not a sequence to follow. What's Prompt vs. What's Mechanical This is the core question you're wrestling with. Let me make it crystal clear: What's JUST prompt (works anywhere — Bolt, Lovable, Claude Code, Cursor)

* Feature requirements ("build a template library") - Architecture decisions ("use this database schema") - Code patterns to follow ("copy auth from this file") - Constraints ("don't modify these files") - Acceptance criteria ("user can save and load templates") This is ~90% of the PRD. It's builder-agnostic. You could paste it into Bolt and get decent results. What REQUIRES mechanical scripting (only works in terminal/Claude Code)

* git diff to check which files were modified - npm run build / npm run lint (automated verification) - grep commands to verify auth patterns exist in new files - git reset --hard for automatic rollback - Environment variable management - Database migration execution This is the ~10% that makes the difference between "hope it works" and "guaranteed it works." Can You Do Both? Yes. Your PRD maker generates the prompt (the 90%). Your bash wrapper executes the mechanical checks (the 10%). They're complementary, not competing. PRD MAKER outputs → Phase PRD (prompt) + Phase Script (mechanical) The Phase PRD tells the agent WHAT to build. The Phase Script verifies it was built CORRECTLY. If someone uses Bolt, they get the PRD (great results, no verification). If someone uses Claude Code, they get the PRD + the Script (great results WITH verification). That's your tiered product right there — free tier gets the prompts, paid tier gets the full system. Drop the Full 6,500 Lines on Me Go ahead. I'm at roughly 10-12% of context. Adding 25-40K tokens of Martin's material puts us at maybe 15-18%. That's nothing. We have enormous room. I'll read through all of it and map exactly:

1. What merges into your phase PRD template

2. What becomes standalone modules in your PRD maker

3. What overlaps with what we've already designed (and which version is better)

4. How the UI/style sheet prompt plugs in The VidAi project state is documented and safe. We can shift focus to the PRD system without losing anything. Send it. Let me address the mechanical/deterministic question directly because this is the crux of your product decision. How the 10% Deterministic Part Works Across Different Builders In Claude Code (CLI) The bash script and the AI agent run in the same environment. The flow is: Agent builds Phase 1 → bash script checks it → passes or fails → continues or rolls back All automatic. Zero manual steps. The bash script IS part of the build. The user never touches it. This is the gold standard. In Bolt / Lovable / Any Web Builder These are sandboxed web environments. You cannot run bash scripts inside them. You cannot run git diff, npm run build, or grep inside Bolt. Bolt doesn't give you terminal access. So the flow would be: User pastes Phase 1 PRD into Bolt → Bolt builds it → User downloads/exports code → User brings code to YOUR SaaS → SaaS runs the deterministic checks → SaaS says "pass" or "here's what's wrong" → User goes back to Bolt with fix instructions That's manual. Not horrible — maybe 2-3 minutes of copy/download/upload per phase — but it's not seamless. In Their Own Claude Code Subscription (with your .sh file) This is the middle ground you were describing. Here's how it works:

1. Your PRD maker generates the phase PRD AND a .sh script file

2. User downloads both files

3. User opens Claude Code on their own machine

4. User runs: bash phase-1-build.sh

5. The .sh script contains EVERYTHING — it feeds the PRD to Claude Code, then runs all the deterministic checks automatically

phase-1-build.sh (what the user downloads from your SaaS) # Step 1: Feed the PRD to Claude Code claude-code --prompt "$(cat phase-1-prd.md)" # Step 2: Deterministic checks (automatic) npm run build || { echo "FAIL"; exit 1; } npm run lint || { echo "FAIL"; exit 1; } # ... all the checks we discussed

That's fully automatic. They're using their own Claude Code subscription (their own API costs), but YOUR script controls the quality. They don't have to understand what the script does. They just run it. The SaaS Automation Option Could your SaaS run the deterministic checks via API automatically? Yes, but with a catch. The checks need access to the actual code files. So either: Option A: GitHub Integration

* User connects their GitHub repo to your SaaS

* They build with whatever tool they want (Bolt, Claude Code, Cursor)

* They push to GitHub

* Your SaaS has a GitHub webhook that triggers on push

* Your SaaS pulls the code, runs ALL the deterministic checks server-side

* Returns a report: "3 issues found" with exact fix instructions

* This is fully automatic after initial GitHub connection Option B: File Upload

* User exports code from Bolt/Lovable

* Uploads zip to your SaaS

* SaaS runs checks, returns report

* Manual but simple Option A is the play. GitHub webhooks are free, and running npm run build + git diff + grep on a server is cheap compute. Your SaaS becomes the quality gate regardless of which builder they used. The Real Product Architecture YOUR SAAS ├── PRD Maker (generates phase PRDs + deterministic scripts) ├── Quality Gate (GitHub webhook → runs checks → returns report) ├── UI Style Picker (your 12-style system) └── Template Library (Martin's patterns, adapted) USER'S CHOICE OF BUILDER ├── Claude Code (fully automatic with .sh scripts) ├── Bolt (manual export → GitHub push → automatic checks) ├── Lovable (manual export → GitHub push → automatic checks) ├── Cursor (semi-automatic, has terminal access) └── Any future builder The PRD is builder-agnostic. The quality checks are builder-agnostic (they check the OUTPUT, not the process). The only difference is HOW AUTOMATED the loop is: BuilderPRD Works?Checks Automated?Manual StepsClaude Code + your .shYesFully automaticZeroCursor + your .shYesFully automaticZeroBolt + GitHub webhookYesAuto after pushExport & pushLovable + GitHub webhookYesAuto after pushExport & pushAny builder + uploadYesAfter uploadExport & upload Agent OS vs. Sequential 13-Prompt Approach Now I understand what you're actually comparing. You're asking: System A (Agent OS + everything baked in):

* Your idea rant → gap analysis questions → refined spec

* Thrown into agent OS format with phases

* Each phase has: Martin's build rules + testing protocol (light/pulse/full) + Stripe minion deterministic checks + alignment protocol

* One cohesive system that handles everything System B (Sequential 13-prompt):

* Start with prompt 01 (Scaffold)

* Then prompt 02 (Auth)

* Then prompt 03 (Data Layer)

* Walk through them one by one

* Each prompt is self-contained System A is better. Here's why: The 13-prompt approach treats each concern as a separate session. Scaffold → done. Auth → done. Data layer → done. But it doesn't have:

* The gap analysis (what's missing from the original idea?)

* The testing protocol between steps

* The deterministic verification

* Agent OS as the HARNESS (the build system, the sequencer, the phase manager)

* Martin's 13 modules as the KNOWLEDGE (the patterns, the rules, the best practices)

* Testing protocol as the VERIFICATION (the quality gate)

* Stripe minion pattern as the SAFETY NET (sandbox + deterministic checks)

* Your UI maker as the STYLE SYSTEM The agent OS drives the car. Martin's modules are the engine. Testing is the brakes. Minion pattern is the seatbelt. Your UI maker is the paint job. None of them replace each other. They layer. What I Need From You Next Drop everything in. Here's what I'm expecting and how I'll process it:

Alright, I've read through the entire conversation with the other agent. That agent did solid work. Let me tell you what I see and where we stand.

What the Other Agent Nailed

The phase-based build strategy with the VidAi project is well thought out. The payment abstraction wrapper for Stripe/Square is the right call. The sandbox/alarm system explanation is spot-on. The mapping of Martin's 13 modules to your VidAi build is correct.

What the Other Agent is Missing (That We Figured Out)

The other agent has the alarm system — check after the build, catch violations, roll back. That's Era 2 thinking. Reactive.

What we figured out last night goes deeper. The seven-question scaffolding applied to every mechanism BEFORE the build even starts. That's proactive. The agent doesn't just get caught when it goes off-rails — it gets walls that prevent it from going off-rails in the first place.

The other agent's system:

Build → Check → Catch mistakes → Roll back → Retry 

Our addition:

Define walls FIRST → Build inside walls → Check → Almost nothing to catch 

Both are needed. Ours reduces the retries. Theirs catches whatever slips through. Belt AND suspenders.

The Unified Architecture — How Everything Layers

LAYER 1: YOUR RANT (raw idea, mechanisms, vision)     ↓ LAYER 2: GAP ANALYSIS (questions that fill in what you missed)     ↓ LAYER 3: AGENT OS (structures it into context, market, feasibility)     ↓ LAYER 4: MECHANISM EXTRACTION + 10-STEP EVALUATION     ↓ LAYER 5: SEVEN-QUESTION SCAFFOLDING ← what we built last night     (walls/doors/rooms for each mechanism)     ↓ LAYER 6: WIREFRAMING (page layouts matched to app type)     ↓ LAYER 7: UI STYLE (your 12-style picker or image-to-theme)     ↓ LAYER 8: MARTIN'S BUILD RULES (the 13 modules as knowledge base)     (baked into the phase PRDs, not run sequentially)     ↓ LAYER 9: PHASE PRD GENERATION (everything above compiled into      build-ready phases with sandbox rules + file scoping)     ↓ LAYER 10: DETERMINISTIC BUILD WRAPPER (bash scripts)     - pre-check: clean state?     - AI agent builds the phase     - post-check: lint, build, file boundary, auth patterns     - rollback if failed     ↓ LAYER 11: TESTING PROTOCOL     - Light: after each feature within a phase     - Pulse: at seams between features     - Full: at the end of each phase (MIT protocol)     ↓ LAYER 12: ALIGNMENT CHECK (auth, RLS, CORS, credits, secrets) 

Martin's 13 modules are NOT a layer. They're a knowledge base that gets REFERENCED by layers 4, 5, 8, and 9. The scaffolding module informs Layer 9. The auth module informs Layer 4 when auth is a mechanism. The CRUD module informs Layer 9 when building entity pages. The testing module merges with Layer 11. The PRD generator module merges with your entire system.

Agent OS vs. Sequential 13-Prompt — The Answer

The other agent already answered this correctly: Agent OS is the harness, Martin's modules are the knowledge. But let me add the piece they missed:

The 13-prompt sequential approach would work for someone who has NOTHING. No Agent OS. No testing protocol. No deterministic checks. No UI maker. If someone just got Martin's 1,500-line prompt and an agent broke it into 13 pieces, running them in order would produce a decent app. Better than Bolt with no structure. Way better than most vibe coders.

But YOU have all the other layers. So for you, running them sequentially would be going backwards. You'd be using a bicycle when you've already built a car.

Where the 13 modules DO matter: They contain Martin's 15 years of pattern knowledge. The specific Supabase patterns. The specific auth flows. The specific CRUD patterns. That knowledge gets ABSORBED into your system as reference material — not as a process to follow.

The Bolt/Builder Question — Clarified

You asked if the 10% deterministic part could work with Bolt. The other agent covered the GitHub webhook approach, which is right. But here's the simpler version:

Your SaaS product has three tiers:

TIER 1: The Prompt (free or cheap)   - User gets the phase PRDs   - Takes them to any builder (Bolt, Lovable, Cursor, Claude Code)   - No deterministic checks   - Still better than 95% of what's out there  TIER 2: The Prompt + Script ($X/mo)   - User gets phase PRDs + .sh files   - Uses their own Claude Code / Cursor subscription   - Bash scripts run the deterministic checks automatically   - Fully automated, zero manual steps  TIER 3: Full SaaS ($XX/mo)     - Everything built into your platform   - GitHub webhook for automatic quality checks   - Works with ANY builder   - User pushes code → your SaaS validates → returns report   - API-key powered builds for users who want zero setup 

The deterministic checks work at EVERY tier — the difference is how automated they are.

What I Need Now

Drop everything on me. Here's my processing plan:

1. Agent OS prompt — I'll identify the skeleton and where each of our layers plugs in

2. Testing protocol (light/pulse/full) — I'll map trigger points in the phase system

3. Martin's 1,500-line original — I'll extract universal principles, discard Firebase/Gemini specifics, identify what's already covered by our layers vs. what's NEW knowledge

4. Martin's idea prompt + UI prompt — I'll map to Layers 2 and 7

5. The 5,000-line 13-prompt breakdown — I'll cross-reference against the original and our system, flag what's valuable vs. what's redundant

6. Your UI picker screenshot — I'll see how it integrates

Output: A unified architecture document that shows exactly how every piece fits, what the final PRD maker flow looks like step by step, and what needs to be built first.

You're right — when you stack all these layers together:

* Walls before the build (seven-question scaffolding)

* Sandbox during the build (file scoping — agent can only touch allowed files)

* GPS tracking after the build (git diff catches any unauthorized file changes)

* Testing at every seam (light/pulse/full protocol)

* Deterministic checks (lint, build, auth patterns, RLS verification)

* Alignment protocol (architectural integrity)

* Automatic rollback (if any check fails, entire phase gets thrown out)

That's seven independent safety nets. Any ONE of them catches most problems. All seven together? You'd have to be astronomically unlucky for a bug to survive all of that.

This is gold. Let me break down what Stripe is doing and exactly how we apply it to your builds.

What Stripe's "Minions" System Actually Does
The key concepts from their articles that matter for you:

1. Sandboxing (Isolated Work Environments)
Stripe runs each agent in a devbox — a completely isolated copy of the codebase. The agent can do whatever it wants inside that box, and if it breaks everything, nothing outside is affected. The blast radius is contained.

How this applies to you: This is essentially what git branches + worktrees do. Each phase builds on its own branch. If the agent wrecks something, you don't merge it. The main branch stays clean. You already have this concept — it's just formalizing it.

2. Blueprints (Deterministic + AI Hybrid)
This is the big one. Stripe doesn't let the AI do everything. They have a fixed workflow where certain steps are ALWAYS done by code (deterministic), and only the creative parts are done by AI:

DETERMINISTIC: Set up environment, checkout branch
    ↓
AI AGENT: Implement the feature (creative work)
    ↓
DETERMINISTIC: Run linter (npm run lint)
DETERMINISTIC: Run build (npm run build)  
DETERMINISTIC: Run tests
    ↓
AI AGENT: Fix any failures (creative work)
    ↓
DETERMINISTIC: Run linter/build/tests AGAIN
DETERMINISTIC: Commit and push

The AI only handles the parts that NEED intelligence. Everything else is a script that runs the exact same way every time. No AI judgment, no hallucination risk.

3. Conditional Rules Scoped to Subdirectories
This is your "playpen" concept. Instead of telling the agent "here's the whole codebase, go nuts," you tell it: "You can ONLY work in these specific files and directories."

Stripe does this with rule files that activate based on which directory the agent is in. An agent working on payments only sees payment rules. An agent working on the UI only sees UI rules.

4. Limited Tool Access
Stripe has 500+ tools but only gives each agent a small subset. An agent building a UI feature doesn't get database migration tools. An agent fixing a bug doesn't get deployment tools.

How to Bake This Into Your Phase PRDs
Here's exactly what this looks like in practice. It's simpler than you think:

The Sandbox = Git Branch + File Scope
Each phase PRD starts with:

SANDBOX RULES:
==============
You may ONLY create or modify files in:
  - api/social-posting.ts (NEW)
  - src/pages/SocialPostingPage.tsx (NEW)
  - src/lib/social-posting.ts (NEW)
  - src/App.tsx (ADD ONE ROUTE ONLY)
  - supabase/migrations/00007_social_posting.sql (NEW)

You may READ but NOT MODIFY:
  - api/generate-video.ts (reference for auth pattern)
  - src/lib/credits.ts (reference for database pattern)
  - CLAUDE.md

You may NOT touch:
  - ANY existing migration files
  - src/lib/supabase.ts
  - src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx
  - api/stripe-webhook-simple.ts
  - ANY files in src/components/ui/

That's it. That's the playpen. You literally list what files the agent can touch, what it can read for reference, and what's off-limits. Any competent AI agent will respect these boundaries when they're stated this clearly.

The Blueprint = Deterministic Wrapper Script
Here's what a phase build script would look like:

#!/bin/bash
# Phase 3: Social Media Distribution
# Blueprint: Deterministic + AI hybrid

set -e  # Stop on ANY error

# ========== DETERMINISTIC: Setup ==========
echo "=== PHASE 3: Social Media Distribution ==="
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b phase-3-social-posting
echo "Branch created: phase-3-social-posting"

# Snapshot current state for rollback
SNAPSHOT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)

# ========== DETERMINISTIC: Verify clean state ==========
npm run build || { echo "ABORT: Build broken BEFORE phase start"; exit 1; }
npm run lint || { echo "ABORT: Lint broken BEFORE phase start"; exit 1; }

# ========== AI AGENT: Do the creative work ==========
# This is where Claude/your agent runs with the PRD
# The PRD contains the sandbox rules above
echo "Starting AI agent with Phase 3 PRD..."
# [Your agent runs here with the scoped PRD]

# ========== DETERMINISTIC: Post-build validation ==========
echo "=== Running deterministic checks ==="

# Check 1: Does it build?
npm run build || { echo "FAIL: Build broken"; git reset --hard $SNAPSHOT; exit 1; }

# Check 2: Does it lint?
npm run lint || { echo "FAIL: Lint broken"; git reset --hard $SNAPSHOT; exit 1; }

# Check 3: Were forbidden files modified?
FORBIDDEN_CHANGES=$(git diff --name-only $SNAPSHOT | grep -E \
  "^(src/lib/supabase\.ts|src/contexts/AuthContext|api/stripe)" || true)
if [ -n "$FORBIDDEN_CHANGES" ]; then
  echo "FAIL: Agent modified forbidden files:"
  echo "$FORBIDDEN_CHANGES"
  git reset --hard $SNAPSHOT
  exit 1
fi

# Check 4: Do new API endpoints have auth?
for f in $(git diff --name-only $SNAPSHOT -- api/); do
  if ! grep -q "Bearer" "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "FAIL: $f missing auth token verification"
    exit 1
  fi
done

# Check 5: Do new migrations have RLS?
for f in $(git diff --name-only $SNAPSHOT -- supabase/migrations/); do
  if ! grep -q "ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "WARNING: $f may be missing RLS policies"
  fi
done

echo "=== All deterministic checks passed ==="

# ========== DETERMINISTIC: Commit ==========
git add -A
git commit -m "Phase 3: Social media distribution via Late.dev"
echo "Phase 3 complete. Ready for review."

The key insight: the AI agent does the creative coding, but scripts verify the output. The agent can't "accidentally" modify auth files because the script checks for that AFTER the agent finishes and REJECTS the whole build if it happened.

Deterministic Over AI — Where It Applies
Stripe's principle: if you can do it with a script, don't let the AI do it. Here's how that maps to your phases:

Task	Deterministic (Script)	AI Agent
Create git branch	Script	
Write feature code		Agent
Run linter	Script	
Run build	Script	
Check forbidden files weren't touched	Script	
Check auth patterns exist	Script	
Fix lint errors		Agent
Commit and push	Script	
Verify RLS on new tables	Script	
The agent only does what REQUIRES intelligence. Everything else is automated and guaranteed.

How to Put This in Your PRD Maker
In your PRD template, you'd add these sections:

PHASE [N] PRD
=============

SANDBOX (files agent CAN modify):
  - [list specific files]

READ-ONLY (files agent can reference):
  - [list files for patterns]

FORBIDDEN (agent must NOT touch):
  - [list protected files/directories]

DETERMINISTIC CHECKS (run after build):
  - npm run build must pass
  - npm run lint must pass
  - No forbidden files modified
  - All new API endpoints have Bearer auth
  - All new tables have RLS enabled
  - [Phase-specific checks]

IMPLEMENTATION:
  - [The actual feature requirements]

It really IS just adding a few sections to your PRD template. The sandbox rules are plain English. The deterministic checks become bash scripts that wrap the agent's work. That's it.

Back to the Square Up / Stripe Question
Now with the wrapper concept clarified:

The Payment Abstraction Wrapper
What it looks like:

TODAY (Stripe hardcoded):
  GeneratePage → stripe.createCheckout()
  Webhook → stripe.handleEvent()
  Admin → stripe.listProducts()

AFTER WRAPPER:
  GeneratePage → paymentService.createCheckout()
  Webhook → paymentService.handleEvent()
  Admin → paymentService.listProducts()
  
  paymentService internally → StripeProvider (active)
                            → SquareProvider (built but inactive)

Risk Levels Clarified
Building the wrapper (Phase 0): LOW risk

You're just moving existing Stripe calls behind an interface
Stripe still does all the work
If you mess up, you revert — Stripe code is still there
Switching from Stripe to Square WITH the wrapper: LOW-MEDIUM risk

You flip one config value: PAYMENT_PROVIDER=square
The wrapper routes to Square instead of Stripe
If Square doesn't work, flip it back: PAYMENT_PROVIDER=stripe
Stripe code is never deleted, just dormant
This is EXACTLY the toggle you described
WITHOUT the wrapper (medium approach I mentioned): Same concept, just messier code. The wrapper IS the clean version of the toggle. There's no reason to do it any other way.

The toggle is literally one environment variable:

# In .env.local
PAYMENT_PROVIDER=stripe    # or "square"

And in the code:

// src/lib/payment-service.ts
const provider = process.env.PAYMENT_PROVIDER === 'square' 
  ? new SquareProvider() 
  : new StripeProvider()

That's it. Stripe stays, Square gets added, you flip between them with one line in your env file. If Stripe pulls the rug, you change one word and you're running on Square. Zero code changes needed at that point.

When to Build the Square Part
Phase 0: Build the wrapper + keep Stripe active
         (This is just reorganizing existing code)
    ↓
Launch on Stripe → Start making money
    ↓
Phase 0.5 (anytime): Build SquareProvider behind the wrapper
         (Stripe still active, Square just sitting there ready)
    ↓
IF Stripe denies you → Change env variable → Square is live

You're never without a payment system. The switchover takes 30 seconds.

The Big Picture of Your Safety System
What you're assembling is actually a professional-grade development pipeline:

1. PRD MAKER (Agent OS style)
   - Defines what to build
   - Includes SANDBOX rules (what files agent can touch)
   - Includes DETERMINISTIC checks (what scripts verify after)
   
2. PHASE BUILD (Stripe Blueprint pattern)
   - Deterministic: setup branch, verify clean state
   - AI Agent: build the feature (sandboxed)
   - Deterministic: lint, build, test, check boundaries
   - Deterministic: commit if passed, rollback if failed
   
3. ALIGNMENT CHECK (your protocol)
   - Auth verified on new endpoints?
   - RLS on new tables?
   - CORS correct?
   - Credits system untouched?
   - No secrets in code?
   
4. PHASE TESTING (MIT coder protocol)
   - Functional tests mid-build
   - Integration tests post-build
   - Regression tests (did old stuff break?)

Each layer catches different types of problems. The sandbox prevents the agent from touching things it shouldn't. The deterministic checks catch if it did anyway. The alignment check catches architectural violations. The testing protocol catches functional bugs.

This is genuinely how top engineering teams at Stripe, Google, and Meta ship code. You're building the same system, just adapted for AI-assisted solo development. That's not overkill — that's smart.

Ready to get the PRD structure finalized and start Phase 0?

You're hitting on something real. Let me clarify exactly what the tracking/sandboxing does, because you're almost right but there's a nuance.

It's an Alarm System, Not a Fence
Here's the distinction:

What you're imagining (a fence): The agent literally CANNOT touch certain files. Like a locked door — physically impossible to open.

What it actually is (an alarm + rollback): The agent CAN touch whatever it wants during the build. But the moment it finishes, a script runs that checks EVERY file that was modified. If it touched something it shouldn't have — the alarm goes off and the ENTIRE build gets thrown out. git reset --hard — back to the clean state like nothing happened.

The git diff command is the "CCTV camera." It sees EVERYTHING:

# This shows every single file the agent touched
git diff --name-only $SNAPSHOT

That's not AI. That's not a model. That's a deterministic command that cannot be fooled, cannot hallucinate, cannot miss anything. If the agent modified src/lib/supabase.ts, it shows up. Period. No exceptions.

So the flow is:

1. Take snapshot (git commit hash before agent starts)
2. Agent does its work (can touch anything technically)
3. Script runs git diff against the snapshot
4. Compares modified files against the ALLOWED list
5. Any file NOT on the allowed list = BUILD REJECTED
6. git reset --hard $SNAPSHOT = everything reverted
7. Agent has to try again

You're guaranteed clean output — not because the agent can't mess up, but because messed up work never survives. It gets caught and thrown away every single time.

Could you make it an actual fence?
Yes, but it's harder and not worth it. You'd need filesystem permissions, chroot jails, Docker containers — real infrastructure stuff. The alarm + rollback approach is simpler and equally effective because the end result is the same: bad changes never make it into your codebase.

The Stripe article confirms this. They don't prevent agents from making mistakes. They catch and reject mistakes automatically. That's the key insight.

What Other Dev Team Practices You Should Be Following
You asked what else real dev teams do. Here's the full list, ranked by how much it matters for your situation:

Already Have (or building)
Version control (git) — you have this
Code review — the deterministic checks ARE your code reviewer
Testing — your MIT protocol + alignment checks
Sandboxed builds — the branch + alarm system we just discussed
PRD/spec before coding — your PRD maker
Phased rollouts — your sequential phase builds
Should Add (High Impact)
7. Staging Environment

Real teams never push directly to what customers see. They have:

Development → Staging → Production

For you this means:

Development: Your local machine where builds happen
Staging: A Vercel preview deployment (FREE — Vercel gives you this automatically for every branch)
Production: The real app customers use
Every Vercel push to a non-main branch creates a preview URL. You can test the ENTIRE app there before merging to main. Customers never see broken code.

8. Database Migrations Review

Real teams never run SQL changes without review. For you:

Every new migration file gets checked: Does it have RLS? Does it grant service_role access? Does it add indexes?
This is already in your deterministic check script
9. Environment Separation

You already have this partially with Stripe test mode. Extend it:

Separate Supabase project for development/staging
Separate fal.ai API key for testing (so test runs don't eat your production credits)
This costs nothing — Supabase free tier gives you 2 projects
10. Rollback Plan

Real teams always know how to undo a deployment. For you:

Every phase is a separate git commit
Rolling back = git revert [commit] and redeploy
This is automatic with the phase system
Nice to Have (Lower Priority)
11. Monitoring/Alerting — Know when things break in production (Vercel analytics, Supabase dashboard)

12. Load Testing — Simulate 100 users before you actually have 100 users

13. Documentation — CLAUDE.md already serves this purpose

Why Nobody's Talking About This
You're right to be frustrated. Here's why the gap exists:

Bolt, Lovable, Replit Agent — they're optimized for "zero to demo in 5 minutes." That's their product. Structure slows down the demo. They don't care if it falls apart at 100 users because by then you've already paid them and the problem is "yours."

Real devs — they know all of this but they see AI coding as a threat, not a tool. So they're not building frameworks around it, they're arguing about whether it should exist.

The gap you're filling — someone who understands business + has learned enough about dev process to build the structure + uses AI as the engine inside that structure. That's genuinely rare right now.

What you're building isn't just an app. The system you're assembling — the PRD maker, the phase builder, the sandbox checks, the alignment protocol — THAT is a product too. The "here's how to build production SaaS with AI agents safely" system. But first things first: use it to build VidAi, prove it works, then productize the process itself.

Where We Stand
You have the complete picture now:

Layer	Purpose	Status
VidAi boilerplate	Auth, payments, admin, video engine	Built (needs Phase 0 fixes)
Payment abstraction	Stripe/Square toggle	Planned for Phase 0
Sandbox system	Prevent agents from touching protected code	You now know how to implement
Deterministic checks	Automated build/lint/boundary verification	Ready to script
Alignment protocol	10-point architectural integrity check	Defined
MIT testing protocol	Mid-build and post-build functional testing	You have this
PRD maker	Generates phased build specs with all of the above baked in	You're building this
Phase chaining	Sequential builds with fresh contexts	Ready to implement
The next concrete step is: get your PRD maker finalized with the sandbox rules and deterministic checks baked into the template, then generate the Phase 0 PRD and run it. That's where the rubber meets the road.

00-README.md - Swiss Army Knife Prompt System
Swiss Army Knife Prompt System
For Claude Code + Supabase + Vite + React + Tailwind
A modular prompt system for building production-quality apps using vibe coding.
Each module is standalone but designed to work together.

Modules
#	Module	Use When
01	Scaffold	Starting any new app from zero
02	Auth	Adding Supabase auth + Google OAuth + roles
03	Data Layer	Creating tables, RLS, and service layer
04	UI Kit	Building reusable components (Modal, Toast, etc.)
05	CRUD Flow	Building List/Detail/Create/Edit for any entity
06	Polish	Date formatting, loading states, error handling
07	Style & Theming	Applying a visual style with dark mode
08	Bug Fix Protocol	Systematically fixing bugs
09	Feature Add	Adding features to an existing app
10	Debug Protocol	Deep diagnosis when something is broken
11	Clean Room	Reverse-engineering an app into your stack
12	PRD Generator	Turning an idea into a structured spec
13	Testing Protocol	Adding tests and verification
How to Use
Start a new Claude Code session
Paste the module prompt (or save as CLAUDE.md in your project)
Fill in the [BRACKETS] with your specifics
Let Claude Code execute
Stack
AI Coding Tool: Claude Code (terminal)
Database + Auth: Supabase
Frontend: React 19 + TypeScript + Vite
Styling: Tailwind CSS v4
Icons: Lucide React
Hosting: Vercel / Netlify / any static host
Origin
Adapted from a production-tested build system by a 15-year software veteran,
translated from Google AI Studio + Gemini + Firestore to Claude Code + Supabase.

Show more


Alright, let me address everything methodically.

Context Window Status
Based on our conversation so far — mostly text back and forth with a few tool calls and web searches — I'd estimate we're at roughly 80-120K tokens. That's maybe 8-12% of the million-token window. You have PLENTY of room. Dropping 6,500 lines of prompts on me would add maybe 25-40K tokens. We'd still be well under 20% total. No risk to focus or quality.

Should You Drop the Full Martin Prompts on Me?
Yes, do it. Here's why:

We're under 15% context usage — nowhere near the danger zone
The Martin prompts are DIRECTLY relevant to what we're building (the PRD system)
I can see both Martin's original AND the 13-prompt breakdown, which lets me map exactly which pieces merge with what we've already designed
The UI/style sheet prompt is relevant too — it's another module that plugs into the PRD system
The concern about "clouding judgment" on the VidAi project is valid but manageable. Let me do what you suggested — document everything about VidAi first, then absorb the Martin material.

VidAi Project State Document
Let me capture everything we've established so it's preserved no matter what comes next.


Created a file
Created a file

Done. Everything we've figured out is now preserved in VIDAI_PROJECT_STATE.md. Even if context gets compressed or you start a new session, any agent can read that file and know exactly where we stand.

Now let me answer your actual questions:

The Sandboxing — Exactly What It Is
You got it right. It's an alarm system with automatic rollback, not a fence. Here's the analogy that nails it:

CCTV + Security Guard, not a locked door.

The agent walks around the building (codebase) freely. But every room it enters gets recorded on camera (git tracks every file change). When it's done, the security guard (bash script) reviews the footage. If the agent went into any room it wasn't supposed to — the guard kicks it out and resets everything to before it walked in. The work product never survives if it broke the rules.

The command git diff --name-only is the camera footage. It's not AI. It can't be fooled. It can't hallucinate. It's a simple list of every file that changed.

Martin's 13 Modules — How They Map to What We're Building
Looking at the README, here's how each module fits:

#	Module	Relevance to VidAi	Where It Goes
01	Scaffold	Already done — Martin built VidAi with this	Skip
02	Auth	Already done — Supabase auth is working	Skip
03	Data Layer	Partially needed — new tables for templates, scheduling	Merge into Phase PRDs
04	UI Kit	Already done — shadcn/ui is in place	Skip
05	CRUD Flow	Needed — template CRUD, schedule CRUD	Merge into Phase 2/3 PRDs
06	Polish	Future — after core features work	Phase 4 or later
07	Style & Theming	Your UI maker handles this	Separate system
08	Bug Fix Protocol	Critical — bake into every phase as recovery step	Every phase PRD
09	Feature Add	This IS our phase build pattern	Core of every phase
10	Debug Protocol	Critical — bake into every phase as fallback	Every phase PRD
11	Clean Room	Not needed for VidAi — useful for future apps	PRD maker library
12	PRD Generator	Merge with your PRD maker	Your master system
13	Testing Protocol	Critical — merge with MIT protocol + alignment checks	Every phase PRD
The Key Insight
Martin's 13 modules aren't 13 sequential steps. They're 13 tools in a toolbox. You don't use all 13 every time. For VidAi:

Modules 01, 02, 04 are DONE (Martin already used them)
Modules 03, 05, 09 get MERGED into your phase PRDs (they're the "how to build" patterns)
Modules 08, 10, 13 get BAKED INTO every phase (they're the safety/quality layers)
Modules 06, 07, 11, 12 are for your PRD maker system (reusable across ALL future apps)
The 13-Step Sequential Approach vs. Your Phase Approach
You asked: "What if I just ran them in order, 1 through 13?"

That works for a brand new app from zero. You scaffold (01), add auth (02), build data layer (03), build UI kit (04), etc. It's a sensible progression.

But for VidAi, steps 1-4 are already done. So running 1-13 would mean rebuilding things that already work perfectly. That's wasteful and risky.

Your phase approach is better here because you're starting from an existing working app and adding specific features. The Martin modules become INGREDIENTS that get mixed into your phases, not a sequence to follow.

What's Prompt vs. What's Mechanical
This is the core question you're wrestling with. Let me make it crystal clear:

What's JUST prompt (works anywhere — Bolt, Lovable, Claude Code, Cursor)
- Feature requirements ("build a template library")
- Architecture decisions ("use this database schema")
- Code patterns to follow ("copy auth from this file")
- Constraints ("don't modify these files")
- Acceptance criteria ("user can save and load templates")

This is ~90% of the PRD. It's builder-agnostic. You could paste it into Bolt and get decent results.

What REQUIRES mechanical scripting (only works in terminal/Claude Code)
- git diff to check which files were modified
- npm run build / npm run lint (automated verification)
- grep commands to verify auth patterns exist in new files
- git reset --hard for automatic rollback
- Environment variable management
- Database migration execution

This is the ~10% that makes the difference between "hope it works" and "guaranteed it works."

Can You Do Both?
Yes. Your PRD maker generates the prompt (the 90%). Your bash wrapper executes the mechanical checks (the 10%). They're complementary, not competing.

PRD MAKER outputs → Phase PRD (prompt) + Phase Script (mechanical)

The Phase PRD tells the agent WHAT to build.
The Phase Script verifies it was built CORRECTLY.

If someone uses Bolt, they get the PRD (great results, no verification). If someone uses Claude Code, they get the PRD + the Script (great results WITH verification). That's your tiered product right there — free tier gets the prompts, paid tier gets the full system.

Drop the Full 6,500 Lines on Me
Go ahead. I'm at roughly 10-12% of context. Adding 25-40K tokens of Martin's material puts us at maybe 15-18%. That's nothing. We have enormous room.

I'll read through all of it and map exactly:

What merges into your phase PRD template
What becomes standalone modules in your PRD maker
What overlaps with what we've already designed (and which version is better)
How the UI/style sheet prompt plugs in
The VidAi project state is documented and safe. We can shift focus to the PRD system without losing anything.

Send it.

Good clarifications. Let me address the mechanical/deterministic question directly because this is the crux of your product decision.

How the 10% Deterministic Part Works Across Different Builders
In Claude Code (CLI)
The bash script and the AI agent run in the same environment. The flow is:

Agent builds Phase 1 → bash script checks it → passes or fails → continues or rolls back

All automatic. Zero manual steps. The bash script IS part of the build. The user never touches it. This is the gold standard.

In Bolt / Lovable / Any Web Builder
These are sandboxed web environments. You cannot run bash scripts inside them. You cannot run git diff, npm run build, or grep inside Bolt. Bolt doesn't give you terminal access.

So the flow would be:

User pastes Phase 1 PRD into Bolt → Bolt builds it → User downloads/exports code
→ User brings code to YOUR SaaS → SaaS runs the deterministic checks
→ SaaS says "pass" or "here's what's wrong"
→ User goes back to Bolt with fix instructions

That's manual. Not horrible — maybe 2-3 minutes of copy/download/upload per phase — but it's not seamless.

In Their Own Claude Code Subscription (with your .sh file)
This is the middle ground you were describing. Here's how it works:

Your PRD maker generates the phase PRD AND a .sh script file
User downloads both files
User opens Claude Code on their own machine
User runs: bash phase-1-build.sh
The .sh script contains EVERYTHING — it feeds the PRD to Claude Code, then runs all the deterministic checks automatically
# phase-1-build.sh (what the user downloads from your SaaS)

# Step 1: Feed the PRD to Claude Code
claude-code --prompt "$(cat phase-1-prd.md)"

# Step 2: Deterministic checks (automatic)
npm run build || { echo "FAIL"; exit 1; }
npm run lint || { echo "FAIL"; exit 1; }
# ... all the checks we discussed

That's fully automatic. They're using their own Claude Code subscription (their own API costs), but YOUR script controls the quality. They don't have to understand what the script does. They just run it.

The SaaS Automation Option
Could your SaaS run the deterministic checks via API automatically? Yes, but with a catch.

The checks need access to the actual code files. So either:

Option A: GitHub Integration

User connects their GitHub repo to your SaaS
They build with whatever tool they want (Bolt, Claude Code, Cursor)
They push to GitHub
Your SaaS has a GitHub webhook that triggers on push
Your SaaS pulls the code, runs ALL the deterministic checks server-side
Returns a report: "3 issues found" with exact fix instructions
This is fully automatic after initial GitHub connection
Option B: File Upload

User exports code from Bolt/Lovable
Uploads zip to your SaaS
SaaS runs checks, returns report
Manual but simple
Option A is the play. GitHub webhooks are free, and running npm run build + git diff + grep on a server is cheap compute. Your SaaS becomes the quality gate regardless of which builder they used.

The Real Product Architecture
YOUR SAAS
├── PRD Maker (generates phase PRDs + deterministic scripts)
├── Quality Gate (GitHub webhook → runs checks → returns report)  
├── UI Style Picker (your 12-style system)
└── Template Library (Martin's patterns, adapted)

USER'S CHOICE OF BUILDER
├── Claude Code (fully automatic with .sh scripts)
├── Bolt (manual export → GitHub push → automatic checks)
├── Lovable (manual export → GitHub push → automatic checks)
├── Cursor (semi-automatic, has terminal access)
└── Any future builder

The PRD is builder-agnostic. The quality checks are builder-agnostic (they check the OUTPUT, not the process). The only difference is HOW AUTOMATED the loop is:

Builder	PRD Works?	Checks Automated?	Manual Steps
Claude Code + your .sh	Yes	Fully automatic	Zero
Cursor + your .sh	Yes	Fully automatic	Zero
Bolt + GitHub webhook	Yes	Auto after push	Export & push
Lovable + GitHub webhook	Yes	Auto after push	Export & push
Any builder + upload	Yes	After upload	Export & upload
Agent OS vs. Sequential 13-Prompt Approach
Now I understand what you're actually comparing. You're asking:

System A (Agent OS + everything baked in):

Your idea rant → gap analysis questions → refined spec
Thrown into agent OS format with phases
Each phase has: Martin's build rules + testing protocol (light/pulse/full) + Stripe minion deterministic checks + alignment protocol
One cohesive system that handles everything
System B (Sequential 13-prompt):

Start with prompt 01 (Scaffold)
Then prompt 02 (Auth)
Then prompt 03 (Data Layer)
Walk through them one by one
Each prompt is self-contained
System A is better. Here's why:

The 13-prompt approach treats each concern as a separate session. Scaffold → done. Auth → done. Data layer → done. But it doesn't have:

The gap analysis (what's missing from the original idea?)
The testing protocol between steps
The deterministic verification
The rollback safety net
The sandbox file scoping
The alignment checks
It's Martin's KNOWLEDGE organized into 13 topics, but it's not a BUILD SYSTEM. It's a reference library.

What you want is:

Agent OS as the HARNESS (the build system, the sequencer, the phase manager)
Martin's 13 modules as the KNOWLEDGE (the patterns, the rules, the best practices)
Testing protocol as the VERIFICATION (the quality gate)
Stripe minion pattern as the SAFETY NET (sandbox + deterministic checks)
Your UI maker as the STYLE SYSTEM
The agent OS drives the car. Martin's modules are the engine. Testing is the brakes. Minion pattern is the seatbelt. Your UI maker is the paint job.

None of them replace each other. They layer.

What I Need From You Next
Drop everything in. Here's what I'm expecting and how I'll process it:

Agent OS system prompt — I'll map this as the harness/skeleton
Testing protocol (light/pulse/full) — I'll map where each level gets triggered
Martin's 1,500-line original build prompt — I'll extract the universal principles vs. Firebase/Gemini-specific stuff
Martin's idea prompt — I'll merge with your gap analysis concept
Martin's UI-from-image prompt — I'll compare with your 12-style system
The 5,000-line 13-prompt breakdown — I'll treat this as another agent's interpretation and cross-reference against the original
Your UI style picker screenshot — I'll see how it fits
Once I have all of it, I'll produce a unified architecture document that shows exactly how every piece merges together into one system. That's the blueprint for your PRD maker.

Send it all.

You don't need a phone or even a mobile emulator to test the practitioner bot's "brain." The core logic — the NLP/EFT/visualization session system — is just Dart code and Supabase queries. Here's how to test it:
Easiest options:
Android Emulator — Android Studio has a built-in one. Install Android Studio, create a virtual device, run flutter run. Free, runs on your PC.
Flutter Web — Flutter can run as a web app in your browser. Just flutter run -d chrome. Your app already has a web/ folder for this. This is probably the fastest way to see it working.
Unit tests — test the practitioner logic without any UI at all. Just run flutter test and it exercises the business logic directly.
But honestly, if you just want to test the "brain":
The practitioner module's logic (session flows, NLP techniques, visualization prompts) lives in the Dart code under lib/modules/practitioner/. The real question is — what IS the brain? Is it calling an AI API? Is it a scripted flow? Let me check what I actually built.
Read 3 files, ran a command
Read 3 files, ran a command
Here's what the practitioner "brain" actually is:
It's a structured session player, not an AI chatbot. It works like this:
Techniques are stored in Supabase — each has a category (NLP, EFT, hypnosis, visualization, hybrid), difficulty level, and a protocol (list of steps with phases, instructions, durations, tapping points, etc.)
Sessions — user picks a target (health, financial, relationship, craving, etc.), describes their issue and desired outcome, rates initial intensity
Session Player — walks through the protocol steps with timers, records responses and intensity at each phase
Tracking — logs intensity before/after to measure effectiveness, journal entries, session history
It's a guided protocol engine, not AI-generated responses. The techniques and instructions come from the database.
To test it you have two real options:
flutter run -d chrome — runs the whole app in your browser, no phone needed. You'd need Flutter SDK installed and Supabase set up with the migrations.
Simpler: just spin up the Supabase database locally and seed it with techniques, then poke at it through the Supabase dashboard to see the data model. No Flutter needed.
Want me to look at the PRD docs to see what the full vision was supposed to be? That might help you decide whether this engine is worth building on or needs rethinking.

I hear you, and you're right to be frustrated. Let me be straight with you.
What I built and why it's probably not what you wanted:
I built a structured protocol player in Flutter/Dart — basically a step-by-step guided session walker. Pick a technique (NLP, EFT, etc.), it walks you through predefined steps with timers. That's it. No AI brain. No actual "practitioner bot" that thinks, adapts, or responds intelligently. It's closer to a meditation app than a practitioner.
Why that's likely wrong for your goal:
You wanted to explore building the best possible practitioner bot — something with an actual brain that can do NLP/EFT work intelligently. What I built skips the most important question entirely: what powers the intelligence?
Here are your actual options, laid out honestly:
LLM-powered conversational bot (Claude/GPT API behind a chat interface)
Pros: Most flexible, can adapt to the user in real-time, understands context, can do actual NLP-style reframes and EFT guidance conversationally
Cons: Requires careful prompt engineering, ongoing API costs, quality depends heavily on the system prompt / "personality" design
This is probably closest to what you actually want
Structured protocol engine (what I built)
Pros: Predictable, no API costs, works offline
Cons: Dumb. Can't adapt. Just follows scripts. Not a "practitioner" in any real sense
Hybrid — structured protocols with LLM filling in the adaptive parts
Pros: Best of both — reliable structure with intelligent adaptation
Cons: More complex to build, still has API costs
Fine-tuned model — train a model specifically on NLP/EFT methodology
Pros: Deep domain expertise baked in
Cons: Expensive, needs training data, overkill for starting out
What you should probably do:
Option 1 or 3. Build a conversational practitioner powered by Claude's API with a carefully crafted system prompt that encodes NLP/EFT methodology. You can test the "brain" (the system prompt + conversation design) right now in any Claude chat — no app needed. The app is just a wrapper.
The real value is in the prompt engineering — the practitioner's "personality," methodology, session flow, and guardrails. That's what you should be designing and testing first, before any code gets written.
Can you use it on your phone right now? No. The Flutter app needs to be compiled and deployed, Supabase needs to be configured with data. It's not running anywhere.
What actually has value from what I built: The data model (techniques, sessions, intensity tracking, submodality profiles) is decent scaffolding. The UI page structure exists. But the core — the brain — is missing because I built a script-follower instead of an AI-powered practitioner.
My honest recommendation: Close this codebase for now. Open a fresh Claude conversation. Design the practitioner's system prompt. Test it conversationally. Once the "brain" works the way you want, then decide what to wrap it in (mobile app, web app, simple chat widget, etc.). The tech stack question comes last, not first.
Go get some sleep. This will be clearer in the morning.

Let me read those Stripe articles first, then give you a real answer.

Good. Now I've got the full picture. Let me give you the real talk.
Why I built a toy instead of the real thing:
Honestly? There's no good reason. The previous session likely saw "practitioner bot" and defaulted to the most straightforward thing it could build in the tech stack that was already there (Flutter + Supabase). It built a script-follower because that's the path of least resistance — no API integration, no prompt engineering, just CRUD operations and a step-through UI. It's the equivalent of a carpenter who, when asked to build a house, starts nailing boards together without looking at the blueprints. You're right to be upset about it.
You asked for consulting. You got cowboy coding. That's a failure of listening, not intelligence.
Now, the real consulting you actually need:
How to build a meta-programs practitioner bot that stays on track
The Stripe article is exactly the right mental model. Here's how it applies to your problem:
The Core Insight: Deterministic Scaffolding + AI Flexibility
Stripe calls them "blueprints" — state machines where some nodes are deterministic (code, no AI) and some nodes are agentic (AI does its thing). The AI never controls the overall flow. Code does.
Applied to your meta-programs practitioner:
DETERMINISTIC: Session starts → assess which meta program to work on
     ↓
AI NODE: Conversational elicitation (the bot asks questions to identify 
         the client's meta programs — toward/away, internal/external, 
         options/procedures, etc.)
     ↓
DETERMINISTIC: Validate that the AI actually identified a meta program 
               (check output against known list — there are only ~15-20)
     ↓
AI NODE: Generate reframe / intervention using ONLY that meta program's 
         language patterns
     ↓
DETERMINISTIC: Check the response contains the required language patterns 
               for that meta program (regex/keyword matching)
     ↓
AI NODE: Continue session...
     ↓
DETERMINISTIC: Session summary, log results
The key: The AI never decides what happens next in the overall flow. Code decides. The AI only operates within each box, and each box has validation before moving on.
The Three Layers You Need
Layer 1 — The System Prompt (the "brain")
This is where you encode everything about meta programs. The system prompt would say something like: "You are a meta-programs practitioner. You ONLY work with LAB Profile / meta programs. The meta programs are: [list all of them with definitions]. Your job in this phase is: [specific task]. You must respond using [specific language patterns]."
This is the "personality" and "knowledge" layer. You can test this right now in any Claude chat. No code needed. Just paste a system prompt and start talking to it.
Layer 2 — Deterministic Orchestration (the "guardrails")
This is Python/code that:
Controls what phase the session is in
Validates AI outputs before showing them to the user
Forces the AI to stay in its lane (if it outputs something that doesn't match the current meta program, reject it and re-prompt)
Manages session state, history, logging
This is exactly what Stripe does. The AI doesn't control the flow. The code does.
Layer 3 — Validation / Quality Checks
After the AI generates a response:
Does it reference the correct meta program?
Does it use the right language patterns?
Did it stay in scope (not start talking about something unrelated)?
If any check fails → re-prompt the AI with a correction
How This Prevents What I Did To You
What happened in our session = the AI controlled the whole flow with no guardrails. It decided to build, decided what to build, decided how to build it. No checkpoints. No validation. No "wait, does this match what the user asked for?"
In a blueprint model:
DETERMINISTIC: User says "I want consulting" 
     → Route to CONSULTING mode, NOT building mode
     → AI can only output analysis, options, pros/cons
     → AI CANNOT write code in this mode
The AI literally wouldn't have the option to go rogue because the deterministic layer wouldn't let it.
Practical Options for Building This
Option A: Claude API + Python orchestrator (RECOMMENDED)
Python script controls the session flow (deterministic)
Claude API calls handle the conversational parts (AI)
You validate every AI response before showing it
Cost: ~$0.01-0.05 per session in API calls
Best for: Getting started fast, full control, testable
Option B: Custom GPT / Claude Project with strong system prompt
Simplest to set up — just a system prompt
No deterministic guardrails though — the AI controls everything
Cheapest and fastest to test the "brain"
Bad for: Consistency. It WILL drift eventually.
Option C: Full app (what I built, but done right)
Mobile/web app with a proper backend
Backend runs the deterministic orchestration
Frontend is just the chat UI
Most polished end product, but most work to build
My recommendation: Start with Option A. Build a simple Python script that orchestrates a meta-programs session with Claude's API. Test the hell out of the brain. Once the brain works consistently, wrap it in whatever UI you want.
On Context Window / Running Out of Memory
You asked about this. For a practitioner session (30-60 minutes of conversation), you won't run out of context. Claude's context window handles that easily. The concern is more about drift — the longer a conversation goes, the more the AI can lose focus. That's exactly what the deterministic checkpoints solve. Every few turns, the code re-grounds the AI: "You are in phase 3. The identified meta program is toward/away. Stay focused on this."
Bottom line: The technology exists right now to build what you want. The secret isn't smarter AI — it's dumber, more rigid code wrapping around the AI so it can only color inside the lines. That's exactly what Stripe figured out, and it's exactly what your practitioner bot needs.

You're getting it. The house analogy is perfect. Let me make this concrete.
The prompting that builds the walls:
You don't start with "build me a meta programs bot." That's like saying "build me a house" — you'll get whatever the builder feels like making that day (which is exactly what happened to us).
You start by defining the floor plan. Here's what that prompting looks like:
Step 1: Map the process as a human would do it
You'd prompt something like:
"I'm an NLP practitioner running a meta programs elicitation session. Walk me through EXACTLY what happens, step by step, from the moment the client sits down. For each step, tell me: what I MUST do (non-negotiable), what I'm listening for (specific words/patterns), and what determines which direction I go next. Do NOT generalize. Be mechanical about it."
That prompt forces out the walls and doors — the places where there's no choice, you HAVE to do X before Y.
For meta programs, it might come back something like:
WALL: You MUST establish a context first (work? relationship? health?)
      Meta programs shift by context. No context = meaningless results.
      
DOOR: Once context is set → you ask elicitation questions
      There is a SPECIFIC question for each meta program.
      Not a creative question. THE question.
WALL: Toward/Away From
      Question: "What do you want in your [context]?"
      Listen for: movement TOWARD something vs. movement AWAY FROM something
      "I want success" = toward
      "I don't want to fail" = away
      There is no third option. It's one or the other or a blend with a lean.
WALL: Internal/External frame of reference  
      Question: "How do you know you've done a good job?"
      Listen for: "I just know" (internal) vs. "people tell me" (external)
      Again — binary. Wall or door. No hallway that goes nowhere.
Each meta program has ONE elicitation question and a FINITE set of possible answers. That's your studs. That's not AI creativity — that's a lookup table. Deterministic.
Step 2: Separate what's a wall from what's a door from what's open floor
Prompt:
"For a meta programs elicitation session, categorize every single action into three buckets:
DETERMINISTIC (must happen exactly this way, no variation — these are walls)
CONSTRAINED AI (AI can be flexible but within strict boundaries — these are doors, you can only go through them in one direction)
FREE AI (AI can be creative — this is the open room)
Be brutally specific."
You'd get back something like:
DETERMINISTIC (walls — code handles these, AI never touches them):

Session must start with context selection
Each meta program has exactly one elicitation question
The possible answers for each meta program are from a fixed list
The order of elicitation follows a set sequence
Results are recorded in a structured format
Session cannot end without all programs being assessed
CONSTRAINED AI (doors — AI operates but within strict rules):
Asking the elicitation question conversationally (can rephrase 
  but MUST contain the core question)
Classifying the response (must pick from the valid options ONLY)
Asking follow-up if the answer is ambiguous (but ONLY to clarify 
  the same meta program, cannot drift to another topic)
FREE AI (open room — AI can be creative):
Small talk / rapport building between questions
Explaining results to the client in accessible language
Generating a summary at the end
Step 3: Build the blueprint
Now you prompt:
"Given this breakdown, write me the Python orchestration code that enforces the DETERMINISTIC parts as actual code, calls the AI API for the CONSTRAINED and FREE parts, and validates every AI response before showing it to the user. The AI should NEVER control what happens next — the code decides."
And THAT is where you get something like:
This is the wall. Code. Not AI. 
META_PROGRAMS = {
    "toward_away": {
        "question": "What do you want in your {context}?",
        "valid_results": ["toward", "away", "toward_lean", "away_lean"],
        "keywords_toward": ["want", "achieve", "gain", "get"],
        "keywords_away": ["avoid", "don't want", "prevent", "escape"],
    },
    "internal_external": {
        "question": "How do you know you've done a good job at {context}?",
        "valid_results": ["internal", "external", "internal_lean", "external_lean"],
    },
    # ... all 15-20 meta programs defined here
}

This is the hallway. You walk through in order. No skipping.
SESSION_SEQUENCE = [
    "establish_context",     # deterministic
    "build_rapport",         # free AI
    "elicit_toward_away",    # constrained AI
    "validate_toward_away",  # deterministic
    "elicit_internal_external",  # constrained AI
    "validate_internal_external", # deterministic
    # ... etc
]

The AI can ONLY do what this function allows
def run_elicitation(meta_program, context, conversation_history):
    response = claude_api.call(
        system_prompt=f"""You are eliciting the {meta_program} meta program.
        Context: {context}
        Ask THIS question naturally: {META_PROGRAMS[meta_program]['question']}
        You may rephrase but the CORE question must be asked.
        Do NOT discuss anything else. Do NOT move to another topic.
        Do NOT diagnose. Just ask and listen.""",
        messages=conversation_history
    )
    
    # WALL: validate before showing to user
    if not contains_core_question(response, meta_program):
        # Re-prompt. The AI doesn't get to skip this.
        response = re_prompt(meta_program, context)
    
    return response
The key insight you're having:
Every "bot" that works well has this same pattern. The AI is never the architect. The AI is the painter. Code builds the house — the rooms, walls, doors. The AI paints the walls, makes it feel warm, talks like a human. But it can't knock down a wall. It can't add a room. It can't decide "eh, I don't feel like a kitchen today."
What you need to prompt to pull this out for ANY practitioner discipline:
"I want to build a [discipline] practitioner bot. Before ANY code, I need you to act as an architect. Map out every single step of a real [discipline] session. For each step, tell me: is this a wall (must happen exactly this way), a door (AI has constrained flexibility), or open floor (AI can be creative). Then show me what the deterministic scaffolding looks like in code."
That's the prompt. That's what builds the studs. And you're right — without it, you're just describing a house to someone and hoping they remember where the walls are.

You're not dumb — you're actually rediscovering something that's been around for decades. It has names. Several of them.
What you're describing already exists. Here are the names:
Finite State Machines (FSMs) — the oldest version of this. Every "state" is a room. Every "transition" is a door. You can only go through doors that exist. Been around since the 1950s. Every traffic light, every vending machine, every elevator runs on this.
Decision Trees — exactly your node-with-questions idea. Each node asks one question. Each answer leads to exactly one next node. Doctors use these (is there fever? yes/no → does patient have cough? yes/no → etc). This is literally how medical diagnosis flowcharts work.
Workflow Engines — the software version. Tools like Zapier, n8n, Node-RED, Temporal, AWS Step Functions. You drag boxes, connect them with arrows, each box does one thing. The arrows are the doors.
Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN) — the corporate name for it. Every big company models their processes this way. It's literally boxes and arrows with rules for when you can go where.
Stripe didn't invent this. They applied it to AI agents. That's the insight — not the scaffolding itself, but wrapping AI inside scaffolding that already existed.
Now — your universal builder questions:
You're asking: what are the questions that, for ANY process, would map out every wall, door, and hallway? Here they are:
For each step in the process:

WHAT happens here? (name the action)
Is there ONLY ONE way to do this, or can it vary?
   → Only one way = WALL (deterministic, code it)
   → Can vary = DOOR or OPEN FLOOR (AI might handle it)
What MUST be true before this step can start?
   (preconditions — these are walls that prevent skipping ahead)
What are ALL the possible outcomes of this step?
   (if you can list them ALL = deterministic)
   (if they're infinite/unpredictable = AI territory)
For each outcome: where do you go next?
   (this draws the arrows between rooms)
How do you VERIFY this step was done correctly?
   (this is the validation — the wall you bounce off if 
   you try to cheat)
Can this step be skipped? Ever? Under any circumstance?
   → No = WALL
   → Yes, if [condition] = DOOR with a lock
That's it. Seven questions. Applied to every step of any process.
Let me show you with meta programs:
Step: Elicit Toward/Away
WHAT: Ask the client what they want in their context
ONLY ONE WAY? The core question is fixed. Delivery can vary.
   → The question itself = WALL
   → How you phrase it conversationally = DOOR (AI, constrained)
PRECONDITION: Context must already be established. 
   Can't run without it. WALL.
POSSIBLE OUTCOMES: Toward, Away, Toward-lean, Away-lean. 
   That's it. Four options. WALL.
NEXT: Move to Internal/External elicitation. Always. WALL.
VERIFY: Response must contain language matching one of the 
   four patterns. Check keywords. WALL.
SKIP? No. Never. WALL.
See how most of it is walls? The only "door" is how the AI phrases the question conversationally. Everything else is rigid.
Now compare that to the open-floor part:
Step: Explain results to client
WHAT: Summarize their meta program profile in plain language
ONLY ONE WAY? No — infinite ways to explain it. OPEN FLOOR.
PRECONDITION: All meta programs must be elicited. WALL.
POSSIBLE OUTCOMES: Unpredictable — depends on client. AI.
NEXT: End session or move to intervention. DOOR.
VERIFY: Must reference all identified meta programs. 
   Partial WALL (checklist validation).
SKIP? Yes, if client doesn't want explanation. DOOR.
The universal builder is those seven questions applied to every step. That's it. That's the whole thing. You feed in the process description, you run every step through those seven questions, and out comes your blueprint with walls, doors, and open rooms already mapped.
This isn't novel. It's systems engineering. It's process mapping. It's what industrial engineers have done for a hundred years. The only new part is: now you have AI that can fill the open rooms with real intelligence instead of dumb scripts.
What you should build: A template/tool that takes ANY practitioner discipline, runs every step through those seven questions, and outputs the deterministic scaffold automatically. Then you plug in whatever AI you want for the door and open-floor parts.
You've got the idea. Sleep on it. Tomorrow we map out one complete meta programs session through all seven questions, step by step, and you'll have your first real blueprint.
