The Verification Tiers — Full Playbook
Three tiers. Not two, not five. Three is the sweet spot.
Tier 1: PULSE CHECK (2-5 minutes)
What it does: "Does the code even compile? Did I break anything obvious?"
Lint check (ruff check / npm run lint)
Type check (mypy / npm run build)
Run existing tests (if any)
That's it. Three commands. Takes 2-5 minutes. Zero thinking.
When to use it:
After EVERY feature or edit, no exceptions
This is like checking your rearview mirror while driving — you just do it constantly
If you're automating with CLI, this runs after every single agent task automatically
Costs almost nothing in time or tokens
What it catches:
Typos, broken imports, syntax errors
Type mismatches (you changed a function but didn't update the callers)
Tests that were passing and now aren't
What it DOESN'T catch:
Logic bugs (code compiles but does the wrong thing)
UI looking broken
Database having wrong data
Features not actually working end-to-end
Analogy: You're tracing the picture, and you just glance down to make sure your pen is still on the line. Takes one second. Doesn't slow you down.
Tier 2: SEAM CHECK (10-20 minutes)
What it does: "Does the thing I just built actually work, and does it play nice with what was already there?"
Everything from Pulse Check, PLUS:
Start the app (confirm it boots without errors)
Test the SPECIFIC thing that just changed:
   - If you added a page → navigate to it, does it render?
   - If you added an API endpoint → hit it, does it return correct data?
   - If you changed the database → query it, is the data right?
Test ONE thing that depends on what you changed:
   - If you changed the login system → does the dashboard still load after login?
   - If you changed how data saves → does the page that displays that data still work?
Check console/terminal for errors or warnings
When to use it:
After any database or API change (you touched the plumbing — check for leaks)
After wiring two things together (frontend calling backend, service A calling service B)
Before building something that depends on what you just built
Roughly every 3-4 features in practice, but triggered by WHAT changed, not a timer
What it catches:
The feature looks right but sends wrong data to the database
The API returns data in a different shape than the frontend expects
The thing you built works alone but breaks when connected to existing code
60-70% of all bugs live at these connection points
What it DOESN'T catch:
Edge cases (what happens with empty data, weird inputs)
Responsive layout issues
Problems in features you DIDN'T test (the ones far away from what changed)
Subtle logic bugs in complex workflows
Analogy: You're tracing the picture, and you just finished a section. You lift the tracing paper and compare that section to the original. "Does this part match?" Takes 30 seconds. If it's off, you fix it now before you trace the next section on top of it.
Tier 3: FULL PROTOCOL (30-60 minutes)
What it does: Everything. The whole protocol your friend gave you. All 8 phases.
Investigation 1: Map every route, every journey, every component
Investigation 2: Database schema and all data flows
Investigation 3: Bug hunt — logic errors, UI issues, security, data integrity
Static verification — lint, type check, full test suite
Functional verification — start app, test EVERY user journey end-to-end
Database validation — query after every action, verify data is correct
Edge cases — empty states, bad input, rapid clicks, auth edge cases
Cross-feature integration — does Feature A's data show up correctly in Feature B?
Responsive/visual check
Fix everything found
Re-verify fixes
Final report
When to use it:
End of every phase in your PRD (non-negotiable)
End of a full build (obviously)
After major refactoring (you reorganized a bunch of stuff)
Before shipping / deploying anything
When something feels off but you can't pinpoint what
What it catches:
Everything the other two miss
The bugs hiding in features you haven't looked at in a while
The cascading failures where Feature 3 broke Feature 7 and nobody noticed
Edge cases that only happen with specific data combinations
The stuff that makes you say "how did we miss this?"
Analogy: You're done tracing a big section. You pull the paper completely off, hold it up next to the original, and compare the ENTIRE thing line by line. This is where you find that one line in the corner that drifted off two sections ago and has been getting worse ever since.
The Playbook: Which Tier, When
Here's your cheat sheet. Pin this to your wall:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AFTER EVERY FEATURE/EDIT:          → PULSE CHECK       │
│  (always, no exceptions, 2 min)                         │
│                                                         │
│  AFTER DATABASE/API CHANGES:        → SEAM CHECK        │
│  AFTER WIRING THINGS TOGETHER:      → SEAM CHECK        │
│  BEFORE BUILDING ON A DEPENDENCY:   → SEAM CHECK        │
│  (triggered by what changed, 10-20 min)                 │
│                                                         │
│  END OF EVERY PHASE:                → FULL PROTOCOL     │
│  END OF BUILD:                      → FULL PROTOCOL     │
│  BEFORE SHIPPING:                   → FULL PROTOCOL     │
│  SOMETHING FEELS WRONG:             → FULL PROTOCOL     │
│  (scheduled checkpoints, 30-60 min)                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Real Example: Your Typical Build
Say your PRD has 4 phases, 16 features total:
PHASE 1: Foundation (Features 1-4)
  Feature 1: Database schema + models          
    → PULSE CHECK (always)
    → SEAM CHECK (database change!)
  Feature 2: Auth system (login/signup)         
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (API endpoints + depends on Feature 1)
  Feature 3: Basic layout/navigation            
    → PULSE CHECK
  Feature 4: Dashboard page                     
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (wires frontend to backend, depends on Features 1-2)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
  → FULL PROTOCOL (end of phase)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
PHASE 2: Core Features (Features 5-8)
  Feature 5: User profile page                  
    → PULSE CHECK
  Feature 6: Data entry form + save to DB       
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (database change + API endpoint)
  Feature 7: Data display page (reads Feature 6 data)  
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (depends on Feature 6 — verify 6 works before building 7)
  Feature 8: Search/filter functionality         
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (new API endpoint)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
  → FULL PROTOCOL (end of phase)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
PHASE 3: Polish (Features 9-12)
  Feature 9: Error handling + loading states     
    → PULSE CHECK
  Feature 10: Form validation                    
    → PULSE CHECK
  Feature 11: Email notifications                
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (new service wired to existing features)
  Feature 12: Settings page                      
    → PULSE CHECK
  ════════════════════════════════════════
  → FULL PROTOCOL (end of phase)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
PHASE 4: Final (Features 13-16)
  Feature 13: Admin panel                        
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (new API endpoints + auth checks)
  Feature 14: Data export                        
    → PULSE CHECK
    → SEAM CHECK (reads from multiple tables)
  Feature 15: Mobile responsive                  
    → PULSE CHECK
  Feature 16: Performance optimization           
    → PULSE CHECK
  ════════════════════════════════════════
  → FULL PROTOCOL (end of build — the big one)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
Total verification runs in this build:
16 Pulse Checks (2 min each = ~32 min)
8 Seam Checks (15 min each = ~120 min)
4 Full Protocols (45 min each = ~180 min)
Total: ~5.5 hours of verification across the whole build
Sounds like a lot? You spent 287 hours debugging. This is 5.5 hours of prevention. And it's automated — you're not sitting there for any of it.
How the Triggers Work Automatically
The key insight for automation: you don't decide when to verify. The features themselves tell you.
Every feature in your PRD gets tagged:
[UI] — only changes visual stuff. Just Pulse Check.
[DATA] — touches database or API. Triggers Seam Check.
[WIRE] — connects two existing things. Triggers Seam Check.
[DEPENDS:X] — needs Feature X to work. Triggers Seam Check on Feature X first.
[PHASE-END] — last feature in a phase. Triggers Full Protocol.
Your PRD maker auto-tags these based on what the feature does. The CLI reads the tags and runs the right verification tier. User never thinks about it.

3 files, 244 lines of verification system added. Here's what was built:
What Got Built
create-spec.md — Phase 4V: Verification Checkpoint Injection (+117 lines)
Auto-tags every feature with [UI], [DATA], [API], [WIRE], [AUTH], [PHASE-END]
Assigns verification tier (PULSE / SEAM / FULL) based on tags
Injects checkpoints into the <implementation_steps> XML
Adds <verification_plan> to the generated app_spec.txt
Shows verification summary in Phase 7 review ("X pulse checks, Y seam checks, Z full verifies")
coding_prompt.template.md — STEP 4.7: Verification Checkpoints (+81 lines)
The coding agent now reads the verification tier from app_spec.txt
Tier 1 (PULSE): lint + type check + tests — every feature, 2-5 min
Tier 2 (SEAM): pulse + test the changed thing + one dependency — after data/API/wire/auth, 10-20 min
Tier 3 (FULL): complete protocol, all routes, all journeys, bug hunt, DB validation, edge cases — phase boundaries, 30-60 min
Outputs verification report to claude-progress.txt
Blocks next phase until Critical/High issues resolved
rant-to-prd-spec.md — Stage 6 Verification Injection (+46 lines)
PRD Compiler auto-tags features and injects checkpoints
Adds verification_plan to final PRD JSON output
Quality gate validates: verification_plan exists, every feature tagged, every phase ends with FULL_VERIFY
How It Works End-to-End
You describe your idea
  → PRD Maker (create-spec) asks questions, fills gaps
  → Phase 4V silently tags every feature and injects checkpoints
  → app_spec.txt comes out with verification baked in
  → Coding agent reads app_spec.txt
  → After each feature: checks its tier tag, runs the right verification
  → At phase boundaries: full protocol catches everything
  → You walk away, come back to clean code

ok a masterclass in everything I didn't understand about coding geeesh so if there's 3 versions of this you would use and the Full version or Version 3 is the full text script below ... what is the exact text script to use for version 1 & 2 or the lite and super lite versions
POST-BUILD VERIFICATION PROTOCOL POST-BUILD VERIFICATION PROTOCOL
Startup commands — exact commands to install dependencies and run the dev server, including URL and port
Authentication — if the app has protected routes, how to create a test account or log in (check .env.example, seed data, or sign-up flow)
Every user-facing route/page — each URL path and what it renders
Every user journey — complete flows a user can take (e.g., "sign up → create profile → view dashboard"). For each journey, list specific steps, interactions (clicks, form fills, navigation), and expected outcomes
Key UI components — forms, modals, dropdowns, pickers, toggles, and other interactive elements Be exhaustive. Testing will only cover what you identify here. Investigation 2: Database Schema & Data Flows Research the database layer. Read .env.example to understand connection variables. DO NOT read .env directly. Document:
Database type and connection — what database is used (Postgres, SQLite, etc.) and the environment variable for the connection string
Full schema — every table, columns, types, and relationships
Data flows per user action — for each user-facing action, document exactly what records are created, updated, or deleted and in which tables
Validation queries — for each data flow, provide the exact query to verify records are correct after the action Investigation 3: Bug Hunting (Code Analysis) Analyze the codebase for potential issues. Focus on:
Logic errors — incorrect conditionals, off-by-one errors, missing null checks, race conditions
UI/UX issues — missing error handling in forms, no loading states, broken layouts, accessibility problems
Data integrity risks — missing validation, potential orphaned records, incorrect cascade behavior
Security concerns — injection vulnerabilities, XSS, missing auth checks, exposed secrets Return a prioritized list with file paths and line numbers. PHASE 2: STATIC VERIFICATION Before touching the browser, verify the code compiles and passes static analysis. 2a. Lint & Type Check
For TypeScript/JavaScript projects npm run lint npm run build # Catches type errors # For Python projects ruff check . mypy .
If lint or type errors exist — fix them now. Do not proceed with broken code. 2b. Run Existing Test Suites
Unit tests npm test # or pytest, or whatever the project uses # Generated E2E tests (if they exist) npx playwright test tests/e2e/ # API tests npx playwright test tests/api/
Document every failure. Fix what you can before proceeding. 2c. Dependency & Import Audit
Check for unused imports in files you modified
Verify no circular dependencies were introduced
Confirm all new dependencies are in package.json / requirements.txt
Run npm ls or pip check to verify dependency tree is clean PHASE 3: FUNCTIONAL VERIFICATION For each feature you built, verify it actually works end-to-end. 3a. Start the Application
Install dependencies if needed
Start the dev server
Confirm it starts without errors
Check the console/terminal output for warnings 3b. Test Every User Journey For each user journey identified in Investigation 1:
Navigate to the starting point
Execute each step in the journey
Verify the expected outcome at each step
Check for errors — console errors, network failures, unexpected behavior
Document what you find If you have browser automation tools available (Playwright MCP, agent-browser, etc.), use them to:
Take screenshots at key steps
Capture console output
Monitor network requests
Interact with forms and buttons If you do NOT have browser tools, verify through:
API endpoint testing with curl/fetch
Database queries to confirm data flows
Log output analysis
Code path tracing (manually walk through the logic) 3c. Database Validation After any interaction that modifies data:
Query the database to verify records were created/updated/deleted correctly
SQLite: sqlite3 path/to/db.sqlite "SELECT * FROM table WHERE condition"
Postgres: psql "$DATABASE_URL" -c "SELECT * FROM table WHERE condition"
Verify:
Records match what was entered in the UI
Relationships between records are correct
No orphaned or duplicate records
Timestamps are reasonable
Default values applied correctly 3d. Edge Cases & Error States Test these explicitly:
Empty states — what happens with no data?
Invalid input — submit forms with missing/malformed data
Boundary values — very long strings, zero values, negative numbers
Concurrent operations — rapid clicks, duplicate submissions
Network failures — what happens when API calls fail?
Auth edge cases — expired sessions, unauthorized access attempts PHASE 4: CROSS-FEATURE INTEGRATION Features don't exist in isolation. Test interactions between them:
Data dependencies — does Feature B correctly read data created by Feature A?
UI state — does navigating between features maintain correct state?
Side effects — does modifying data in one feature break another?
Shared components — do shared UI components behave consistently across features? PHASE 5: RESPONSIVE & VISUAL CHECK If browser tools are available, check key pages at these viewports: DeviceWidthHeightMobile375812Tablet7681024Desktop1440900 Look for:
Layout overflow or horizontal scrolling
Overlapping elements
Unreadable text sizes
Touch targets too small on mobile
Missing responsive breakpoints If browser tools are NOT available, review CSS/Tailwind classes for responsive patterns:
Check for sm:, md:, lg: breakpoint usage
Verify flex/grid layouts handle different widths
Look for hardcoded pixel widths that should be responsive PHASE 6: ISSUE HANDLING When you find an issue:
Document it — expected vs actual behavior, file path, line number
Classify severity:
Critical — app crashes, data loss, security vulnerability
High — feature doesn't work, wrong data displayed
Medium — UI glitch, poor UX, missing error message
Low — cosmetic, minor text issue
Fix critical and high issues immediately
Re-verify the fix works
Document medium/low issues for follow-up if time is limited PHASE 7: FINAL VERIFICATION PASS After all fixes:
Re-run lint and type check — confirm still clean
Re-run test suites — confirm nothing broke
Restart the dev server fresh — confirm clean startup
Quick smoke test of each feature — confirm still working
Check git status — no untracked files that should be committed PHASE 8: REPORT Output a structured summary:
Verification Report Features Verified: [count] User Journeys Tested: [count] Issues Found: [count] ([count] fixed, [count] remaining) ### Static Analysis - Lint: PASS/FAIL - Type Check: PASS/FAIL - Tests: [X] passing, [Y] failing ### Issues Fixed - [Description] — [file:line] — [severity] ### Remaining Issues - [Description] — [file:line] — [severity] ### Bug Hunt Findings (Code Analysis) - [Description] — [severity] — [file:line] ### Database Validation - [Table/query results summary] ### Notes - [Anything the next agent or developer should know]
IMPORTANT RULES
Fix as you go — don't just document issues, fix them
Never skip database validation — the UI looking right doesn't mean the data is right
Test with real data — not mocks, not empty states (unless testing empty states)
Check console output — zero errors, zero unhandled warnings
Commit your fixes — verification that finds bugs and fixes them is worth nothing if you don't save the work
Be thorough — the goal is that by the time this finishes, every part of the application has been exercised and verified
Show more
Here are all three versions — ready to copy-paste:
Version 1: PULSE CHECK (2-5 minutes)
The "glance in the rearview mirror." Use after every single feature or edit.
PULSE CHECK PROTOCOL
Run these commands. All must pass before you move on to the next task:
Lint check:
   - TypeScript/JavaScript: npm run lint
   - Python: ruff check .
Type check:
   - TypeScript/JavaScript: npm run build
   - Python: mypy .
Run existing tests (if any exist):
   - npm test 2>/dev/null || true
   - npx playwright test tests/e2e/ 2>/dev/null || true
   - pytest 2>/dev/null || true
Check for unused imports in files you just modified
Confirm all new dependencies are in package.json or requirements.txt
If ANY lint or type errors exist — fix them NOW. Do not proceed with broken code. Do not skip this. Do not say "I'll fix it later." Fix it now, re-run, confirm clean.
IMPORTANT RULES:
Zero errors. Zero warnings. No exceptions.
If a test that was passing is now failing, you broke something. Fix it before moving on.
This should take 2-5 minutes. If it's taking longer, something is wrong.
Version 2: SEAM CHECK (10-20 minutes)
The "lift the tracing paper and compare this section." Use after any database change, API change, auth change, or when you wire two things together.
SEAM CHECK PROTOCOL
STEP 1: Run the full Pulse Check first (lint, type check, tests). Fix any issues.
STEP 2: Start/restart the application
Install dependencies if needed
Start the dev server
Confirm it starts without errors
Check the console/terminal output for warnings — zero warnings allowed
STEP 3: Test the specific thing you just built
If you added or changed a DATABASE TABLE:
  * Connect to the database directly
  * Verify the table exists with correct columns, types, and relationships
  * Create a test record, read it back, verify it matches
  * Delete the test record, confirm it's gone
  
If you added or changed an API ENDPOINT:
  * Call the endpoint with valid data, verify correct response
  * Call it with invalid data, verify it returns proper error (not 500)
  * Check that auth is enforced (unauthorized request gets 401/403)
If you WIRED frontend to backend:
  * Navigate to the page in the browser
  * Verify real data displays (not placeholder/mock data)
  * Create something through the UI, verify it appears
  * Refresh the page, verify it persists
  * Check browser console — zero errors
If you changed AUTH/PERMISSIONS:
  * Test login flow end-to-end
  * Verify protected routes block unauthenticated access
  * Verify role-based access (admin vs user) if applicable
  * Test logout, confirm session is cleared
STEP 4: Test ONE thing that depends on what you just built
Pick one feature that uses or builds on your change
Verify it still works correctly
If it's broken, fix it now
STEP 5: Check console and terminal output
Browser console: zero errors, zero unhandled warnings
Server terminal: no error messages, no stack traces
Network tab: no failed requests (no 500s, no 404s on valid routes)
IMPORTANT RULES:
If any check fails — fix it NOW. Do not move to the next feature.
Never skip database validation — the UI looking right doesn't mean the data is right.
This should take 10-20 minutes. If longer, you likely found real bugs — that's good, fix them.
Commit your fixes before moving on.
Version 3: FULL VERIFY (30-60 minutes)
The full protocol — your friend's original script. This is the "hold the tracing paper up to the original and compare the entire picture." Use at the end of every phase and end of every build.
That's the one you already have — the complete 8-phase protocol you pasted.
Quick Reference Card
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    WHEN TO USE WHAT                          │
│                                                             │
│  JUST FINISHED A FEATURE/EDIT:        → PULSE CHECK (V1)   │
│  "Does my code even compile?"           Copy-paste V1       │
│  2-5 min. Every time. No exceptions.                        │
│                                                             │
│  CHANGED DATABASE / API / AUTH:        → SEAM CHECK (V2)    │
│  WIRED TWO THINGS TOGETHER:            Copy-paste V2        │
│  ABOUT TO BUILD ON A DEPENDENCY:                            │
│  10-20 min. Targeted.                                       │
│                                                             │
│  END OF A PHASE:                       → FULL VERIFY (V3)   │
│  END OF A BUILD:                        Copy-paste V3       │
│  BEFORE SHIPPING:                       (your friend's      │
│  SOMETHING FEELS WRONG:                 full protocol)      │
│  30-60 min. Everything.                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
All three are now also baked into your PRD system, so when your automated CLI loop runs a build, the agent knows which version to run and when — without you or anyone else having to remember to paste anything.

Show more
Why Nobody Told You
The honest answer: coders don't think testing needs to be explained because to them it's like telling someone to breathe.
Testing is taught in the first semester of computer science. It's so fundamental that every professional coder assumes everyone knows it. It's like a chef not mentioning "taste your food while you cook" — they can't imagine someone not doing it.
But here's the gap nobody saw coming: vibe coding skipped the education entirely. You went from "I have an idea" straight to "AI, build this" — bypassing the 4 years of school where testing gets drilled in. The AI tools (Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code) don't mention it either because they were built BY coders FOR coders. Nobody designed the onboarding for someone who's never written a line of code.
Your friend didn't realize the value of what he gave you because to him it's like handing someone a fork. He doesn't think about forks. He's been using one his whole life.
That's your marketing angle right there. "The thing every coder knows but nobody told the vibe coders."
The 3 Magic Tricks
Here are three demos that prove this in seconds, are visually undeniable, and link to third-party proof:
Magic Trick #1: "Show Me Your Code, I'll Show You Your Bugs"
The trick: Ask someone to paste ANY 50 lines of their vibe-coded app into a live demo. Run lint + type check on it in real time. Watch 5-15 errors pop up instantly — red lines, error counts, specific file and line numbers.
Why it's a jaw-dropper: They wrote this code. They thought it worked. They're USING it right now. And in 3 seconds, a machine just found 12 things wrong with it that they had no idea existed. Some of those errors are why their app "randomly crashes sometimes" or "that one page never loads right."
The proof link: Send them to ESLint's rules page (eslint.org/docs/rules) or TypeScript's error codes (typescriptlang.org/docs). Every error has a name, a description, and an explanation of why it matters. It's not your opinion. It's not AI making stuff up. It's the same rules that Microsoft, Google, and Meta use on their code. Wikipedia-level authority.
The visual: Split screen. Left side: their code looking clean and normal. Right side: the same code with red underlines everywhere like a teacher grading a paper. Counter ticking up: 1... 4... 7... 12 errors found. They can see their own code bleeding red.
The analogy: "You know how spell check puts red squiggles under misspelled words? This is spell check for code. You've been writing an entire book without spell check turned on."
Magic Trick #2: "The Restart Test" (Data Persistence)
The trick: Take their running app. Create something in it — a post, a record, whatever their app does. Show it on screen. Now kill the server and restart it. The data is gone.
Why it's a jaw-dropper: They had NO IDEA their data wasn't real. They've been using their app, adding things, and it all looked fine. But the moment the server restarts — which happens during updates, crashes, deployments, or literally just closing the terminal — everything vanishes. Their app has been a whiteboard, not a filing cabinet.
The proof link: Send them to SQLite documentation (sqlite.org) or any "in-memory vs persistent storage" explainer. The concept is binary — data either survives a restart or it doesn't. There's no gray area. This is computer science 101, and it's on every database documentation site in existence.
The visual: Screen recording. "Here's your app. Here's the thing you created. Watch." Terminal: kill server. 5 second pause. start server. Navigate back. Empty. Gone. Their face does the rest. Then show the same test AFTER the verification protocol fixed it — data survives. Side by side. Before/after.
The analogy: "Imagine you wrote a 10-page document, saved it, closed your laptop, opened it back up, and the document was blank. That's what's happening to your app right now. You just didn't know because you never closed the laptop."
Magic Trick #3: "The Console Truth" (Hidden Errors)
The trick: Open their app in a browser. Open the browser console (F12 → Console tab). Navigate through their app normally. Watch the red errors cascade — 5, 10, 20, 50 errors they've never seen because they never opened the console.
Why it's a jaw-dropper: Their app LOOKS like it works. The pages load. The buttons seem to do things. But underneath, the console is screaming. Failed API calls, null reference errors, unhandled promises, CORS failures. Every red line is a bug hiding in plain sight. Some of these are why "it works on my computer but not my friend's" or "sometimes it just shows a blank screen."
The proof link: MDN Web Docs (developer.mozilla.org) — Mozilla's documentation. Every error in the console has a name that maps to an MDN article. TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined → here's the MDN page explaining exactly what that means and why it breaks your app. It's the Wikipedia of web development, maintained by Mozilla (the Firefox people), not some random blog.
The visual: Split screen again. Top: their app looking normal, everything seems fine. Bottom: the console, a wall of red. Counter: errors per page navigation. Click Home: 3 errors. Click Profile: 7 errors. Click Settings: 12 errors. Total: 22 hidden errors in a "working" app. Then show the same walkthrough AFTER verification — console is clean. Zero red.
The analogy: "Your car dashboard has a check engine light. Right now, your app's check engine light has been on for months. You just didn't know there was a dashboard. I'm showing you the dashboard."
The Presentation Flow
HOOK: "Nobody told you this. Not Cursor. Not Claude. Not any 
   YouTube tutorial. The thing every professional coder knows 
   that vibe coders were never taught."
TRACING ANALOGY: Visual of tracing paper, the picture drifting,
   the reveal at the end. "You've been drawing from memory instead
   of tracing. Here's what that looks like in code."
MAGIC TRICK #1 (Lint): "Give me 50 lines of your code."
   → Instant red lines. Proof link: eslint.org
MAGIC TRICK #2 (Restart): "Let me restart your app."  
   → Data gone. Proof link: sqlite.org
MAGIC TRICK #3 (Console): "Let me open your dashboard."
   → Wall of red errors. Proof link: developer.mozilla.org
THE SOLVE: "All three of these get caught automatically, 
   every few features, without you thinking about it. 
   That's what this does."
CTA: "Don't believe me. Go check. Here are the links.
   This isn't my opinion. This is how code works.
   I just built the thing that checks it for you."
The beauty: all three tricks work on ANY vibe-coded app. Every single one will have lint errors, probably has in-memory storage, and definitely has console errors. It's not a gamble — it's deterministic, like you said. You're not hoping it fails. You KNOW it fails. That's the confidence of a magician who knows the card is already in the right pocket.

The bug spray mascot concept has legs — a character that "exterminates" code bugs with WWE energy is memorable and endlessly memeable. The format writes itself: before/after screenshots of real bug catches, the spray character doing finishing moves on different bug types (the "Null Pointer Piledriver," the "Type Error Tombstone"), short-form video of actual lint scans with commentary.
For the product name, you want something that sounds like pest control meets coding. Think along the lines of: BugBlaster, CodeRaid, DebugSpray, Exterminator.dev — something a non-coder would laugh at and remember.
The content flywheel: every time someone runs the protocol and catches bugs, that's a screenshot/clip. User-generated content where people share their "kill count" per build. "I found 12 bugs in a single build" becomes social proof AND entertainment.
What do you want to tackle next? You've got the verification system committed — ready to push and deploy, or pivot to something else?
MARTINS 1500 LINE BUILD PROMPT 
IDEA CODE: App Builder Prompt Template
How To Use This Template
Step 1: Clarify Your App Idea (Optional)

Have a rough idea but not sure how to describe it?
Use the App Idea Generator Prompt (separate file) to structure it
Paste the output into Sections 1 & 2 below
Step 2: Generate Your Style Guide (Optional but Recommended)
Find a screenshot of an app/website whose visual style you love
Use the Style Guide Generator Prompt (separate file) with that image
Paste the output into Section 3 below
Step 3: Fill In Any Remaining Details
Review Sections 1, 2, and 3
Fill in anything that's still blank
Step 4: Generate Your App
Copy everything below "THE PROMPT" line
Paste into Google AI Studio "Build" mode
After generation, add your Firebase config to config/firebase.ts
DO NOT MODIFY Section 4 - these are the technical guardrails that make it work.
THE PROMPT (Copy Everything Below This Line)
SECTION 1: APP IDENTITY [FILL THIS IN]
App Name: [YOUR_APP_NAME]
One-Line Description: [What does this app do in one sentence?]
Target User: [Who is this for? Be specific]
Core Problem It Solves: [What pain point does this eliminate?]
SECTION 2: FEATURES [FILL THIS IN]
Core Features (3-5 max):

[Feature 1 - be specific about what it does]
[Feature 2]
[Feature 3]
[Feature 4 - optional]
[Feature 5 - optional]
What Users Can Do:
[Main action 1 - e.g., "Create and save recipes"]
[Main action 2 - e.g., "Organize recipes into collections"]
[Main action 3 - e.g., "Search their saved recipes"]
SECTION 3: STYLE GUIDE [FILL THIS IN OR PASTE FROM STYLE GENERATOR]
Tip: Use the Style Guide Generator Prompt with a screenshot of an app you like, then paste the output here.
Visual Style: [Modern/Minimal/Playful/Corporate]
Primary Brand Color: [e.g., #DFFF5E or "electric lime"]
Secondary Color: [e.g., #10B981 or "emerald green"]
Personality/Tone: [Friendly/Professional/Casual/Serious]
Design Inspiration: [Optional - "Like Notion" or "Like Linear" or "Clean SaaS dashboard"]
SECTION 4: TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS [DO NOT MODIFY]
STACK (MANDATORY)
React 19 with TypeScript
Tailwind CSS for all styling
Firebase Authentication (Google Sign-In only)
Cloud Firestore for database
React Context for auth state
NO external state libraries
NO Docker
NO backend APIs
FILE STRUCTURE (MINIMUM BASE - EXPAND AS NEEDED)
src/
├── App.tsx                    # Main app with routing
├── main.tsx                   # Entry point
├── index.css                  # Tailwind imports only
├── config/
│   └── firebase.ts            # Firebase configuration
├── contexts/
│   ├── AuthContext.tsx        # Auth state + user profile with role
│   ├── ThemeContext.tsx       # Dark mode state + toggle
│   ├── ToastContext.tsx       # Toast notification state
│   └── [FeatureName]Context.tsx  # ADD contexts for complex state
├── hooks/
│   ├── useAuth.ts             # Auth hook (re-exported from context)
│   ├── usePageTitle.ts        # Dynamic document title
│   └── use[Feature].ts        # ADD custom hooks per feature
├── components/
│   ├── ProtectedRoute.tsx     # Route guard - any authenticated user
│   ├── AdminRoute.tsx         # Route guard - admin only
│   ├── ProRoute.tsx           # Route guard - pro/admin only (optional)
│   ├── ErrorBoundary.tsx      # Catches crashes, shows fallback UI
│   ├── Layout.tsx             # Main layout - handles responsive sidebar
│   ├── Sidebar.tsx            # Sidebar nav + help link at bottom
│   ├── MobileNav.tsx          # Mobile header with hamburger toggle
│   ├── ui/                    # REQUIRED reusable UI components
│   │   ├── Modal.tsx          # REQUIRED - for confirmations, dialogs
│   │   ├── ConfirmModal.tsx   # REQUIRED - delete/destructive action confirmation
│   │   ├── Toast.tsx          # REQUIRED - success/error notifications
│   │   ├── Button.tsx         # REQUIRED - with loading state support
│   │   ├── Avatar.tsx         # REQUIRED - user avatar with fallback
│   │   ├── ThemeToggle.tsx    # REQUIRED - dark/light mode toggle
│   │   ├── Card.tsx           # Standard card wrapper
│   │   ├── Skeleton.tsx       # REQUIRED - loading placeholders
│   │   ├── EmptyState.tsx     # REQUIRED - empty list states with CTA
│   │   └── Spinner.tsx        # Loading spinner
│   └── [FeatureName]/         # ADD folders grouping feature components
├── pages/
│   ├── LandingPage.tsx        # Public landing page
│   ├── LoginPage.tsx          # Login page
│   ├── Dashboard.tsx          # Main authenticated view (list view)
│   ├── Profile.tsx            # User profile + delete account (danger zone)
│   ├── NotFoundPage.tsx       # 404 page
│   ├── [Item]DetailPage.tsx   # ADD: Detail view for saved items
│   ├── [Item]CreatePage.tsx   # ADD: Create new item
│   └── [Item]EditPage.tsx     # ADD: Edit existing item (if separate from create)
├── services/
│   └── firestore.ts           # All Firestore operations + deleteUserAccount
├── utils/
│   ├── formatDate.ts          # Date formatting helpers (relative time)
│   └── pluralize.ts           # Pluralization helper (1 item vs 2 items)
└── types/
    └── index.ts               # ALL TypeScript interfaces (UserProfile, etc.)

File rules: One component per file. Group related components in feature folders. Create interfaces for all data types. Add custom hooks for reusable logic.

CRITICAL: index.html (COPY EXACTLY - DO NOT MODIFY IMPORTMAP)
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
    <title>APP_NAME</title>
    <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>
    <script>
      tailwind.config = {
        darkMode: 'class',
        theme: {
          extend: {
            colors: {
              brand: {
                light: 'PRIMARY_COLOR_LIGHT',
                DEFAULT: 'PRIMARY_COLOR',
                dark: 'PRIMARY_COLOR_DARK',
              },
              // Light mode colors (defaults)
              surface: {
                canvas: '#F9FAFB',
                base: '#FFFFFF',
                muted: '#F3F4F6',
              },
              text: {
                primary: '#111827',
                secondary: '#6B7280',
                tertiary: '#9CA3AF',
              },
              border: {
                subtle: '#E5E7EB',
              }
            },
            fontFamily: {
              sans: ['Inter', 'ui-sans-serif', 'system-ui', 'sans-serif'],
            },
            borderRadius: {
              card: '12px',
            },
            boxShadow: {
              card: '0 1px 3px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05), 0 1px 2px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.03)',
            },
          }
        }
      }
    </script>
    <style>
      /* Dark mode overrides - applied when .dark class is on html */
      .dark {
        --color-surface-canvas: #0F172A;
        --color-surface-base: #1E293B;
        --color-surface-muted: #334155;
        --color-text-primary: #F1F5F9;
        --color-text-secondary: #94A3B8;
        --color-text-tertiary: #64748B;
        --color-border-subtle: #334155;
      }
      .dark .bg-surface-canvas { background-color: var(--color-surface-canvas); }
      .dark .bg-surface-base { background-color: var(--color-surface-base); }
      .dark .bg-surface-muted { background-color: var(--color-surface-muted); }
      .dark .text-text-primary { color: var(--color-text-primary); }
      .dark .text-text-secondary { color: var(--color-text-secondary); }
      .dark .text-text-tertiary { color: var(--color-text-tertiary); }
      .dark .border-border-subtle { border-color: var(--color-border-subtle); }
      .dark .bg-white { background-color: var(--color-surface-base); }
      .dark .shadow-card { box-shadow: 0 1px 3px 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3); }
    </style>
    <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    <script type="importmap">
    {
      "imports": {
        "react": "https://esm.sh/react@^19.2.3",
        "react/": "https://esm.sh/react@^19.2.3/",
        "react-dom/": "https://esm.sh/react-dom@^19.2.3/",
        "react-router-dom": "https://esm.sh/react-router-dom@^7.12.0",
        "firebase/": "https://esm.sh/firebase@^12.8.0/",
        "lucide-react": "https://esm.sh/lucide-react"
      }
    }
    </script>
  </head>
  <body class="bg-surface-canvas font-sans text-text-primary antialiased">
    <div id="root"></div>
    <script type="module" src="./index.tsx"></script>
  </body>
</html>

IMPORTANT: DARK-FIRST STYLE GUIDES
If the style guide in Section 3 is dark-themed (dark backgrounds, light text), you MUST:

Use CSS variables for BOTH modes - not hardcoded hex values
Set light mode values in :root (the default)
Set dark mode values in .dark class
Example for a dark-first design:
:root {
  /* Light mode (yes, still define this even if style guide is dark) */
  --color-surface-canvas: #F9FAFB;
  --color-surface-base: #FFFFFF;
  --color-text-primary: #111827;
}
.dark {
  /* Dark mode - use the style guide's dark colors here */
  --color-surface-canvas: #05010D;
  --color-surface-base: #0F0A1F;
  --color-text-primary: #FFFFFF;
}

DO NOT hardcode dark colors directly in Tailwind config. Use var(--color-*) references so the theme toggle works.

IF YOUR APP USES GEMINI AI (Image Generation, etc.)
Add this single line to the importmap:

"@google/genai": "https://esm.sh/@google/genai"

DO NOT pin a version number. Let esm.sh resolve the latest compatible version.
IMPORTMAP IS LOCKED. DO NOT MODIFY.
⚠️ CRITICAL: DO NOT ADD firebase/app, firebase/auth, or firebase/firestore to the importmap!
⚠️ The "firebase/" trailing slash handles ALL Firebase imports. Adding specific paths will BREAK the app.
⚠️ DO NOT change versions. DO NOT add extra imports. COPY THE IMPORTMAP EXACTLY AS SHOWN.

FIREBASE CONFIGURATION (EXACT PATTERN)
config/firebase.ts:

// CRITICAL: Import order matters - app must be initialized FIRST
import { initializeApp } from 'firebase/app';
import { getAuth, GoogleAuthProvider } from 'firebase/auth';
import { getFirestore } from 'firebase/firestore';
// USER: Paste your Firebase config here from Firebase Console
const firebaseConfig = {
  apiKey: "YOUR_API_KEY",
  authDomain: "YOUR_PROJECT.firebaseapp.com",
  projectId: "YOUR_PROJECT_ID",
  storageBucket: "YOUR_PROJECT.appspot.com",
  messagingSenderId: "YOUR_SENDER_ID",
  appId: "YOUR_APP_ID"
};
// Step 1: Initialize app FIRST
const app = initializeApp(firebaseConfig);
// Step 2: THEN get auth/firestore
export const auth = getAuth(app);
export const googleProvider = new GoogleAuthProvider();
export const db = getFirestore(app);

AUTH CONTEXT (EXACT PATTERN)
contexts/AuthContext.tsx:

import { createContext, useContext, useEffect, useState, ReactNode } from 'react';
import { User, onAuthStateChanged, signInWithPopup, signOut } from 'firebase/auth';
import { auth, googleProvider, db } from '../config/firebase';
import { doc, setDoc, getDoc, serverTimestamp } from 'firebase/firestore';
// User profile stored in Firestore (includes role)
export interface UserProfile {
  uid: string;
  email: string;
  displayName: string;
  photoURL: string | null;
  role: 'user' | 'pro' | 'admin';
  createdAt: any;
  updatedAt: any;
}
interface AuthContextType {
  user: User | null;           // Firebase auth user
  userProfile: UserProfile | null;  // Firestore profile with role
  loading: boolean;
  signInWithGoogle: () => Promise<void>;
  logout: () => Promise<void>;
  // Convenience helpers
  isAdmin: boolean;
  isPro: boolean;
}
const AuthContext = createContext<AuthContextType | undefined>(undefined);
export function AuthProvider({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);
  const [userProfile, setUserProfile] = useState<UserProfile | null>(null);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
  useEffect(() => {
    const unsubscribe = onAuthStateChanged(auth, async (firebaseUser) => {
      if (firebaseUser) {
        const userRef = doc(db, 'users', firebaseUser.uid);
        const userSnap = await getDoc(userRef);
       
        if (!userSnap.exists()) {
          // First sign-in: create profile with default role
          const newProfile: UserProfile = {
            uid: firebaseUser.uid,
            email: firebaseUser.email || '',
            displayName: firebaseUser.displayName || '',
            photoURL: firebaseUser.photoURL,
            role: 'user',  // Default role - change via Firebase Console
            createdAt: serverTimestamp(),
            updatedAt: serverTimestamp()
          };
          await setDoc(userRef, newProfile);
          setUserProfile({ ...newProfile, createdAt: new Date(), updatedAt: new Date() });
        } else {
          // Existing user: fetch profile
          const profile = userSnap.data() as UserProfile;
          setUserProfile(profile);
          // Update last login
          await setDoc(userRef, { updatedAt: serverTimestamp() }, { merge: true });
        }
        setUser(firebaseUser);
      } else {
        setUser(null);
        setUserProfile(null);
      }
      setLoading(false);
    });
    return unsubscribe;
  }, []);
  const signInWithGoogle = async () => {
    await signInWithPopup(auth, googleProvider);
  };
  const logout = async () => {
    await signOut(auth);
    setUserProfile(null);
  };
  // Convenience helpers for role checks
  const isAdmin = userProfile?.role === 'admin';
  const isPro = userProfile?.role === 'pro' || userProfile?.role === 'admin';
  return (
    <AuthContext.Provider value={{
      user,
      userProfile,
      loading,
      signInWithGoogle,
      logout,
      isAdmin,
      isPro
    }}>
      {children}
    </AuthContext.Provider>
  );
}
export function useAuth() {
  const context = useContext(AuthContext);
  if (!context) throw new Error('useAuth must be used within AuthProvider');
  return context;
}

THEME CONTEXT (DARK MODE)
contexts/ThemeContext.tsx:

import { createContext, useContext, useEffect, useState, ReactNode } from 'react';
type Theme = 'light' | 'dark';
interface ThemeContextType {
  theme: Theme;
  toggleTheme: () => void;
  setTheme: (theme: Theme) => void;
}
const ThemeContext = createContext<ThemeContextType | undefined>(undefined);
export function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setThemeState] = useState<Theme>(() => {
    // Check localStorage first, then system preference
    if (typeof window !== 'undefined') {
      const stored = localStorage.getItem('theme') as Theme;
      if (stored) return stored;
      if (window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches) return 'dark';
    }
    return 'light';
  });
  useEffect(() => {
    // Apply theme to document
    const root = document.documentElement;
    if (theme === 'dark') {
      root.classList.add('dark');
    } else {
      root.classList.remove('dark');
    }
    localStorage.setItem('theme', theme);
  }, [theme]);
  const toggleTheme = () => setThemeState(t => t === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light');
  const setTheme = (t: Theme) => setThemeState(t);
  return (
    <ThemeContext.Provider value={{ theme, toggleTheme, setTheme }}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext.Provider>
  );
}
export function useTheme() {
  const context = useContext(ThemeContext);
  if (!context) throw new Error('useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider');
  return context;
}

components/ui/ThemeToggle.tsx:

import { Moon, Sun } from 'lucide-react';
import { useTheme } from '../../contexts/ThemeContext';
export default function ThemeToggle() {
  const { theme, toggleTheme } = useTheme();
 
  return (
    <button
      onClick={toggleTheme}
      aria-label={`Switch to ${theme === 'light' ? 'dark' : 'light'} mode`}
      className="p-2 rounded-lg hover:bg-surface-muted transition-colors"
    >
      {theme === 'light' ? (
        <Moon className="w-5 h-5 text-text-secondary" />
      ) : (
        <Sun className="w-5 h-5 text-text-secondary" />
      )}
    </button>
  );
}

Add ThemeProvider to App.tsx (wrap inside AuthProvider):

<AuthProvider>
  <ThemeProvider>
    <ToastProvider>
      ...
    </ToastProvider>
  </ThemeProvider>
</AuthProvider>

Add ThemeToggle to header (next to user avatar):

<header className="h-16 border-b border-border-subtle bg-surface-base flex items-center justify-between px-6">
  <Logo />
  <div className="flex items-center gap-4">
    <ThemeToggle />
    <Avatar />
    <SignOutButton />
  </div>
</header>

PROTECTED ROUTE (EXACT PATTERN)
components/ProtectedRoute.tsx:

import { Navigate } from 'react-router-dom';
import { useAuth } from '../contexts/AuthContext';
import Spinner from './ui/Spinner';
// Basic auth protection - any logged-in user
export default function ProtectedRoute({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const { user, loading } = useAuth();
  if (loading) return <Spinner />;
  if (!user) return <Navigate to="/login" replace />;
  return <>{children}</>;
}

components/AdminRoute.tsx:

import { Navigate } from 'react-router-dom';
import { useAuth } from '../contexts/AuthContext';
import Spinner from './ui/Spinner';
// Admin-only protection
export default function AdminRoute({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const { user, userProfile, loading, isAdmin } = useAuth();
  if (loading) return <Spinner />;
  if (!user) return <Navigate to="/login" replace />;
  if (!isAdmin) return <Navigate to="/dashboard" replace />;  // Redirect non-admins
  return <>{children}</>;
}

components/ProRoute.tsx (optional - for premium features):

import { Navigate } from 'react-router-dom';
import { useAuth } from '../contexts/AuthContext';
import Spinner from './ui/Spinner';
// Pro or Admin only
export default function ProRoute({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const { user, loading, isPro } = useAuth();
  if (loading) return <Spinner />;
  if (!user) return <Navigate to="/login" replace />;
  if (!isPro) return <Navigate to="/upgrade" replace />;  // Redirect to upgrade page
  return <>{children}</>;
}

Usage in App.tsx routes:

// Public routes
<Route path="/" element={<LandingPage />} />
<Route path="/login" element={<LoginPage />} />
// Protected routes (any authenticated user)
<Route path="/dashboard" element={<ProtectedRoute><Dashboard /></ProtectedRoute>} />
<Route path="/profile" element={<ProtectedRoute><Profile /></ProtectedRoute>} />
// Admin-only routes
<Route path="/admin" element={<AdminRoute><AdminDashboard /></AdminRoute>} />
<Route path="/admin/users" element={<AdminRoute><UserManagement /></AdminRoute>} />
// Pro-only routes (optional)
<Route path="/pro-feature" element={<ProRoute><ProFeature /></ProRoute>} />

Conditional UI based on role:

// In any component
const { isAdmin, isPro, userProfile } = useAuth();
// Show admin-only button
{isAdmin && (
  <Link to="/admin" className="text-text-secondary hover:text-text-primary">
    Admin Dashboard
  </Link>
)}
// Show upgrade prompt for free users
{!isPro && (
  <Card className="bg-brand/10 border-brand">
    <p>Upgrade to Pro for unlimited generations</p>
    <Button>Upgrade Now</Button>
  </Card>
)}
// Show current role (for debugging/profile)
<p>Account type: {userProfile?.role}</p>

FIRESTORE DATA STRUCTURE
User Profile Document: users/{uid}

// Created on first sign-in, role only editable via Firebase Console
{
  email: string;
  displayName: string;
  photoURL: string | null;
  role: 'user' | 'pro' | 'admin';  // Default: 'user'
  createdAt: Timestamp;
  updatedAt: Timestamp;
}

User Data Subcollections: users/{uid}/{collectionName}/{documentId}
All user-specific data stored in subcollections under their user document.
Role Definitions:

user - Default role, basic access
pro - Premium features (if applicable)
admin - Full access, can view admin dashboard
Security Rules (Firebase Console):
rules_version = '2';
service cloud.firestore {
  match /databases/{database}/documents {
    // User profile - users can read own, only create (not update role)
    match /users/{userId} {
      allow read: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId;
      allow create: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId
                    && request.resource.data.role == 'user'; // Force default role
      // role field can only be changed via Firebase Console (admin SDK)
      allow update: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId
                    && request.resource.data.role == resource.data.role; // Can't change own role
    }
   
    // User subcollections - full access to own data
    match /users/{userId}/{collection}/{document} {
      allow read, write: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId;
    }
  }
}

FIRESTORE SERVICE PATTERN
services/firestore.ts:

import { collection, doc, addDoc, updateDoc, deleteDoc, getDocs, query, orderBy, onSnapshot, serverTimestamp } from 'firebase/firestore';
import { db } from '../config/firebase';
export const getUserCollection = (uid: string, collectionName: string) =>
  collection(db, 'users', uid, collectionName);
export const addDocument = async (uid: string, collectionName: string, data: any) => {
  return await addDoc(getUserCollection(uid, collectionName), {
    ...data,
    createdAt: serverTimestamp(),
    updatedAt: serv
**Breakpoints (Tailwind defaults):**
- Mobile: < 640px (default styles, no prefix)
- Tablet: sm:640px and up
- Desktop: lg:1024px and up
**Layout Behavior:**

MOBILE (< 640px):
┌─────────────────────┐
│ HEADER    [☰] [👤]  │  ← Hamburger menu, compact header
├─────────────────────┤
│                     │
│    MAIN CONTENT     │  ← Full width, no sidebar visible
│    (scrollable)     │
│                     │
│    Cards stack      │
│    vertically       │
│                     │
└─────────────────────┘
TABLET/DESKTOP (≥ 1024px):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ HEADER: Logo          User Avatar | Sign Out    │
├──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┤
│   SIDEBAR    │         MAIN CONTENT             │
│   (240px)    │         (scrollable)             │
└──────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

**Mobile Navigation:**
- Sidebar hidden by default on mobile
- Hamburger icon (☰) in header toggles sidebar
- Sidebar slides in as overlay (not push)
- Clicking outside or nav item closes sidebar
- Add close (✕) button inside mobile sidebar
**Component Responsive Rules:**
| Component | Mobile | Desktop |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Sidebar | Hidden, hamburger toggle | Always visible, 240px |
| Cards | Full width, stack vertically | Grid 2-3 columns |
| Forms | Full width inputs | Max-width container |
| Buttons | Full width for primary actions | Auto width |
| Modals | Full screen or nearly full | Centered, max-w-md |
| Text | Base size 16px minimum | Can be smaller |
**Touch Targets:**
- Minimum 44px × 44px for all clickable elements on mobile
- Add padding to small icons/buttons to meet minimum
- Adequate spacing between touch targets (no accidental taps)
**Responsive Classes Pattern:**

// Card grid

<div className="grid grid-cols-1 sm:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4"> // Hide on mobile, show on desktop <div className="hidden lg:block"> // Show on mobile, hide on desktop   <div className="lg:hidden"> // Full width mobile, contained desktop <div className="w-full lg:max-w-md"> // Padding adjustments <div className="p-4 lg:p-8"> ``` ### UI/UX STANDARDS (MANDATORY) #### REQUIRED UI COMPONENTS You MUST create and use these components. They are NOT optional: 1. **Modal.tsx** - Base modal with overlay, close button, title, content slots 2. **ConfirmModal.tsx** - "Are you sure?" dialog for destructive actions 3. **Toast.tsx** - Slide-in notification (success, error, info variants) 4. **ToastContext.tsx** - Global toast state with `showToast(message, type)` function 5. **Skeleton.tsx** - Animated placeholder matching content shape 6. **EmptyState.tsx** - Illustration/icon + message + CTA button #### BANNED - DO NOT USE These are strictly forbidden. Using them fails the build: - ❌ `alert()` - Use Toast for messages - ❌ `confirm()` - Use ConfirmModal for confirmations - ❌ `prompt()` - Use a proper form Modal - ❌ `console.log` for user feedback - Use Toast - ❌ Text-only empty states - Use EmptyState component with icon and CTA - ❌ Browser default dialogs of any kind #### PAGE TYPES FOR USER DATA Any data the user creates/saves MUST follow this pattern: **List View** (e.g., Dashboard) - Shows all items as cards or rows - Clicking an item navigates to Detail View - Has "Create New" button **Detail View** (e.g., /items/:id) - Read-only display of single item - Shows all item data nicely formatted - Action buttons: Edit, Delete, Share, etc. - Delete opens ConfirmModal, then redirects to List on success **Create View** (e.g., /items/new) - Form to create new item - Save navigates to Detail View of new item - Cancel returns to List View **Edit View** (e.g., /items/:id/edit) - Form pre-filled with existing data - Save navigates back to Detail View - Cancel returns to Detail View (not List) #### NAVIGATION FLOW ``` ┌──────────┐     click item      ┌──────────────┐ │   LIST   │ ─────────────────▶  │    DETAIL    │ │   VIEW   │                     │     VIEW     │ └──────────┘                     └──────────────┘      │                                  │      │ click "New"              click "Edit"      ▼                                  │ ┌──────────┐                           │ │  CREATE  │                           │ │   VIEW   │                           ▼ └──────────┘                     ┌──────────────┐      │                           │     EDIT     │      │ save                      │     VIEW     │      ▼                           └──────────────┘ ┌──────────────┐                       │ │    DETAIL    │ ◀─────────────────────┘ │     VIEW     │         save └──────────────┘ ``` #### ANTI-PATTERNS - DO NOT DO THESE - ❌ Clicking saved item opens it in edit mode directly - ❌ Using Create form as Edit form by pre-loading data - ❌ No way to view an item without editing it - ❌ Single "smart" component that handles both view and edit - ❌ Delete with no confirmation - ❌ Success/error with no feedback to user - ❌ Empty lists with just "No items" text (needs icon + CTA) - ❌ Loading states that are just the word "Loading..." #### FEEDBACK PATTERNS **On Success:** - Show success Toast ("Item created successfully") - Navigate to appropriate view **On Error:** - Show error Toast with helpful message - Stay on current view - Keep form data intact **On Delete:** 1. User clicks delete 2. ConfirmModal appears: "Delete this item? This cannot be undone." 3. User confirms 4. Show loading state on button 5. On success: Toast + redirect to List 6. On error: Toast + close modal **On Loading:** - Lists: Show Skeleton cards (not spinner) - Detail View: Show Skeleton matching content layout - Buttons during action: Show spinner inside button, disable button ### POLISH & UX DETAILS (MANDATORY) These details separate amateur apps from professional ones. Implement ALL of them. #### DATE & TIME FORMATTING Never show raw timestamps. Format dates for humans: ```typescript // Create a utils/formatDate.ts helper function formatRelativeTime(date: Date): string {   const now = new Date();   const diffInSeconds = Math.floor((now.getTime() - date.getTime()) / 1000);     if (diffInSeconds < 60) return 'Just now';   if (diffInSeconds < 3600) return `${Math.floor(diffInSeconds / 60)}m ago`;   if (diffInSeconds < 86400) return `${Math.floor(diffInSeconds / 3600)}h ago`;   if (diffInSeconds < 172800) return 'Yesterday';   if (diffInSeconds < 604800) return `${Math.floor(diffInSeconds / 86400)}d ago`;   return date.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric' }); } ``` Display examples: - "Just now", "5m ago", "2h ago" (within 24 hours) - "Yesterday" (24-48 hours) - "3d ago" (within a week) - "Jan 15" (older than a week) - "Jan 15, 2024" (different year) #### TEXT TRUNCATION Long text MUST be truncated to prevent layout breaking: ```typescript // Sidebar items: max 30 characters <span className="truncate max-w-[200px]">{title}</span> // Card descriptions: max 2 lines <p className="line-clamp-2">{description}</p> // Table cells: single line truncate <td className="truncate max-w-[150px]">{content}</td> ``` Tailwind classes: - `truncate` - Single line with ellipsis - `line-clamp-2` - Multi-line (2 lines) with ellipsis - Always set `max-w-` when using truncate #### BACK NAVIGATION Every detail/edit page MUST have back navigation: ```tsx // At top of detail pages <button   onClick={() => navigate(-1)}   className="flex items-center gap-2 text-text-secondary hover:text-text-primary mb-6" >   <ArrowLeftIcon className="w-4 h-4" />   Back </button> // Or explicit route <Link to="/dashboard" className="...">   ← Back to Dashboard </Link> ``` #### TRANSITIONS & ANIMATIONS Add subtle animations for polish: ```css /* Add to index.css or as Tailwind config */ /* Modal backdrop fade */ .modal-backdrop {   @apply transition-opacity duration-200; } /* Modal content scale */ .modal-content {   @apply transition-all duration-200 ease-out; } /* Toast slide in */ .toast {   @apply transition-transform duration-300 ease-out; } /* Card hover lift */ .card-hover {   @apply transition-all duration-200 hover:shadow-md hover:-translate-y-0.5; } /* Button press */ .btn {   @apply transition-all duration-150 active:scale-[0.98]; } /* Page transitions */ .page-enter {   @apply opacity-0; } .page-enter-active {   @apply opacity-100 transition-opacity duration-200; } ``` Required animations: - Modals: Fade in backdrop, scale up content - Toasts: Slide in from top-right - Cards: Subtle lift on hover - Buttons: Slight scale on press - Sidebar: Slide in on mobile #### ACCESSIBILITY BASICS Implement these accessibility features: **Focus States:** ```tsx // All interactive elements need visible focus className="focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-brand focus:ring-offset-2" ``` **Keyboard Navigation:** ```tsx // Modals must handle Escape key useEffect(() => {   const handleEsc = (e: KeyboardEvent) => {     if (e.key === 'Escape') onClose();   };   document.addEventListener('keydown', handleEsc);   return () => document.removeEventListener('keydown', handleEsc); }, [onClose]); // Focus trap in modals - focus first element, trap Tab key ``` **Icon Buttons:** ```tsx // Icon-only buttons need aria-label <button aria-label="Close modal" onClick={onClose}>   <XIcon className="w-5 h-5" /> </button> <button aria-label="Delete item" onClick={onDelete}>   <TrashIcon className="w-5 h-5" /> </button> ``` **Screen Reader Text:** ```tsx // Loading states <span className="sr-only">Loading...</span> // Status updates <div role="status" aria-live="polite">{message}</div> ``` #### PAGINATION / LIST LIMITS Lists MUST handle large amounts of data: **Option 1: Pagination** ```tsx // Show 10-20 items per page const ITEMS_PER_PAGE = 10; const [page, setPage] = useState(1); // Pagination controls at bottom <div className="flex justify-center gap-2 mt-6">   <Button disabled={page === 1} onClick={() => setPage(p => p - 1)}>Previous</Button>   <span className="py-2 px-4">Page {page} of {totalPages}</span>   <Button disabled={page === totalPages} onClick={() => setPage(p => p + 1)}>Next</Button> </div> ``` **Option 2: Load More** ```tsx // Show initial batch, then "Load More" button const [limit, setLimit] = useState(10); <Button onClick={() => setLimit(l => l + 10)}>   Load More ({remaining} remaining) </Button> ``` **Option 3: Infinite Scroll** ```tsx // Load more when scrolled near bottom (use Intersection Observer) ``` Choose ONE approach and implement it consistently. #### FORM FIELD STATES Forms need proper visual states: ```tsx // Input wrapper with all states <div className="space-y-1">   <label className="text-sm font-medium text-text-primary">     Email   </label>   <input     className={`       w-full px-4 py-3 rounded-lg border transition-colors       ${error         ? 'border-red-500 bg-red-50 focus:ring-red-500'         : 'border-border-subtle bg-surface-muted focus:ring-brand'       }       ${disabled ? 'opacity-50 cursor-not-allowed' : ''}       focus:outline-none focus:ring-2     `}     disabled={isSubmitting}   />   {error && <p className="text-sm text-red-600">{error}</p>}   {helperText && !error && <p className="text-sm text-text-tertiary">{helperText}</p>} </div> ``` States to handle: - Default (empty) - Focused (ring) - Filled (value present) - Error (red border, error message) - Disabled (greyed out, during submission) - Helper text (optional hints) #### UNSAVED CHANGES WARNING Warn users before losing form data: ```tsx // Hook to track unsaved changes function useUnsavedChanges(hasChanges: boolean) {   useEffect(() => {     const handleBeforeUnload = (e: BeforeUnloadEvent) => {       if (hasChanges) {         e.preventDefault();         e.returnValue = '';       }     };         window.addEventListener('beforeunload', handleBeforeUnload);     return () => window.removeEventListener('beforeunload', handleBeforeUnload);   }, [hasChanges]); } // Usage in form const [isDirty, setIsDirty] = useState(false); useUnsavedChanges(isDirty); // Also intercept in-app navigation with a ConfirmModal ``` #### 404 / NOT FOUND HANDLING Handle invalid routes and missing data: **Route-level 404:** ```tsx // In App.tsx routes <Route path="*" element={<NotFoundPage />} /> ``` **NotFoundPage component:** ```tsx function NotFoundPage() {   return (     <div className="flex flex-col items-center justify-center min-h-[60vh] text-center">       <h1 className="text-6xl font-bold text-text-tertiary">404</h1>       <p className="text-xl text-text-secondary mt-4">Page not found</p>       <Link to="/dashboard" className="mt-6 text-brand hover:underline">         ← Back to Dashboard       </Link>     </div>   ); } ``` **Data-level not found:** ```tsx // In detail pages when item doesn't exist if (!loading && !item) {   return (     <EmptyState       icon={<SearchIcon />}       title="Item not found"       description="This item may have been deleted or doesn't exist."       action={{ label: "Back to Dashboard", href: "/dashboard" }}     />   ); } ``` #### HOVER STATES All interactive elements need hover feedback: ```tsx // Cards - lift effect className="hover:shadow-md hover:-translate-y-0.5 transition-all cursor-pointer" // Buttons - darken className="hover:bg-brand-dark transition-colors" // Links - underline or color change className="hover:text-brand hover:underline" // Icon buttons - background className="hover:bg-surface-muted rounded-lg p-2 transition-colors" // Table rows - highlight className="hover:bg-surface-muted transition-colors" // Sidebar items - background className="hover:bg-surface-muted rounded-lg transition-colors" ``` Every clickable element should visually respond to hover. ### ADDITIONAL STANDARDS (MANDATORY) #### ICONS - USE LUCIDE REACT Use Lucide React for all icons. Consistent style, tree-shakeable. **Add to importmap:** ```json "lucide-react": "https://esm.sh/lucide-react" ``` **Usage:** ```tsx import { Home, Settings, Trash2, Copy, Check, X, Menu, ArrowLeft, Search, Plus, Loader2 } from 'lucide-react'; // Standard size <Home className="w-5 h-5" /> // In buttons <button><Plus className="w-4 h-4 mr-2" /> New Item</button> // Spinning loader <Loader2 className="w-5 h-5 animate-spin" /> ``` Common icons needed: - Navigation: `Home`, `Settings`, `User`, `LogOut`, `Menu`, `X`, `ArrowLeft` - Actions: `Plus`, `Trash2`, `Edit`, `Copy`, `Check`, `Download`, `Share` - Status: `Loader2` (spinner), `AlertCircle`, `CheckCircle`, `XCircle` - UI: `Search`, `ChevronDown`, `ChevronRight`, `MoreVertical` #### ERROR BOUNDARY Wrap app in error boundary to prevent white screen of death: ```tsx // components/ErrorBoundary.tsx import { Component, ReactNode } from 'react'; interface Props {   children: ReactNode; } interface State {   hasError: boolean; } class ErrorBoundary extends Component<Props, State> {   constructor(props: Props) {     super(props);     this.state = { hasError: false };   }   static getDerivedStateFromError(): State {     return { hasError: true };   }   render() {     if (this.state.hasError) {       return (         <div className="min-h-screen flex items-center justify-center bg-surface-canvas">           <div className="text-center p-8">             <h1 className="text-2xl font-semibold text-text-primary mb-2">Something went wrong</h1>             <p className="text-text-secondary mb-4">Please refresh the page to try again.</p>             <button               onClick={() => window.location.reload()}               className="bg-brand hover:bg-brand-dark text-text-primary px-6 py-3 rounded-lg"             >               Refresh Page             </button>           </div>         </div>       );     }     return this.props.children;   } } export default ErrorBoundary; // Wrap in App.tsx <ErrorBoundary>   <AuthProvider>     ...   </AuthProvider> </ErrorBoundary> ``` #### LOADING BUTTON PATTERN Buttons during async actions must show loading state: ```tsx // components/ui/Button.tsx interface ButtonProps {   loading?: boolean;   disabled?: boolean;   children: React.ReactNode;   onClick?: () => void;   type?: 'button' | 'submit';   variant?: 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'danger'; } function Button({ loading, disabled, children, onClick, type = 'button', variant = 'primary' }: ButtonProps) {   return (     <button       type={type}       onClick={onClick}       disabled={loading || disabled}       className={`         flex items-center justify-center gap-2 px-6 py-3 rounded-lg font-medium         transition-all duration-150 active:scale-[0.98]         disabled:opacity-50 disabled:cursor-not-allowed         ${variant === 'primary' ? 'bg-brand hover:bg-brand-dark text-text-primary' : ''}         ${variant === 'secondary' ? 'bg-surface-muted hover:bg-border-subtle text-text-primary' : ''}         ${variant === 'danger' ? 'bg-red-600 hover:bg-red-700 text-white' : ''}       `}     >       {loading && <Loader2 className="w-4 h-4 animate-spin" />}       {loading ? 'Loading...' : children}     </button>   ); } // Usage <Button loading={isSubmitting} onClick={handleSubmit}>   Save Changes </Button> ``` #### USER AVATAR WITH FALLBACK Google profile images can fail. Always have fallback: ```tsx // components/ui/Avatar.tsx interface AvatarProps {   src?: string | null;   name?: string | null;   size?: 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg'; } function Avatar({ src, name, size = 'md' }: AvatarProps) {   const sizes = {     sm: 'w-8 h-8 text-xs',     md: 'w-10 h-10 text-sm',     lg: 'w-12 h-12 text-base'   };   const initials = name     ?.split(' ')     .map(n => n[0])     .join('')     .toUpperCase()     .slice(0, 2) || '?';   if (src) {     return (       <img         src={src}         alt={name || 'User'}         className={`${sizes[size]} rounded-full object-cover`}         onError={(e) => {           // Hide broken image, show fallback           e.currentTarget.style.display = 'none';           e.currentTarget.nextElementSibling?.classList.remove('hidden');         }}       />     );   }   return (     <div className={`${sizes[size]} rounded-full bg-brand flex items-center justify-center font-medium text-text-primary`}>       {initials}     </div>   ); } ``` #### FAVICON Every app needs a favicon. Add to index.html: ```html <head>   ...   <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="/favicon.svg" /> </head> ``` Create simple SVG favicon matching brand: ```svg <!-- public/favicon.svg --> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 32 32">   <rect width="32" height="32" rx="8" fill="#DFFF5E"/>   <text x="16" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-family="system-ui" font-weight="bold" font-size="18" fill="#111827">A</text> </svg> ``` Replace "A" with app's first letter and fill color with brand color. #### DYNAMIC PAGE TITLES Update document title on each page: ```tsx // hooks/usePageTitle.ts import { useEffect } from 'react'; function usePageTitle(title: string) {   useEffect(() => {     const appName = 'AppName'; // Replace with your app name     document.title = title ? `${title} - ${appName}` : appName;   }, [title]); } // Usage in pages function Dashboard() {   usePageTitle('Dashboard');   return ...; } function ItemDetailPage() {   usePageTitle(item?.name || 'Loading...');   return ...; } ``` #### AUTOFOCUS ON FORMS First input should be focused when page/modal loads: ```tsx // Using ref const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null); useEffect(() => {   inputRef.current?.focus(); }, []); <input ref={inputRef} ... /> // Or use autoFocus attribute (simpler) <input autoFocus ... /> // For modals - focus first input when modal opens useEffect(() => {   if (isOpen) {     const firstInput = modalRef.current?.querySelector('input, textarea');     firstInput?.focus();   } }, [isOpen]); ``` #### PLURALIZATION HELPER Never show "1 items" - always handle plurals: ```tsx // utils/pluralize.ts function pluralize(count: number, singular: string, plural?: string): string {   const pluralForm = plural || `${singular}s`;   return count === 1 ? `${count} ${singular}` : `${count} ${pluralForm}`; } // Usage pluralize(1, 'item')        // "1 item" pluralize(5, 'item')        // "5 items" pluralize(1, 'generation')  // "1 generation" pluralize(3, 'generation')  // "3 generations" pluralize(1, 'entry', 'entries') // "1 entry" pluralize(2, 'entry', 'entries') // "2 entries" ``` #### SEARCH / FILTER FOR LISTS Any list that can grow needs search/filter: ```tsx // In Dashboard or any list view const [searchQuery, setSearchQuery] = useState(''); const filteredItems = items.filter(item =>   item.title.toLowerCase().includes(searchQuery.toLowerCase()) ||   item.description?.toLowerCase().includes(searchQuery.toLowerCase()) ); // Search input at top of list <div className="relative mb-6">   <Search className="absolute left-3 top-1/2 -translate-y-1/2 w-5 h-5 text-text-tertiary" />   <input     type="text"     placeholder="Search..."     value={searchQuery}     onChange={(e) => setSearchQuery(e.target.value)}     className="w-full pl-10 pr-4 py-3 bg-surface-muted rounded-lg outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-brand"   /> </div> // Show filtered results {filteredItems.length === 0 && searchQuery && (   <p className="text-center text-text-secondary py-8">     No results for "{searchQuery}"   </p> )} ``` #### RETRY ON ERROR Error states should be actionable, not dead ends: ```tsx // Instead of just showing error message <div className="text-center py-8">   <AlertCircle className="w-12 h-12 text-red-500 mx-auto mb-4" />   <p className="text-text-secondary mb-4">{error}</p>   <Button onClick={retry} variant="secondary">     Try Again   </Button> </div> // Toast with retry showToast({   type: 'error',   message: 'Failed to save',   action: { label: 'Retry', onClick: handleSave } }); ``` #### NETWORK / OFFLINE HANDLING Handle Firebase/network errors gracefully: ```tsx // In firestore service, wrap calls export async function addDocument(uid: string, collection: string, data: any) {   try {     return await addDoc(...);   } catch (error: any) {     if (error.code === 'unavailable') {       throw new Error('You appear to be offline. Please check your connection.');     }     if (error.code === 'permission-denied') {       throw new Error('You don\'t have permission to do this.');     }     throw new Error('Something went wrong. Please try again.');   } } // Check online status const [isOnline, setIsOnline] = useState(navigator.onLine); useEffect(() => {   const handleOnline = () => setIsOnline(true);   const handleOffline = () => setIsOnline(false);     window.addEventListener('online', handleOnline);   window.addEventListener('offline', handleOffline);     return () => {     window.removeEventListener('online', handleOnline);     window.removeEventListener('offline', handleOffline);   }; }, []); // Show banner when offline {!isOnline && (   <div className="bg-yellow-100 text-yellow-800 px-4 py-2 text-center text-sm">     You're offline. Some features may not work.   </div> )} ``` #### SESSION EXPIRY HANDLING Firebase tokens expire. Handle gracefully: ```tsx // In AuthContext - listen for auth errors import { onAuthStateChanged } from 'firebase/auth'; useEffect(() => {   const unsubscribe = onAuthStateChanged(auth, (user) => {     if (user) {       setUser(user);     } else {       // User signed out or session expired       setUser(null);       // If they were on a protected route, redirect       if (window.location.pathname !== '/' && window.location.pathname !== '/login') {         window.location.href = '/login';       }     }     setLoading(false);   });   return unsubscribe; }, []); // Wrap Firestore calls to catch auth errors try {   await addDocument(...); } catch (error: any) {   if (error.code === 'unauthenticated' || error.code === 'permission-denied') {     // Session likely expired     showToast({ type: 'error', message: 'Session expired. Please sign in again.' });     await logout();   } } ``` #### CONSOLE CLEAN Production apps must have zero console errors/warnings: **Rules:** - No `console.log` statements (use proper error handling) - No React key warnings (always use unique keys in lists) - No missing dependency warnings (fix useEffect deps) - No unused variable warnings - No TypeScript errors **Before deploying:** 1. Open browser DevTools Console 2. Navigate through entire app 3. Fix any red errors or yellow warnings 4. Repeat until console is clean ### CRITICAL RULES **Technical:** 1. Firebase config in firebase.ts with placeholder values 2. NO Firestore calls in components - use firestore service only 3. NO unprotected routes for authenticated features 4. NO inline styles - Tailwind only 5. NO `any` types - define TypeScript interfaces 6. ALL Firestore writes include createdAt/updatedAt timestamps 7. ALL user data in subcollections under users/{uid}/ 8. Wrap app in ErrorBoundary component **UI/UX:** 9. NO alert(), confirm(), prompt() - use Modal/ConfirmModal/Toast 10. ALL destructive actions require ConfirmModal 11. ALL async operations show loading state (Skeleton for lists, spinner in buttons) 12. ALL empty lists use EmptyState component with icon and CTA 13. ALL success/error actions show Toast feedback 14. ALL saved items have Detail View (read-only) separate from Edit View 15. ALL forms validate before submission 16. ALL buttons show loading state during async actions 17. ALL avatars have fallback for failed images 18. ALL pages set document title via usePageTitle hook 19. ALL forms autofocus first input 20. ALL lists have search/filter when > 5 items expected 21. ALL error states have retry action 22. ALL dates formatted as relative time (not raw timestamps) 23. ALL long text truncated with ellipsis 24. ALL detail pages have back navigation 25. Use Lucide React for all icons 26. Zero console errors in production --- ## BUILD THIS APP NOW Using all the information above: 1. Create the complete file structure 2. Implement all components following the exact patterns 3. Build the features described in Section 2 4. Apply the styling from Section 3 using the design system 5. Ensure all routes, auth flows, and Firestore operations work 6. Make it production-ready with proper error handling Generate the complete, working application. --- ## POST-GENERATION: Add Firebase Config After generation, open `src/config/firebase.ts` and replace the placeholder values with your actual Firebase config from the Firebase Console. MARTINS APP IDEA PROMPT # IDEA CODE: App Idea Generator ## How To Use 1. Copy the prompt below 2. Paste it into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini 3. Describe your app idea (can be rough/vague - that's fine!) 4. Copy the structured output into **Sections 1 & 2** of the main Idea Code template --- # THE PROMPT (Copy Everything Below This Line) --- **Role:** You are a product strategist and startup advisor who helps people turn vague app ideas into clear, buildable MVPs. **Objective:** I'm going to describe an app idea. It might be rough, incomplete, or just a general concept. Your job is to help me clarify it and output a structured specification I can use to build it. **Your Process:** 1. If my idea is unclear, ask me 2-3 quick clarifying questions first 2. Once you understand, output the structured format below 3. Keep it MVP-focused - only essential features, nothing fancy **Output Format (Follow Exactly):** ``` ## SECTION 1: APP IDENTITY **App Name:** [Suggest a short, memorable name] **One-Line Description:** [What it does in one sentence - be specific] **Target User:** [Who is this for? Be specific about their situation] **Core Problem It Solves:** [What pain point does this eliminate?] --- ## SECTION 2: FEATURES **Core Features (3-5 max):** 1. [Feature 1 - specific and actionable] 2. [Feature 2] 3. [Feature 3] 4. [Feature 4 - if needed] 5.
