[Feature 5] (if needed) ## Data Entities (for Module 03) [Entity definitions from Section 4] ## Pages (for Module 05) [Page map from Section 5] ## Style Direction (for Module 07) - Visual feel: [from Section 1 context] - Personality: [professional / casual / playful / premium] - Color preference: [if any] - Inspiration: [apps they admire, if any] ## Out of Scope for v1 - [Not building X] - [Not building Y] - [Not building Z] ``` **This output becomes the input for Module 01 (Scaffold).** The user copies the features into Section 2, the style direction into Section 3, and starts building. --- ## --- END PROMPT --- ================================================================================ 13-testing-protocol.md - MODULE 13: TESTING PROTOCOL ================================================================================ # MODULE 13: TESTING PROTOCOL ## Verify Everything Works Before You Ship **What this does:** A comprehensive testing checklist and automated test setup for your app. This isn't unit-test-every-function testing (overkill for vibe coding). This is "make sure everything actually works and doesn't break when you ship it" testing. **When to use:** - Before showing your app to anyone - Before deploying to production - After making significant changes - When something feels "off" but you can't pinpoint it **Two levels:** 1. **Manual Testing Checklist** — Walk through the app like a user (fast, no code) 2. **Automated Smoke Tests** — Playwright tests that catch regressions (optional, for apps you maintain long-term) --- ## --- START PROMPT --- ## TASK: Test This Application Thoroughly Run through the complete testing protocol. Report every issue found, categorized by severity. --- ## SECTION 1: PRE-FLIGHT CHECKS Before testing functionality, verify the basics: ### Build Check ```bash npm run build ``` - [ ] Zero TypeScript errors - [ ] Zero build warnings - [ ] Build completes successfully ### Lint Check (if configured) ```bash npm run lint ``` - [ ] Zero lint errors - [ ] Zero lint warnings (or only pre-existing ones) ### Dev Server ```bash npm run dev ``` - [ ] Starts without errors - [ ] No warnings in terminal - [ ] Accessible at localhost URL ### Environment - [ ] `.env.local` exists with Supabase credentials - [ ] Supabase project is accessible (check Dashboard) - [ ] At least one test user account exists --- ## SECTION 2: MANUAL TESTING CHECKLIST Open the app in browser. Open DevTools Console. Clear it. Then test each flow: ### Authentication Flow - [ ] Landing page loads without console errors - [ ] "Sign In" / "Get Started" button is visible and clickable - [ ] Google OAuth popup/redirect works - [ ] After sign-in, redirected to Dashboard - [ ] User name/avatar appears correctly - [ ] Profile page shows correct info - [ ] Sign out works — returns to landing - [ ] After sign out, `/dashboard` redirects to `/login` - [ ] Refresh the page while signed in — session persists - [ ] Console: zero errors during entire auth flow ### Navigation Flow - [ ] Every sidebar link works - [ ] Active page highlighted in sidebar - [ ] Back buttons work on every detail/edit page - [ ] Browser back button works correctly - [ ] Invalid URL shows 404 page - [ ] 404 page has link back to home ### CRUD Flow (for EACH entity) **Create:** - [ ] "New" button visible on list page - [ ] Form loads with first input focused - [ ] Required fields show validation errors when empty - [ ] Submit shows loading state on button - [ ] Success: toast appears, redirected to detail page - [ ] New item appears in list (navigate back) **Read (List):** - [ ] Items display correctly in cards/list - [ ] Dates show relative time (not raw timestamps) - [ ] Long titles truncated properly - [ ] Long descriptions truncated (line-clamp) - [ ] Search filters results correctly - [ ] Empty search shows "no results" message - [ ] Clear search shows all items - [ ] Pagination works (if > 12 items) **Read (Detail):** - [ ] All fields display correctly - [ ] Null/empty fields show "—" not "null" - [ ] Dates formatted as full date - [ ] Edit button navigates to edit form - [ ] Delete button opens ConfirmModal **Update:** - [ ] Edit form pre-filled with current data - [ ] Changes are saved correctly - [ ] Success: toast + redirect to detail with updated data - [ ] Cancel returns to detail (not list) without saving **Delete:** - [ ] ConfirmModal appears with correct message - [ ] Cancel closes modal without deleting - [ ] Confirm shows loading state - [ ] Success: toast + redirect to list, item gone - [ ] Cannot delete an item that doesn't exist (handle gracefully) ### UI Component Testing - [ ] Toast: success, error, info types all display correctly - [ ] Toast: auto-dismisses after ~4 seconds - [ ] Toast: dismiss button works - [ ] Modal: Escape key closes - [ ] Modal: clicking backdrop closes (if enabled) - [ ] Modal: focus stays inside modal (tab doesn't escape) - [ ] Buttons: loading state works (spinner + disabled) - [ ] Skeleton: shows during loading, replaced by real content - [ ] EmptyState: shows when lists are empty, with working CTA ### Error Handling - [ ] Turn off WiFi → "You're offline" banner appears - [ ] Turn WiFi back on → banner disappears - [ ] Failed data fetch shows error with "Try Again" button - [ ] "Try Again" actually retries - [ ] Form submission with network error shows error toast, keeps form data ### Responsive Design Test at each breakpoint (resize browser window): **Mobile (375px):** - [ ] No horizontal scroll on any page - [ ] Sidebar hidden, hamburger menu works - [ ] Cards stack vertically - [ ] Buttons full-width where appropriate - [ ] Text readable (16px minimum body text) - [ ] Touch targets at least 44x44px - [ ] Forms usable — inputs not cut off **Tablet (768px):** - [ ] Layout adapts (2-column grids) - [ ] Sidebar may or may not show (either is fine) **Desktop (1280px):** - [ ] Sidebar visible - [ ] Multi-column card grids - [ ] Comfortable spacing ### Dark Mode - [ ] Toggle works - [ ] Every page looks correct in dark mode - [ ] No white/light backgrounds leaking through - [ ] Text is readable against dark backgrounds - [ ] Brand color still looks good on dark - [ ] Cards, modals, inputs all have dark variants - [ ] Preference saved — refresh keeps the mode ### Console Audit - [ ] Navigate through EVERY page - [ ] Perform EVERY action - [ ] Zero `console.log` output - [ ] Zero React warnings - [ ] Zero TypeScript runtime errors - [ ] Zero 404 network requests - [ ] Zero failed API calls (other than intentional error testing) --- ## SECTION 3: SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION For every issue found, classify it: | Severity | Definition | Example | |----------|-----------|---------| | **P0 — Blocker** | App is broken, can't do the core thing | Can't sign in, data doesn't save, white screen | | **P1 — Critical** | Major feature broken | Delete doesn't work, search crashes, mobile unusable | | **P2 — Major** | Feature works but poorly | Slow load, missing loading state, bad error message | | **P3 — Minor** | Cosmetic or edge case | Alignment off, dark mode color wrong, rare edge case | | **P4 — Nit** | Polish | Animation missing, hover state missing, date format wrong | ### Issue Report Format For each issue: ``` [P1] Title of the issue   Page: /dashboard   Steps: 1. Click X  2. See Y   Expected: Z   Actual: W   Console error: (if any) ``` --- ## SECTION 4: AUTOMATED SMOKE TESTS (OPTIONAL) For apps you'll maintain long-term, add Playwright tests that catch regressions. ### Setup ```bash npm install -D @playwright/test npx playwright install chromium ``` **playwright.config.ts:** ```typescript import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test' export default defineConfig({   testDir: './tests',   use: {     baseURL: 'http://localhost:5173',     screenshot: 'only-on-failure',   },   webServer: {     command: 'npm run dev',     port: 5173,     reuseExistingServer: true,   }, }) ``` ### Core Smoke Tests **tests/smoke.spec.ts:** ```typescript import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test' test.describe('Smoke Tests', () => {   test('landing page loads', async ({ page }) => {     await page.goto('/')     // Check that the page has content (not a white screen)     await expect(page.locator('body')).not.toBeEmpty()     // Check for console errors     const errors: string[] = []     page.on('console', msg => {       if (msg.type() === 'error') errors.push(msg.text())     })     await page.waitForTimeout(2000)     expect(errors).toHaveLength(0)   })   test('login page loads', async ({ page }) => {     await page.goto('/login')     await expect(page.locator('body')).not.toBeEmpty()   })   test('404 page shows for invalid routes', async ({ page }) => {     await page.goto('/this-page-does-not-exist')     await expect(page.locator('text=404')).toBeVisible()   })   test('unauthenticated user redirected from protected routes', async ({ page }) => {     await page.goto('/dashboard')     // Should redirect to login     await expect(page).toHaveURL(/login/)   })   test('no console errors on key pages', async ({ page }) => {     const errors: string[] = []     page.on('console', msg => {       if (msg.type() === 'error') errors.push(msg.text())     })     const pages = ['/', '/login']     for (const url of pages) {       await page.goto(url)       await page.waitForTimeout(1000)     }     expect(errors).toHaveLength(0)   })   test('responsive: no horizontal scroll on mobile', async ({ page }) => {     await page.setViewportSize({ width: 375, height: 812 })     await page.goto('/')     const body = page.locator('body')     const scrollWidth = await body.evaluate(el => el.scrollWidth)     const clientWidth = await body.evaluate(el => el.clientWidth)     expect(scrollWidth).toBeLessThanOrEqual(clientWidth + 1) // 1px tolerance   }) }) ``` ### Run Tests ```bash npx playwright test npx playwright test --ui  # with visual UI ``` **Add to package.json scripts:** ```json {   "scripts": {     "test:e2e": "playwright test",     "test:e2e:ui": "playwright test --ui"   } } ``` --- ## SECTION 5: PRE-DEPLOY CHECKLIST Final checks before deploying to production: - [ ] `npm run build` passes - [ ] All P0 and P1 issues fixed - [ ] Smoke tests pass (if set up) - [ ] Environment variables documented in `.env.example` - [ ] Production Supabase URL (not dev) ready for deploy config - [ ] Redirect URL added in Supabase for production domain - [ ] SPA routing configured:   - **Vercel:** `vercel.json` with `"rewrites": [{"source": "/(.*)", "destination": "/index.html"}]`   - **Netlify:** `public/_redirects` with `/* /index.html 200` - [ ] Favicon set - [ ] Page title set (not "Vite + React + TS") - [ ] OpenGraph meta tags (optional but nice for sharing) - [ ] One final manual walkthrough of the complete app --- ## SECTION 6: COMMIT ```bash git add -A git commit -m "test: add testing protocol and Playwright smoke tests" ``` --- ## POST-TESTING: FIX ISSUES Use Module 08 (Bug Fix) for each issue found, starting with P0 blockers and working down. --- ## --- END PROMPT --- Okay, so below is the agent that I was telling One important correction from its findings: pulsing and seams have a more specific definition than what I initially captured. Pulsing = PULSE_CHECK — the lightest verification tier (lint + type check + run tests, 2-5 min, after every feature) Seams = SEAM_CHECK — medium verification at integration points where two systems meet (data↔API, frontend↔backend, auth↔routes). Pulse + test the changed thing + test one dependency. 10-20 min. FULL_VERIFY = the heavy tier — complete protocol after phase-end features. 30-60 min. The PRD I wrote captures the concept correctly but uses slightly different terminology. The protocol/seam definitions map to feature tags: [UI] → PULSE_CHECK, [DATA][API][WIRE][AUTH] → SEAM_CHECK, [PHASE-END] → FULL_VERIFY. The PRD is solid and complete. Here's how it works architecturally: The theme/style picker IS the free tier product. It's the front door. User signs up, gets a clean UI tool: Pick from 12 styles Customize colors/fonts Preview on 4 page types (Landing, Dashboard, Settings, Feed) Export CSS/Tailwind config Done. Free or $5/mo. The full PRDM pipeline already exists behind it, just gated. The code is all there — it's just display: none until they upgrade. The tier gating middleware we're already building (F04) handles this. Same database, same app, same deployment. The user flow becomes: Free/Cheap: "I need a UI theme"   → Style Picker → Preview → Export CSS   → "Want a full PRD for this app?" [Upgrade prompt]  Starter ($29): "Actually yeah, let me spec this out"     → Style carries forward into PRD   → Rant/Questionnaire → Full PRD pipeline   → "Want to actually build it?" [Upgrade prompt]  Pro ($79): "Build it"   → PRD + Boilerplate + AutoForge bridge   → Theme already applied to boilerplate  Why this works without a separate rebuild: The style picker is just a few components (StylePreview, ColorCustomizer, ThemeExporter) It doesn't depend on any pipeline code It's already a natural "Step 1" in the PRD flow (choose your look before describing your app) We just make it accessible without logging into the full dashboard What I'd do in the build: Put the style picker at /themes as a public route (no auth required for browsing, auth required for export). The rest of the app lives behind /dashboard which requires auth + tier check. One codebase, one deploy, two experiences. The pitch writes itself: "Free UI theme generator" → lands on Product Hunt → users try it → "Oh wait, this thing can also write my entire PRD and build my app?" → upgrade. Want me to update the PRD to reflect this funnel structure? It's a small change — just reorganizing F05 (Intake Dock) to put the style picker as the public entry point, and adding a /themes public route. but he main system will be promoted more because its more serched for ... so they can both be pitched as an entry is the popint so it could be interchangable as what leads maybe or I can just build the ui part seperate which soulnd like a good idea since its so simple to make ..what do you think Build it separate. Here's why: The theme picker is a weekend project. It's literally: Static Next.js site (or even just Tailwind + vanilla) 12 style cards → preview → export CSS No auth, no database, no AI, no Supabase Could be free forever or $5 one-time Its own domain: stylepicker.dev or whatever The PRDM Maker is the real product. It's what people search for. "AI PRD generator", "app spec builder", "product requirements tool" — that's the high-intent traffic. That's your main funnel. Two separate entry points, cross-promoting each other: stylepicker.dev (simple, free/cheap)        prdmmaker.com (main product, SaaS)   "Pick your UI theme"                        "Idea to PRD in minutes"   └─ Banner: "Build the whole app? →"         └─ Step 1 happens to include       links to prdmmaker.com                      a theme picker built-in  Why separate wins over modular here: Different SEO keywords, different audiences Two Product Hunt launches instead of one Theme picker stays lean forever (no PRDM bloat) Main product doesn't get diluted as "a theme tool" If the theme picker blows up, it doesn't affect your main app's infrastructure The only connector you need: An "Import Theme" button on the PRDM Maker that accepts a JSON config exported from the style picker. What Your Mentor Built (and Why It's Brilliant) This prompt is essentially 15 years of dev team knowledge compressed into a single document. It's a complete "build spec" that eliminates the #1 problem in vibe coding: the AI making random architectural decisions. Every pattern is locked down — file structure, auth flow, data layer, UI components, error handling, even how dates should display. That's not a prompt, that's a codified engineering culture. The three-prompt system (Idea → Design → Build) mirrors what real dev teams do: product spec → design spec → engineering spec. He just automated the handoff. The Big Question: Gemini/Firestore-Specific vs. Agnostic? Here's the breakdown: ~60% is completely agnostic (pure gold you can steal directly): The entire UI/UX standards section (modals, toasts, skeletons, empty states) The navigation flow patterns (List → Detail → Create → Edit) The responsive design rules The anti-patterns list (no alert(), no console.log for user feedback) The polish section (date formatting, truncation, pagination, hover states) The design system (typography, spacing, cards, buttons) The file structure concept (separation of concerns) Error boundaries, loading states, unsaved changes warnings Accessibility patterns The entire Section 1 (App Identity) and Section 2 (Features) ~25% is Firestore/Firebase-specific (needs Supabase translation): config/firebase.ts → becomes config/supabase.ts AuthContext.tsx with Google Sign-In via Firebase → Supabase Auth (which also supports Google OAuth, but the API is different) services/firestore.ts with subcollections → Supabase tables with Row Level Security (RLS) Security rules (Firestore rules → Supabase RLS policies) The users/{uid}/{collection} data model → Supabase tables with user_id foreign keys onSnapshot realtime listeners → Supabase Realtime subscriptions deleteUserAccount pattern → Supabase cascade deletes or edge function Session expiry handling (Firebase token refresh → Supabase session refresh) ~15% is Gemini/Google AI Studio-specific (needs Claude Code translation): The importmap approach (esm.sh CDN imports) — this is a Google AI Studio "Build" mode thing where everything runs in-browser with no build step. Claude Code would generate a proper Vite/Next.js project @google/genai import — becomes Anthropic SDK or just server-side Claude calls The "paste into Google AI Studio Build mode" workflow — becomes "run in Claude Code terminal" The "DO NOT MODIFY importmap" lockdown — becomes proper package.json dependency management What Claude Code Would Look Like Instead Here's the key difference: Google AI Studio "Build" mode generates a single-shot app in the browser. Claude Code builds iteratively in your terminal with real files. This means: No importmap hack needed. Claude Code would npm create vite@latest, install real dependencies, and you'd have a proper build pipeline. That entire index.html section with CDN imports goes away. The prompt becomes a CLAUDE.md file (or a set of them). Instead of pasting a mega-prompt into AI Studio, you'd have Claude Code read project instructions from a file. This is actually better because it persists across sessions. Iterative instead of one-shot. His prompt tries to get everything right in one generation. With Claude Code, you'd break it into phases: scaffold → auth → data layer → UI components → features → polish. Each phase can be verified before moving on. Real testing. His prompt can't test anything — it's a prayer that the generated code works. Claude Code can run npm run dev, check for errors, fix them, run lints, etc. What Supabase Equivalents Look Like Firestore Concept Supabase Equivalent initializeApp(config) createClient(url, anonKey) onAuthStateChanged supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange signInWithPopup(googleProvider) supabase.auth.signInWithOAuth({ provider: 'google' }) signOut(auth) supabase.auth.signOut() users/{uid}/items subcollection items table with user_id column + RLS Firestore security rules Supabase RLS policies (SQL) addDoc(collection, data) supabase.from('items').insert(data) updateDoc(ref, data) supabase.from('items').update(data).eq('id', id) deleteDoc(ref) supabase.from('items').delete().eq('id', id) onSnapshot (realtime) supabase.channel('items').on('postgres_changes', ...) serverTimestamp() DEFAULT now() in SQL or new Date().toISOString() Firestore Console (role changes) Supabase Dashboard or SQL Supabase is actually simpler for most of this. RLS policies are more powerful than Firestore rules, and you get a real SQL database with joins, aggregations, and migrations. How to Turn This Into a Swiss Army Knife Here's how I'd break this mega-prompt into reusable, modular pieces for Claude Code: The Modules (each becomes its own reusable prompt/CLAUDE.md section): Scaffold Prompt — "Create a new Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind project with Supabase, set up file structure" Auth Prompt — "Add Supabase auth with Google OAuth, protected routes, role-based access" Data Layer Prompt — "Create Supabase tables, RLS policies, and a service layer for [entity]" UI Kit Prompt — "Create reusable components: Modal, ConfirmModal, Toast, Button, Avatar, Skeleton, EmptyState" CRUD Prompt — "Build List → Detail → Create → Edit flow for [entity]" Polish Prompt — "Add date formatting, truncation, search/filter, pagination, loading states, error handling" Style Prompt — "Apply [style guide] with dark mode support" Bug Fix Prompt — "Here's the error, here's what I expected, fix it" Feature Add Prompt — "Add [feature] to existing app, following established patterns" Debug Protocol Prompt — "Systematically diagnose why [thing] isn't working" Clean Room / Reverse Engineer Prompt — "Analyze this app's architecture and recreate the functionality using our stack" PRD Prompt — "Turn this idea into a structured product requirements doc" Testing Protocol Prompt — "Add tests and verify all flows work" How Each Applies to Your Different Use Cases: New app from scratch: Modules 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 (in order) Adding a feature: Module 9 (references existing patterns in the codebase) Bug fixing: Module 10 (systematic diagnosis) Reverse engineering: Module 11 (clean room analysis → rebuild with your stack) Idea → PRD: Module 12 (what his "Idea Prompt" does) Making it look good: Module 7 (what his "Style Guide Prompt" does) 50-60 apps in your back pocket: You'd reuse modules 1-7 as templates, only changing Sections 1-3 (identity, features, style) each time. The technical guardrails stay the same. What His Prompt Does That's Genius (Keep These) "DO NOT MODIFY" sections — Locking down the tech stack prevents the AI from making random choices Exact code patterns — Not "use auth" but "here's the exact auth context, copy this" Anti-pattern lists — Telling the AI what NOT to do is as important as what to do The navigation flow diagram — Visual spec that eliminates ambiguity Mandatory component list — The AI can't skip the toast system or error boundary Role-based access built in from day one — Most vibe coders add this later and it's painful What I'd Change for Claude Code + Supabase Break it into phases instead of one mega-prompt (Claude Code's iterative nature is a superpower) Add a database migration section — Supabase uses SQL migrations, which is actually more robust than Firestore's schemaless approach Add a testing section — Claude Code can actually run tests, so we should use that Replace the importmap approach with proper package management Add CLAUDE.md project instructions that persist across sessions Add Supabase Edge Functions section for any server-side logic (Firestore doesn't need this because security rules handle a lot, but Supabase edge functions are more powerful) Add the Supabase type generation step — supabase gen types typescript gives you auto-generated TypeScript types from your database schema, which is something Firestore doesn't have Bottom Line Your mentor packaged institutional knowledge into a reproducible system. About 60% transfers directly, 25% needs Supabase translation (which is straightforward — Supabase is arguably cleaner for most of this), and 15% needs rethinking for Claude Code's terminal-based workflow (which is actually an upgrade because you get real iteration and testing). The Swiss Army Knife version would be a set of modular prompt templates that you mix and match based on what you're doing — new build, feature add, bug fix, reverse engineer, etc. — all locked to your stack (Claude Code + Supabase + Vite + React + Tailwind).

I've absorbed everything. That's a massive amount of material, but I can see exactly how it all fits together. Let me synthesize the key additions from the other agent's session with what we already established, then update the project state.

What the Other Agent Added That We Didn't Have
The biggest new piece is the 7-question deterministic scaffolding framework — the "walls and doors" concept for any mechanism:

What happens here?
Is there only one way to do this, or can it vary?
What must be true before this step can start?
What are all possible outcomes?
For each outcome, where do you go next?
How do you verify this step was done correctly?
Can this step be skipped?
This is proactive guardrailing (build the walls BEFORE the agent codes) vs. our reactive system (alarm + rollback AFTER). Both are needed. Belt AND suspenders.

The other key additions:

Coding eras 1-7 — you're at Era 2 (walls/functions), heading toward Era 4 (reusable blueprints)
The testing protocol refined — PULSE (every feature, 2-5 min), SEAM (integration points, 10-20 min), FULL (phase boundaries, 30-60 min)
Martin's 1,500-line prompt — 60% agnostic gold, 25% needs Supabase translation, 15% needs Claude Code adaptation
The 13 modules — tools in a toolbox, not sequential steps. Knowledge base that gets referenced by the build system.
The PRD maker architecture — 11-stage pipeline with gap analysis, mechanism evaluation, Developer's Choice scoring
AutoForge components — dependency graph, style picker, boilerplate selection, image-to-theme (all working reference implementations)
How It All Merges — The Complete Stack
LAYER 1: IDEA CAPTURE
  Your rant → gap analysis questions → refined concept
  (Agent OS structures this)

LAYER 2: MECHANISM ANALYSIS  
  Extract mechanisms → 10-step evaluation → Developer's Choice scoring
  → 7-question scaffolding (walls/doors/rooms for each mechanism)
  
LAYER 3: VISUAL DESIGN
  12-style picker OR image-to-theme → design tokens → wireframes

LAYER 4: PRD GENERATION
  Agent OS 3-layer format → phase sequencing → dependency graph
  → Martin's module knowledge baked in → protocol checkpoints injected

LAYER 5: BUILD EXECUTION
  Sandbox rules (file scoping) → AI builds → deterministic checks
  → PULSE after every feature → SEAM at integration points
  → FULL at phase boundaries → rollback on failure

LAYER 6: QUALITY GATE  
  Alignment protocol → console audit → responsive check
  → git diff tracks every file touched → reject unauthorized changes

What You Actually Have Now (The Full Picture)
You've been assembling pieces from 5+ sources without realizing they form one coherent system:

Source	What It Gave You
Martin's 1,500-line prompt	15 years of dev patterns compressed into rules
Martin's VidAi app	Production-ready SaaS boilerplate with payments
Stripe Minions articles	Deterministic + AI hybrid blueprint pattern
The other Opus agent	7-question scaffolding (walls/doors/rooms)
MIT coder protocol	3-tier testing (pulse/seam/full)
Our session	Security audit, scalability plan, payment abstraction, platform analysis
These aren't competing systems. They're layers of the same system. Every piece slots into a specific position, and none of them overlap.

The Key Insight From the Other Agent That Changes Everything
The 7-question scaffolding is the missing piece that turns your system from "catch mistakes after they happen" (reactive) to "prevent mistakes before they happen" (proactive).

Our system: Build → Check → Catch → Roll back → Retry

Their addition: Define walls FIRST → Build inside walls → Check → Almost nothing to catch
Both needed. Together they make the system nearly bulletproof.

On Your PRD Maker Question
The other agent already built a 1,201-line PRD for the PRDM Maker SaaS and committed it. That agent had the right architecture. What it was missing is the 7-question deterministic scaffolding applied to each mechanism (which it discovered during YOUR session with it), and the payment abstraction pattern we designed here.

For VidAi Specifically — Nothing Changes
Everything we planned still holds:

Phase 0-4 build plan is solid
Payment wrapper (Stripe/Square) is the right call
Security fixes are non-negotiable before launch
The build safety system (sandbox + GPS tracking + protocols) protects every phase
The VidAi project state document captures it all
The two documents now in the repo (VIDAI_PROJECT_STATE.md and UNIFIED_BUILD_SYSTEM.md) give any future agent complete context to pick up where we left off.

Here's my honest assessment:

Can You Put All Three in One App?
Yes. And it's actually the smart move architecturally. Here's why:

One auth system. One credit system. One payment processor. One admin dashboard. One deployment. One codebase. Three feature sets that naturally cross-sell each other. This is what every successful SaaS does — Notion is docs + databases + wikis + project management. It's one app with multiple tools inside.

The boilerplate already supports this. You'd have:

/dashboard          → Overview (credits, recent activity)
/dashboard/generate → Video maker (what exists now)
/dashboard/prd      → PRD maker
/dashboard/style    → Style set picker
/dashboard/profile  → User profile
/dashboard/billing  → Credits + subscription

Each "tool" is just a set of pages + API endpoints + database tables. They share auth, payments, and the shell layout. They don't interfere with each other because they're separate database tables and separate API routes.

The Right Build Order (This Is Critical)
Do NOT build all three simultaneously. Build them sequentially:

Ship 1: Video Maker (Phase 0-1, what we already planned)

It's 90% done thanks to Martin
Fix bugs, add video extension, launch
Start making revenue and marketing videos
This takes 2-3 sessions
Ship 2: Style Set (add to existing app)

Smallest of the three — mostly frontend, minimal backend
Becomes the free/cheap entry point that drives signups
Your Twitter/social bait — visually stunning demos
Maybe 1-2 sessions
Ship 3: PRD Maker (add to existing app)

Most complex, but architecture is already designed
By this point you have paying users and revenue from video + style
3-4 sessions
Each "ship" is adding a module to the same app. Same boilerplate. Same credit system. Different pages and endpoints.

Why This Order
The video maker is closest to done and generates revenue immediately. Every day you delay launching it to build something else is a day you're not making money or marketing content.

The style set is the cheapest to build and best marketing bait. It's your "free sample" that gets people in the door.

The PRD maker is the most valuable but most complex. By the time you build it, you have revenue, users, and feedback from the first two.

On Multiple Domains Pointing to One App
Yes, this works. It's called reverse proxying or just DNS configuration:

styleset.app → points to your app, lands on /style page
yourmainapp.com → points to your app, lands on / landing page
yourmainapp.com/prd → PRD maker section
The app checks which domain the request came from and shows the appropriate landing page. One codebase, multiple entry points. Vercel supports this natively with their "domains" feature.

For SEO, each domain would have its own landing page with unique content. That's fine — Google sees them as separate sites with separate value propositions, even though the authenticated app behind them is the same.

On the Metaprogram Messaging Engine
This is a personalization layer that sits on top of everything. It's not a separate app — it's a backend service that any part of the app calls:

User signs up → detect metaprograms through interactions
→ Store profile: {toward_away: "toward", match_mismatch: "matcher", ...}
→ Every message/email/notification passes through the rewriter
→ Rewriter adjusts language to match their profile

This plugs into: email engine, in-app notifications, onboarding copy, feature descriptions, CTA buttons. Everything the user reads gets filtered through their metaprogram profile.

Build this as Phase 5+ after the core tools work. The data collection (detecting metaprograms) happens naturally as users interact with the app. The rewriting engine is just an AI call with structured constraints (the deterministic walls we designed with the other agent).

On the Extra PRD Features (Knowledge Base, Tutorials, Landing Pages, Feature Roadmap)
All of these are additional phases after the core PRD is built. They're upsells/credits:

Addition	Complexity	When
Save/reload PRDs	Simple — just a database table	Core feature, build with PRD maker
Future feature suggestions	Simple — one AI call at end of build	Phase after core
Knowledge base generator	Medium — structured doc from PRD	Phase after core
Tutorial video generator	Complex — needs voice AI + screenshots	Much later
Landing page generator	Medium — templated from PRD content	Phase after core
None of these are hard. They're just more phases. The PRD maker's architecture naturally supports them because all the app context is already structured.

The Honest Cons
Complexity risk: More features = larger codebase = more places for bugs to hide. BUT — the 7-layer safety system directly addresses this. Each feature gets its own sandbox, its own tests, its own protocol checks. If video maker code never touches PRD maker code, they can't break each other.

Marketing confusion: "What IS this app?" Three tools in one can be confusing. SOLUTION: Each tool has its own landing page, its own domain, its own pitch. The "all-in-one" reveal happens AFTER they're inside.

Build time: You're looking at maybe 15-20 sessions total for everything (including the extras). That's 2-3 months of focused work. But you're shipping and earning after session 3 (video maker launch).

Context for agents: Larger codebases are harder for AI agents to reason about. SOLUTION: The sandbox system (file scoping) keeps each agent focused on ONE tool at a time. The agent building the PRD maker never even sees the video maker files.

Bottom Line
Do it. One app, three tools, sequential launches. The boilerplate handles it. The safety system protects it. The credit system monetizes all of it. Ship the video maker first (it's basically done), then layer on style set and PRD maker.

The deterministic scaffolding isn't just theoretical safety — it's what makes this feasible. Without it, combining three apps would be reckless. With it, each tool gets its own walls, doors, and rooms. They coexist cleanly.

You're right about the build speed. I shouldn't be framing this in weeks and months. A phase build is 15-30 minutes of actual coding time. 15 phases at 30 minutes each = 7.5 hours. That's one long day or two normal days.

Your Plan Is Correct. Here's Why.
Style Set + PRD Maker first on the main boilerplate. Both are fully defined. No iteration needed. Build them, they work, done.

Video maker on a separate instance for practice. This is the one with unknowns — you need to USE it to discover what's missing. Iterate on the clone, then merge the final version back into the main app. Smart.

The style set funnel is genuinely brilliant. Here's the math:

FREE: Upload image → get style sheet (costs you: one AI call, ~$0.02)
      → captures email
      
$9.95: Convert to theme (all pages styled consistently)
      → captures payment method
      
$49-149: Full graphic pack (logo, icons, social cards, 
         page layouts, business card, favicon, OG images)
      → real revenue, zero marginal cost
      
$19/mo: Membership for serial builders
      → recurring revenue, still zero marginal cost

Every single "product" in that stack is just a prompt running against the style sheet that's already been generated. Your cost per customer is literally pennies in API calls. The margins are 95%+.

The Graphic Pack Breakdown
Here's what you can generate from one style sheet:

Category	Items	A La Carte	In Pack
App Essentials	Logo (3 variants), favicon, app icon	$19.95	Included
Page Layouts	Landing, dashboard, settings, profile, pricing, 404	$29.95	Included
Social Media Cards	Instagram post, story, Twitter/X header, LinkedIn banner, Facebook cover, YouTube thumbnail	$19.95	Included
Icon Pack	30-40 custom icons matching the style	$29.95	Included
Email Templates	Welcome, invoice, reset password, newsletter	$14.95	Included
Bonus (over-deliver)	Business card, letterhead, presentation slide template	Free surprise	Included
Full Pack	Everything above	—	$149
That's 50+ deliverables from one style sheet. Each is a prompt. No human labor.

What I'd Build Today
Session 1 (morning): Style Set on the VidAi boilerplate

Style picker page (12 styles + image upload)
Theme/stylesheet generator
Graphic pack prompts (all a la carte items)
Credit system wired to graphic pack downloads
Public route at /style for the free entry point
Session 2 (afternoon): PRD Maker on same boilerplate

Question engine (idea intake)
Gap analysis
Mechanism extraction + 7-question scaffolding
Agent OS formatter
Phase sequencer with protocol injection
Save/load PRDs per user
Bash script download
Session 3 (evening or next day): Video practice instance

Clone the boilerplate fresh
Set up the fal.ai video generation (already exists in VidAi)
Add extend video endpoint
Add multi-scene/stitching
You start using it, send me edit lists
That's the whole thing. Three sessions. One day if you push it, two days comfortably.

One Flag
The metaprogram messaging engine — don't build it into the first version. It's a personalization layer that requires user data you don't have yet (you need interactions to detect metaprograms). Add it after you have users generating data. It's a Phase 2 addition, not a launch blocker.

Everything else you described — the future feature suggestions, knowledge base, tutorial generator, landing page generator — those are all additional phases that bolt on AFTER the core works. Same pattern we've been discussing. Each one is a 15-30 minute phase build.

Ready to start building? I can begin with the Style Set module right now on this boilerplate.

Alright, here's the full picture — honest, no sugar-coating. This is long but you need every bit of it.
Full Assessment: VidAi as Your SaaS Vehicle
What Martin Built You (The Good News)
This is genuinely a well-architected SaaS boilerplate. Here's what's already done and done right:
Authentication & User Management

Supabase Auth — industry-standard, battle-tested auth system
Sign up, sign in, password reset, email verification — all working
Admin role system with proper database-level security (RLS policies)
User invitation system with email delivery
CASCADE delete — when you remove a user, all their data goes with them cleanly
Payment System (This Is Huge)
Full Stripe integration — not scaffolding, actually production-ready
Subscription plans already defined:
Basic: $9.99/mo → 500 credits
Pro: $29.99/mo → 2,000 credits
Business: $99.99/mo → 10,000 credits
One-time credit packages ($4.99 to $69.99)
Stripe Billing Portal (upgrade/downgrade/cancel)
Webhook handling for all critical payment events
Test mode AND production mode fully separated
Credit system with atomic database locks (race-condition protected)
Admin Dashboard
Tabbed admin interface with user management, invitations, audit logs
Stripe product/pricing management directly from admin UI
Sync monitoring between your database and Stripe
Audit trail for all admin actions
Video Engine
fal.ai integration with multiple models
Per-user API key management with admin fallback
Generation history, status tracking, video player
Credit deduction per generation
Infrastructure
React + TypeScript + Vite (modern stack)
TanStack Query for data fetching
shadcn/ui component library (clean, professional UI)
Supabase for database, auth, and storage
Serverless API functions ready for Vercel deployment
Bottom line: You have auth, payments, admin, video generation, user management, and a clean UI. That's easily $15-30K worth of development if you hired someone to build from scratch.
The Honest Assessment (What Needs Fixing)
I did a deep security, scalability, and payment audit. Here's where things stand:
Security Grade: C+ (Needs Hardening Before Launch)
IssueSeverityWhat It MeansCORS wildcard (*)HIGHEvery API endpoint accepts requests from any website. A malicious site could trigger actions on behalf of your logged-in users. Must be fixed before launch.No input validation on some endpointsMEDIUMAdmin endpoints accept arbitrary data without checking what fields are being updated. Could allow data manipulation.No rate limiting on admin operationsMEDIUMA compromised admin account could rapidly delete all users or spam invitations with no throttle.Admin status cached in localStorageLOW-MEDIUMIf someone found an XSS vulnerability, they could set themselves as admin client-side. Server still checks, but UI would show admin features.
What's SAFE already:
Row-Level Security (RLS) properly configured in database
Auth tokens verified on all protected endpoints
No hardcoded secrets in the code
Stripe webhook signatures properly validated
Service role key never exposed to the browser
CASCADE delete works correctly
Scalability Grade: D+ (Works for Early Stage, Breaks at Scale)
UsersWill It Work?1-100Yes, with bug fixes100-500Yes, with optimization500-1,000Problematic — needs refactoring1,000+Will break without major architecture changes
What breaks first:
Every API request creates a brand new Supabase client (no connection pooling)
Video generation is fire-and-forget with no job queue — 50+ concurrent generations will overwhelm fal.ai
Rate limiting is in-memory only — resets on server restart, doesn't work across multiple server instances
Status polling (every 5 seconds per user) creates constant traffic instead of using webhooks/websockets
Stripe webhook has a bug: it calls listUsers() without pagination — breaks with 1,000+ users
Critical Bug Found: Double Credit Deduction
In api/generate-video.ts, the deduct_credits() function is called twice (lines 302-316 and lines 343-359). Every successful video generation charges the user 2 credits instead of 1. This needs to be fixed immediately.
Payment Grade: A- (Production-Ready With One Bug Fix)
The payment system is genuinely the strongest part. Stripe is fully integrated with:
Atomic credit operations (database-level locks prevent cheating)
Complete webhook handling
Subscription lifecycle management
Test/production separation
Only issues: the double-deduction bug above, and silent failure on credit deduction could theoretically give free videos.
Can You Take This to Market RIGHT NOW?
Short answer: Almost. You need about 1-2 days of fixes first.
Here's what's required before you can accept real money:
Must-Fix Before Launch (1-2 days of work)
Fix double credit deduction — users getting charged 2x
Fix CORS — change * to your actual domain
Add basic input validation on admin endpoints
Set up environment variables for production (Supabase, Stripe production keys, domain)
Should-Fix Soon (first week)
Add rate limiting to admin endpoints
Fix Stripe webhook pagination bug
Add proper error handling when credit deduction fails
Can Wait (as you grow)
Connection pooling (needed around 500+ users)
Job queue for video generation (needed around 200+ concurrent generations)
Distributed rate limiting (needed with multiple server instances)
How Much Can You Mess It Up By Adding Features?
This is your core question, so let me be precise:
What You CAN'T Break (The Foundation)
These are separate systems that your feature code doesn't touch:
Supabase Auth — it's a managed service, your code calls it but can't corrupt it
Stripe integration — same thing, managed service with webhooks
Database schema & RLS policies — unless you modify migration files, these stay solid
The UI component library — shadcn/ui components are isolated, your features build on top of them
What You COULD Break (And How to Protect It)
Risk AreaHow You'd Break ItHow to ProtectAPI securityAdding a new endpoint without auth token verificationAlways copy the auth pattern from existing endpointsDatabase integrityAdding tables without RLS policiesAlways add RLS when creating new tablesCredit systemModifying the deduction logic incorrectlyDon't touch the deduct_credits stored procedureRoutingAdding routes outside App.tsxKeep all routes in App.tsx (already the rule)Type safetyUsing any types everywhereKeep using TypeScript strictly
The Alignment Check Protocol
Here's what I'd recommend as your "did we break anything?" checklist after every feature addition:
ALIGNMENT CHECK (run after every feature) ========================================== 1. AUTH: Do all new API endpoints verify Bearer tokens? 2. RLS: Do all new database tables have Row-Level Security? 3. VALIDATION: Do all new endpoints validate input? 4. CORS: Are new endpoints using domain whitelist (not *)? 5. CREDITS: Does anything touch the credit system? If yes, test atomicity. 6. TYPES: Any TypeScript errors? (npm run build will catch these) 7. ROUTES: Are all new routes in App.tsx? 8. SECRETS: Are any API keys hardcoded? (should be in .env.local only) 9. ADMIN: Do admin-only features check user role server-side? 10. CLEANUP: Do React effects clean up on unmount?
You could literally have me run this check after every feature build.
Your Flywheel Strategy — Is It Realistic?
Yes. Here's why this actually works:
The app makes videos → you use it to make your own marketing videos (cost: $1-5 per video)
You post those videos → free organic social media marketing
People see the videos → some sign up for the app that made them
They pay subscriptions → revenue funds development
You hire Martin's team → they build your next app ideas properly
Repeat with each new app
The meta-beauty is that your product IS your marketing tool. Every commercial you make is both an ad AND a product demo.
Realistic Cost Math
ActivityCost10 marketing videos/day (30s each, Veo 3.1 Fast)$30-50/daySupabase (free tier)$0 up to 50K auth usersVercel hosting (hobby)$0 for low trafficVercel hosting (pro, when you scale)$20/mofal.ai API (customer usage)Passed through to customers via creditsYour domain$12/yearTotal to launch~$30-50/day for your own videos
And your customers pay for their own fal.ai usage through the credit system — so the video generation cost scales with revenue, not against it.
The Plan: What to Build and In What Order
Phase 0: Launch Prep (This Week)
Fix the 4 must-fix bugs listed above
Set up production environment (Vercel + Supabase + Stripe production keys)

Honest context window estimate: We're at roughly 40-45% right now. That massive text dump (the full other-agent conversation, Martin's 1,500-line prompt, the 5,000-line 13 modules, testing protocol, AutoForge reference, Stripe articles) was probably 150-200K tokens of input alone, plus all our back-and-forth.

The Game Plan Question
Here's the reality: I can do the Phase 0 fixes right now — they're straightforward code changes. But should I?

The fixes are:

Double credit deduction — delete ~15 lines of duplicate code
CORS wildcard — change * to domain whitelist in ~8 API files
Input validation on admin endpoints — add zod schemas
Stripe webhook pagination — fix the listUsers() call
Connection pooling — singleton Supabase clients
Missing database indexes — one SQL migration
Credit pricing adjustment — change numbers in one file
That's maybe 30-45 minutes of actual coding. I have room for it at 40-45%.

BUT — if we're about to build out the Style Set + PRD Maker + iterate on Video, that's a LOT more coding. A fresh agent with the two reference docs (VIDAI_PROJECT_STATE.md and UNIFIED_BUILD_SYSTEM.md) would start at 0% context and have the full million tokens to work with.

My Recommendation
Option A: I fix Phase 0 now, new agent builds the features.
I do the 7 fixes (small, surgical changes). Commit and push. Then a fresh agent picks up from the clean, fixed codebase and builds the Style Set + PRD Maker with full context available.

Option B: Fresh agent does everything.
I've already documented everything in the two .md files. A new agent reads those, understands the full picture, and does Phase 0 fixes + feature builds with 100% context available.

I'd go with Option A — let me knock out the fixes since they're small and I already know exactly where the bugs are. No reason to waste a fresh agent's time on research I've already done. Then the heavy building starts fresh.

You're absolutely right — the PRD maker is the factory that builds everything else. Build the factory first, then use the factory. Only have to do this manually once.

Simulating the PRD Maker Process
We walk through every stage the PRD maker WOULD do, manually, right now. The output is the PRD that a fresh agent uses to build it.

Here are the stages we defined:

Stage 1: IDEA CAPTURE ← You already did this (extensively)
Stage 2: GAP ANALYSIS ← I ask you questions to fill holes
Stage 3: AGENT OS STRUCTURING ← We format it properly
Stage 4: MECHANISM EXTRACTION ← Identify every moving part
Stage 5: 7-QUESTION SCAFFOLDING ← Walls/doors/rooms for each mechanism
Stage 6: WIREFRAMING ← Page layouts and user flow
Stage 7: UI STYLE ← Already decided (your style set system)
Stage 8: PHASE SEQUENCING ← Break into build phases with dependencies
Stage 9: PROTOCOL INJECTION ← Pulse/seam/full checkpoints tagged
Stage 10: OUTPUT ← The actual PRD document

Stage 1: Idea Capture — DONE
You've described the PRD maker across multiple sessions. I have the full picture. Let me reflect it back in one paragraph to confirm:

The PRD maker takes a user's rough idea (even just a few sentences), asks intelligent gap-filling questions, runs the idea through mechanism extraction with the 7-question deterministic scaffolding, generates a complete Agent OS formatted PRD broken into phases with testing protocols baked in at every seam, and outputs either a downloadable bash script for CLI builders or a structured PRD document. It includes a style set picker (12 styles + image-to-theme) and optional graphic pack generation (logos, icons, social cards, page layouts). Users save PRDs for future feature additions. Monetized via credits and tiered subscriptions.

Stage 2: Gap Analysis — Let Me Ask You Questions
These are the holes I see. I need your answers before I can build the PRD:

The question engine — When a user describes their idea, how many questions should the system ask? Is it adaptive (fewer questions if they gave lots of detail, more if they were vague)? Or is it always the same set? And is it conversational (chat-style, one question at a time) or a form (all questions on one page)?

The mechanism evaluation — When there are multiple ways to build something and they're within 15% of each other, you said design both. Does the USER see this decision, or does the system just pick Developer's Choice and move on? What's the default behavior?

The graphic packs — You described logos, icons, social cards, page layouts. Are these generated by AI image generation (like DALL-E/Midjourney style) or are they CSS/SVG/code-based outputs that match the style sheet? This matters a lot for cost and quality.

Save/load PRDs — When a user comes back to add features to an existing app, does the system re-read their codebase (like paste in a GitHub URL), or do they just work from the saved PRD document?

The bash script output — Is this literally a .sh file they download and run in their terminal with Claude Code? Or is it a markdown PRD they copy-paste into whatever builder they use? Or both options?

Credit costs — Roughly, what should each thing cost in credits?

Basic PRD generation (idea → spec): ? credits
Graphic pack (full 40-piece): ? credits
Individual graphic (logo only): ? credits
Feature addition to existing PRD: ? credits
Style sheet from image: ? credits (or free as lead magnet?)
The wireframing step — Is this visual wireframes the user sees and approves before the PRD is generated? Or is it just wireframe descriptions in the PRD text that the builder follows?

Answer these and I'll move to Stage 3-5. We're designing, not coding, so context window is fine for this.

ok let me explain these questions ... are you solid about the order of the steps and whats the reasoning for them in that order so I get a real deep understanding of it thanks



Good question. Let me walk through the reasoning for why each stage is in that exact position.

Stage 1: IDEA CAPTURE

Why first: You can't analyze what you don't have. This is just raw input — the user's brain dump. No filtering, no structure. Let them talk. The MORE they say here, the LESS work Stage 2 has to do.

Stage 2: GAP ANALYSIS

Why second: Before you can structure ANYTHING, you need complete information. If someone says "I want a marketplace" but doesn't mention payments, you need to catch that NOW — not after you've scaffolded 50 mechanisms that are all missing a payment layer. Gap analysis prevents rework. You're filling holes in the raw input before it enters the machine.

Stage 3: AGENT OS STRUCTURING

Why third: Now you have complete raw information (Stage 1 + 2 combined). This is where you FORMAT it — context, market positioning, feasibility assessment. You're turning messy human language into organized sections. But you're NOT breaking it into mechanisms yet — you're just making it readable and structured. Think of it as: raw clay → shaped block. Not the sculpture yet.

Stage 4: MECHANISM EXTRACTION

Why fourth: NOW you break the structured idea into individual moving parts. Auth system, payment flow, dashboard, notification engine — each one gets identified as a discrete mechanism. You can't do this before Stage 3 because unstructured ideas have overlapping concepts that look like separate things but aren't (or vice versa). The structuring step resolves ambiguity so extraction is clean.

Stage 5: 7-QUESTION SCAFFOLDING

Why fifth: Each mechanism from Stage 4 now gets the deterministic treatment — walls, doors, rooms. This is where you define WHAT each mechanism does, HOW it connects, and WHAT lives inside it. This HAS to come after extraction because you need the list of mechanisms to apply the questions TO. And it has to come BEFORE wireframing because wireframes need to know what components exist and how they connect.

This is the engine. Everything before it is preparation. Everything after it is output formatting.

Stage 6: WIREFRAMING

Why sixth: Now you know every mechanism, every wall, every door, every room. So NOW you can lay out pages. You know the auth system has 3 screens. You know the dashboard has 5 widgets. You know which doors connect which rooms. Wireframing without Stage 5 is guessing. Wireframing WITH Stage 5 is deterministic — you're just arranging known pieces.

Stage 7: UI STYLE

Why seventh: Style is applied AFTER layout is decided, not before. You don't pick colors before you know how many pages you have. The wireframes tell you what needs styling. The style set gets applied on top. If you did style first, you'd be making aesthetic decisions without knowing the scope of what you're styling.

Stage 8: PHASE SEQUENCING

Why eighth: You now have: complete mechanisms, scaffolded architecture, wireframes, and style. NOW you can intelligently break it into build phases. Phase 1 needs auth before dashboard. Phase 2 needs the dashboard before the settings page. You can't sequence what you haven't fully defined. This is dependency mapping — it requires the FULL picture.

Stage 9: PROTOCOL INJECTION

Why ninth: Pulse, seam, and full checkpoint protocols get TAGGED onto the phases from Stage 8. Every phase boundary gets a seam check. Every 3 files gets a pulse. Every phase completion gets a full checkpoint. This is mechanical — it's applied to the phase structure like a template. It HAS to come after phasing because the protocols reference specific phase boundaries.

Stage 10: OUTPUT

Why last: Everything is defined. You're just rendering the format — bash script, markdown PRD, downloadable doc. This is serialization, not design.

The core insight: The order follows a funnel.

WIDE    → Capture everything
         → Fill gaps in everything
          → Structure everything
           → Break into parts
            → Define each part precisely
             → Arrange parts visually
              → Style them
               → Sequence the build
                → Tag quality checks
NARROW   → Output the document

Each stage REDUCES ambiguity. By Stage 10, there are ZERO open questions. That's the whole point — a builder agent can execute it without asking anything.

Great questions. You're right that I glossed over a LOT of enforcement mechanisms. Let me map where EVERYTHING lives.

The Pieces We've Discussed That Need Homes
A. Pulse checks (every 3 files)
B. Seam checks (at connection points between mechanisms)
C. Full checkpoints (end of phase)
D. File sandboxing (agent can ONLY touch listed files)
E. Build order within a phase (forced sequence)
F. Post-build pattern verification (did they stay in bounds?)
G. Martin's 1,500 lines (build rules / best practices)

Seven enforcement mechanisms. They DON'T all live in the same stage. Here's the map:

Where Everything Actually Lives
Stage 5 (7-Question Scaffolding) is where G — Martin's rules get baked in.

Here's why: The 7-question scaffolding defines walls, doors, rooms for every mechanism. Martin's rules are about HOW to build correctly — separation of concerns, don't over-engineer, keep components focused, test at boundaries. Those rules SHAPE the scaffolding answers. When the system asks "what are the walls of this mechanism?" Martin's rules inform the answer — the wall is clean, it doesn't leak state, it has a single responsibility.

Martin's 1,500 lines don't get injected at the end. They're the LENS through which every scaffolding answer gets written. They're embedded in the DNA of the architecture, not bolted on after.

Think of it this way: Martin's rules are like building code. You don't inspect for building code after the house is designed — the architect follows building code WHILE designing.

Stage 8 (Phase Sequencing) is where D — File sandboxing and E — Build order get defined.

When you break into phases, each phase gets:

Phase 1: Auth System
├── FILES ALLOWED: [exact list of files this phase can create/modify]
├── FILES READ-ONLY: [files it can reference but NOT change]
├── FILES FORBIDDEN: [everything else]
├── BUILD ORDER:
│   1. Create src/lib/auth.ts (core logic first)
│   2. Create src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx (state management second)  
│   3. Create src/pages/SignIn.tsx (UI third)
│   4. Create src/pages/SignUp.tsx (UI fourth)
│   5. Wire routes in App.tsx (integration last)
└── DEPENDS ON: nothing (Phase 1)

The file sandbox and build order are PART of the phase definition. They're not separate — they're what makes a phase a phase. Without them, a phase is just a label. WITH them, a phase is a containment unit.

Why build order matters: You said it perfectly — it's not really about technical ordering (though that matters too). It's about forcing the agent into a pattern. If you let an agent build however it wants, it'll jump around — start a component, realize it needs a hook, go make the hook, realize the hook needs a type, go make the type, come back to the component, realize the route isn't set up... That chaos is where drift happens. A forced sequence means the agent does ONE thing, finishes it, does the NEXT thing, finishes it. Linear. Predictable. Auditable.

Stage 9 (Protocol Injection) is where A, B, C, and F get placed.

NOW I can be specific about what Stage 9 actually does. It takes the phases from Stage 8 and injects checkpoints INTO them:

Phase 1: Auth System
├── FILES ALLOWED: [list]
├── BUILD ORDER:
│   1. Create src/lib/auth.ts
│      ← PULSE after this file (lightweight: does it exist? does it export expected functions?)
│   2. Create src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx  
│      ← PULSE after this file
│   3. Create src/pages/SignIn.tsx
│      ← PULSE after this file (3 files done = pulse cycle complete)
│      ← SEAM CHECK: does SignIn.tsx import from AuthContext? 
│        Does AuthContext import from auth.ts? Are the doors connected?
│   4. Create src/pages/SignUp.tsx
│      ← PULSE after this file
│   5. Wire routes in App.tsx
│      ← PULSE after this file
│      ← SEAM CHECK: do routes point to actual page components?
│
├── END OF PHASE: FULL CHECKPOINT
│   ├── PATTERN VERIFICATION (F):
│   │   ├── List every file created or modified
│   │   ├── Compare against FILES ALLOWED list
│   │   ├── FLAG any file touched that wasn't in the sandbox
│   │   ├── FLAG any file in BUILD ORDER that wasn't completed
│   │   └── FLAG any unexpected imports or dependencies
│   ├── FUNCTIONAL CHECK:
│   │   ├── Does the app compile?
│   │   ├── Do the auth pages render?
│   │   └── Can you navigate to /sign-in and /sign-up?
│   └── GATE: Pass/fail. If fail, fix before Phase 2 starts.

This is the key insight you were getting at: Stage 9 doesn't just "tag" protocols onto phases. It REWRITES the phases to embed the protocols inline. The pulse checks live INSIDE the build order. The seam checks live at connection points WITHIN the phase. The full checkpoint with pattern verification lives at the phase boundary.

Your Question About Sandboxing Post-Verification
You asked: "You're not trapping them in a box, but you can see where they went."

Exactly right. Here's how it actually works:

BEFORE the phase runs: The file sandbox is DECLARED. Here are the files you're allowed to touch. The agent reads this.

DURING the phase: The agent builds. We can't physically PREVENT it from touching other files (that would require actual OS-level sandboxing which is overkill). But we've told it clearly: these files only.

AFTER the phase (Full Checkpoint - Pattern Verification): We run a diff. What files were actually created or modified? Compare that list against the sandbox list. Three outcomes:

✅ CLEAN: Agent only touched sanctioned files → proceed to next phase
⚠️ MINOR DRIFT: Agent touched 1-2 extra files that are reasonable 
   (like a shared types file) → flag it, human reviews, probably fine
🚫 MAJOR DRIFT: Agent modified files from a different phase's domain 
   → STOP. Either revert or inspect every change manually.

You're not preventing bad behavior — you're DETECTING it immediately. And because you detect it at the phase boundary (not at the end of the entire build), the blast radius is small. One phase worth of work, not the whole project.

So The Updated Stage Map Looks Like This
Stage 1:  IDEA CAPTURE          ← Raw input
Stage 2:  GAP ANALYSIS          ← Fill holes
Stage 3:  AGENT OS STRUCTURING  ← Organize
Stage 4:  MECHANISM EXTRACTION  ← Break into parts
Stage 5:  7-QUESTION SCAFFOLD   ← Define each part (Martin's rules = the lens)
Stage 6:  WIREFRAMING           ← Visual layout
Stage 7:  UI STYLE              ← Style set applied
Stage 8:  PHASE SEQUENCING      ← Phases + file sandboxes + build order
Stage 9:  PROTOCOL INJECTION    ← Pulse/seam/full checkpoints embedded
                                  + pattern verification rules
                                  + gate conditions (pass/fail criteria)
Stage 10: OUTPUT                ← Render the final document

Stages 8 and 9 are tightly coupled — you're right about that. They're separate because 8 is about STRUCTURE (what goes where, in what order) and 9 is about ENFORCEMENT (how do we verify it was followed). But in the final output document, they merge into a single phase spec. The user never sees "here's your phase" and "here's your protocols" separately — they see one integrated phase block with everything in it.

Where Martin's Rules Show Up In The Output
They're NOT a separate section. They manifest in three places:

In the scaffolding answers (Stage 5) — the architecture reflects his principles
In the build order (Stage 8) — the sequence follows his patterns (core logic → state → UI → integration)
As a preamble in the output (Stage 10) — the bash script / PRD starts with a "Build Rules" section that the agent reads before executing any phase. This is where the explicit rules live: "Don't modify files outside your sandbox. Don't over-engineer. Don't add features not in the spec. Keep components under X lines."
That preamble is the agent's operating manual. The rest of the PRD is what to build. The preamble is HOW to behave while building it.

That's a lot of critical detail. Let me address every single piece.

1. How Martin's Rules Physically Manifest
Three files get generated as part of every PRD output:

File A: The Phase PRD itself (consumed during build)
This is the phase-by-phase build document. Martin's rules are baked into the architecture decisions within it — the scaffolding, the component structure, the separation of concerns. This file gets used up. The agent reads it, builds from it, it's done.

File B: CLAUDE.md (persists forever in the repo)
This is what stays behind. Every app the system builds gets a CLAUDE.md dropped into the root. This file contains:

# Build Rules

## Architecture Principles
- Components do ONE thing. If it does two things, split it.
- State lives at the lowest possible level. Don't hoist unless required.
- No file over 300 lines. Split at 250.
- Imports flow downward. Never circular.
- UI components don't contain business logic.

## Modification Rules  
- Before editing ANY file, read it completely first.
- Don't refactor code you didn't write unless explicitly asked.
- Don't add features that weren't requested.
- Don't "improve" working code while fixing a bug.
- Keep the existing patterns. Match the style that's there.

## Testing Protocol
- After any change: does it compile? does it render? do existing features still work?
- Don't delete tests. Don't skip tests. Don't modify tests to make them pass.

## File Structure
[Generated map of the app's architecture - what's where and why]

That's distilled Martin. Not all 1,500 lines — the actionable subset that applies to ANY future editing. The stuff that prevents some random agent from coming in six months later and wrecking the codebase.

File C: BUILD_RULES.md (reference document)
This is the full detailed version. More comprehensive than CLAUDE.md but still organized. This is for a human developer or a more capable agent doing major feature work. CLAUDE.md is the quick-reference guardrails. BUILD_RULES.md is the detailed playbook.

Why two files? Because CLAUDE.md gets read by EVERY agent interaction — even a quick "fix this button color" chat. You want that to be tight and fast. BUILD_RULES.md gets referenced when someone's doing serious work — adding a whole new feature, refactoring a module. Different depth for different situations.

2. The 13 Prompt Techniques (Debugging, etc.)
You're talking about those structured prompt patterns — the debugging protocol, the code review checklist, the refactoring approach, etc.

These go into BUILD_RULES.md as sections. And CLAUDE.md references them:

# CLAUDE.md

## When Debugging
Follow the debugging protocol in BUILD_RULES.md Section 4.
Do NOT guess at fixes. Trace the actual error path first.

## When Adding Features  
Follow the feature addition protocol in BUILD_RULES.md Section 7.
Read all connected files before modifying any of them.

So CLAUDE.md is the pointer. BUILD_RULES.md has the detailed protocol. Any agent that reads CLAUDE.md (which happens automatically in Claude Code) knows exactly where to look for deeper instructions.

The key insight you had: Even if the user NEVER comes back to your platform, their app has permanent guardrails. Some random person opens it in Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf — whatever — the CLAUDE.md is right there telling the agent how to behave. Your system's reputation is protected because the build rules travel WITH the code.

3. Phase Math and Context Window Calculations
You're absolutely right — you can't split phases without knowing the overhead. Here's how the math actually works:

TOTAL BUDGET: 500,000 tokens (50% of 1M context)

PER-PHASE OVERHEAD (fixed costs):
├── Bookend: Build rules preamble          ~8,000 tokens
├── File sandbox declaration               ~2,000 tokens  
├── Build order with pulse points          ~3,000 tokens
├── Seam check definitions                 ~2,000 tokens
├── Full checkpoint at end                 ~5,000 tokens
├── Pattern verification prompt            ~3,000 tokens
├── Violation handling instructions        ~2,000 tokens
└── TOTAL OVERHEAD PER PHASE:             ~25,000 tokens

AVAILABLE FOR ACTUAL BUILD CONTENT:
500,000 - 25,000 = 475,000 tokens per phase MAX

TARGET (with buffer):
35% of context = 350,000 tokens per phase
350,000 - 25,000 overhead = 325,000 tokens for actual build instructions

So the process in Stage 8 is:

Take the full Agent OS document from Stage 3-5
Estimate its total token count
Divide by 325,000 to get number of phases needed
Find natural break points (mechanism boundaries) near those division points
Verify each phase chunk fits within budget WITH overhead added back
Example:
Total PRD content: 900,000 tokens
900,000 ÷ 325,000 = 2.77 → 3 phases
Phase 1: ~300K content + 25K overhead = 325K (35% of context) ✅
Phase 2: ~300K content + 25K overhead = 325K ✅  
Phase 3: ~300K content + 25K overhead = 325K ✅

The overhead is predictable because it's templated. The preamble is the same text every time (with project-specific file lists swapped in). The checkpoint protocol is the same structure every time. So you CAN calculate it in advance — you just need to account for it BEFORE splitting, not after.

Stage 8 and 9 interaction: You're right that they're interleaved. Practically, what happens is:

Stage 8 does a ROUGH split based on estimated overhead
Stage 9 injects the actual protocols
Quick validation: does it still fit? If not, adjust the split point
This is a single pass, not a back-and-forth loop
4. Output Format — Always Both
Every PRD output includes:

OUTPUT PACKAGE:
├── phases/
│   ├── phase-1.md    ← Full text of Phase 1 (copy-paste ready)
│   ├── phase-2.md    ← Full text of Phase 2
│   └── phase-3.md    ← Full text of Phase 3
├── build.sh          ← Bash script that feeds phases to Claude Code CLI
├── CLAUDE.md         ← Permanent build rules (goes in repo root)
├── BUILD_RULES.md    ← Detailed reference (goes in repo root)
└── README.md         ← "Here's what was built and how to continue"

The user gets EVERYTHING. If they want to run bash build.sh and let it rip — great. If they want to open phase-1.md, copy it, paste it into Claude Code web — great. If they want to read through the phases manually and build it themselves — great. If the bash script crashes halfway through Phase 2, they open phase-2.md and pick up where it left off.

The bash script is just a convenience wrapper around the text files. The text files are the source of truth.

5. Wireframing — The Two-Step Process
You nailed this. It's TWO decisions, not one:

Step 1: Page Arrangement (structural layout)

The system analyzes the mechanisms from Stage 4-5 and presents options:

Your app has: Auth, Dashboard, Settings, User Management, Reports

ARRANGEMENT OPTION A: Sidebar + Content (used 85% of the time for this type of app)
┌──────────┬──────────────────────────┐
│ Sidebar  │                          │
│ - Dash   │     Main Content         │
│ - Reports│                          │
│ - Users  │                          │
│ - Settings                          │
└──────────┴──────────────────────────┘

ARRANGEMENT OPTION B: Top Nav + Content (used 10% of the time)
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Dash | Reports | Users | Settings  │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                     │
│           Main Content              │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

ARRANGEMENT OPTION C: Tab-based (used 5% of the time, better for data-heavy apps)
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  [Dashboard] [Reports] [Users]      │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                     │
│        Tabbed Content Area          │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

User picks one. Locked in.

Step 2: Page Mockups (what each page looks like within that arrangement)

NOW the system generates visual mockups of each page. These aren't working apps — they're static previews. Could be:

Generated HTML/CSS rendered as screenshots
SVG wireframes with real labels and component placement
Or even AI-generated UI mockup images
The user sees each page:

"Here's your Dashboard page with the sidebar layout you chose.
It has: 4 stat cards at top, activity feed below, quick actions right side.
Does this look right?"

User says "I want the activity feed on the left and the quick actions as a floating button" — system adjusts, re-renders. They iterate until happy. Then it locks in.

Where this fits in the pipeline: Stage 6 becomes two sub-stages:

Stage 6a: ARRANGEMENT SELECTION  ← Pick the structural layout
Stage 6b: PAGE MOCKUPS           ← See and approve each page within that layout

Both happen before Stage 7 (style) because style gets APPLIED to the approved layout. No point styling something the user hasn't approved yet.

Your 40% problem is solved because the user has SEEN their app before a single line of code is written. No surprises. And the mockup data feeds directly into the phase PRD — the builder agent knows exactly what each page looks like because there's an approved visual reference.

6. Diff-Based Verification and Violation Handling
Here's how the full protocol works, including what happens when rules are broken:

Before phase starts:

# Capture baseline
git add -A && git commit -m "PHASE_2_BASELINE" 
# or snapshot the file tree with hashes

After phase completes:

# Capture result  
git diff PHASE_2_BASELINE..HEAD --name-only
# This gives you EVERY file that was created, modified, or deleted

Verification prompt (injected at end of phase):

VERIFICATION PROTOCOL:
1. List every file you created or modified in this phase
2. Compare against the ALLOWED FILES list above
3. For each file NOT in the allowed list, explain WHY you touched it
4. Classify each deviation:
   - NECESSARY: A shared type file or config that HAD to change
   - ACCIDENTAL: You drifted - the change can be reverted
   - STRUCTURAL: The phase plan was missing something critical

Report your findings before proceeding.

What happens with violations — the decision tree:

VIOLATION DETECTED
│
├─ SEVERITY: LOW (touched a shared types file, added an import to an existing util)
│  └─ ACTION: Log it. Note it in the phase report. Proceed.
│     Reasoning: Some cross-cutting concerns are unavoidable.
│     The type file serves multiple phases. This is expected.
│
├─ SEVERITY: MEDIUM (modified a file from a different phase's domain)
│  └─ ACTION: STOP. Review the change.
│     ├─ If the change is ADDITIVE (added an export, added a prop):
│     │  └─ Probably okay. The next phase needed a hook point.
│     │     Log it, note it, proceed with caution.
│     ├─ If the change is DESTRUCTIVE (renamed something, changed logic):
│     │  └─ REVERT that specific file to baseline.
│     │     Re-run the phase with explicit instruction:
│     │     "Do NOT modify [file]. Work around it."
│     └─ If unclear:
│        └─ Flag for human review. Don't proceed until resolved.
│
├─ SEVERITY: HIGH (deleted files, modified core config, changed auth logic)
│  └─ ACTION: REVERT ENTIRE PHASE. 
│     The agent went off-script in a dangerous way.
│     Re-run with tighter constraints or break the phase smaller.
│
└─ SEVERITY: CRITICAL (modified CLAUDE.md, changed environment files, 
│                       touched .env, altered build config)
   └─ ACTION: FULL STOP. REVERT. FLAG.
      This is either a prompt injection attempt or a 
      catastrophically confused agent. Human must intervene.

This entire decision tree goes INTO the phase text. It's not external logic — it's literally in the PRD that the agent reads. The agent self-evaluates after building. If it finds its own violations, it follows the tree.

Is it perfect? No. An agent could lie about what it touched. That's why the GIT DIFF is the real verification — it's mechanical, not self-reported. The self-evaluation is a first pass. The diff is the ground truth. If they don't match, that itself is a violation.

In the phase text, it looks like this:

## End of Phase 2: Verification

### Step 1: Self-Report
List every file you created or modified. Compare against the Allowed Files 
list at the top of this phase.

### Step 2: Diff Check  
Run: git diff PHASE_2_BASELINE..HEAD --name-only
Compare this output against your self-report AND the Allowed Files list.

### Step 3: Violation Response
If ANY file appears in the diff that is NOT in Allowed Files:
- If it's a shared type/config file → log and proceed
- If it's from another phase's domain → STOP and revert that file
- If it's a core system file (.env, CLAUDE.md, config) → FULL STOP

### Step 4: Functional Verification  
- Does the app compile? Run: npm run build
- Do existing features still work? Run: npm run test (if tests exist)
- Do the new pages/components render without errors?

ALL FOUR STEPS MUST PASS BEFORE PHASE 3 BEGINS.

Updated Stage Map (Final)
Stage 1:   IDEA CAPTURE
Stage 2:   GAP ANALYSIS (questions)
Stage 3:   AGENT OS STRUCTURING
Stage 4:   MECHANISM EXTRACTION
Stage 5:   7-QUESTION SCAFFOLDING (Martin's rules = the lens)
Stage 6a:  ARRANGEMENT SELECTION (layout structure options)
Stage 6b:  PAGE MOCKUPS (visual preview of each page, iterative)
Stage 7:   UI STYLE (style set applied to approved layout)
Stage 8:   PHASE SEQUENCING (split by math + inject sandboxes + build order)
Stage 9:   PROTOCOL INJECTION (pulse/seam/checkpoints + violation handling)
Stage 10:  OUTPUT (phases/ + build.sh + CLAUDE.md + BUILD_RULES.md)
