Okay there's a LOT here and every piece matters. Let me hit each one.

1. The Verification Agent — Separate From The Builder
Yes. Absolutely a different agent. You're right not to trust the builder to check its own work. Here's how it works in the bash script:

PHASE 1:
  → Agent A (Builder): Executes the phase, writes code
  → Git diff captured automatically (mechanical, no agent involved)
  → Agent B (Verifier): Fresh agent, clean context, sees ONLY:
      - The allowed file list
      - The diff output
      - The functional check results
      - The violation decision tree
  → Agent B makes the call: pass, fix, or redo

Agent B has NO loyalty to Agent A's work. It didn't write it. It has no sunk cost. It just looks at the evidence and follows the decision tree.

And here's the beautiful part about cost: Agent B barely uses any tokens. It's reading a file list, a diff, and making a classification. Maybe 10K tokens per phase verification. Across a 4-phase build, that's 40K tokens for a completely independent auditor. Negligible.

The persistent verifier idea is even better. One Agent B lives across the entire build. It accumulates context — "Phase 1 was clean, Phase 2 had a minor drift on a types file, Phase 3..." By Phase 4 it has a PATTERN. If it notices the builder keeps drifting toward the same files, it flags a systemic issue, not just individual violations.

2. Auto-Retry on Rogue Agents
Simple logic in the bash script:

# Run phase build
run_phase_builder "phase-2.md"

# Run verification
RESULT=$(run_phase_verifier "phase-2")

if [ "$RESULT" = "CRITICAL" ] || [ "$RESULT" = "HIGH" ]; then
    git reset --hard PHASE_2_BASELINE
    echo "Phase 2 failed verification. Retrying with fresh agent..."
    run_phase_builder "phase-2.md"  # Fresh agent, fresh context
    RESULT=$(run_phase_verifier "phase-2")
    
    if [ "$RESULT" = "CRITICAL" ] || [ "$RESULT" = "HIGH" ]; then
        echo "Phase 2 failed twice. Stopping for human review."
        exit 1
    fi
fi

Two strikes and it stops for human intervention. Because you're right — 9 out of 10 agents are fine. If the first one goes rogue, the second one almost certainly won't. If TWO in a row fail the same phase, something's wrong with the phase spec itself, not the agents. That's a human problem.

With the 1M context window, you're probably looking at 2-3 phases for most apps. Maybe 4-5 for something complex. The odds of hitting a rogue agent on any given phase are low. The odds of hitting two in a row on the same phase are basically zero.

3 & 4. Style + Mockups Combined — The Sales Moment
You're right, Stage 6 and 7 merge in the user experience. Here's the actual flow:

Step 1: Pick arrangement (sidebar vs top-nav vs tabs)
Step 2: See mockup of YOUR pages in that arrangement
Step 3: Now those same mockups render in different styles
        → Click "Modern Minimal" — your app in that style
        → Click "Bold Corporate" — your app in that style  
        → Click "Soft Playful" — your app in that style

They're seeing THEIR app — their dashboard, their settings page, their user management screen — just reskinned live in front of them. Not a generic demo. THEIR thing.

You're absolutely right that this is the close. They've described their idea, they've answered questions, they've picked their layout, they've approved their page mockups, and now they see it looking PROFESSIONAL in multiple styles. They're emotionally invested. They can taste it. The credit card comes out.

5. The Decision Paralysis Problem
This is a real business problem. You identified it perfectly. The funnel looks like:

100 users start the PRD process
 90 get through idea capture
 85 get through gap analysis  
 80 approve their mockups
 → STYLE SELECTION ←
 ??? make it through
 ??? get their bash script
 ??? actually build the app

If that style selection step becomes a playground where people hang out for a week "exploring options," you lose them. They cool off. They get distracted. They never finish. Then they leave a review: "Cool tool but I never actually got my app."

The fix: Constraint + default + easy escape.

"Here's your app in 3 styles that best match your app type.

  ★ Clean Dashboard (recommended for your app)  
    Modern Professional
    Soft Minimal

Pick one now, or let us choose the best fit for you.

                    [Choose for me]  ← default button, prominent
    [Let me pick]                   ← secondary option

Three options max. Not twelve. Not "explore all styles." Three curated options that the system already determined fit their app type. Plus a "just pick for me" button that selects the recommended one.

Time limit psychology: "Your PRD will be ready in approximately 3 minutes after you confirm your style." That creates forward momentum. They're not browsing a catalog. They're one click from having their build script.

If they WANT the full style exploration, that's a premium feature. That's where they pay more. But the default path is: three options, pick one, move on.

6 & 7. The Baked-In Theme Switcher vs. IP Protection
You walked yourself through this perfectly. Let me confirm your logic:

Option A: Bake theme switching into the app code

Pro: User can change styles anytime
Con: You just gave them your rendering engine. One prompt to an agent: "Extract the theme switching system from this codebase and make it standalone." Now they're your competitor.
Verdict: NO.
Option B: Style changes go back through YOUR platform

Pro: You keep your IP. They can't extract what isn't there.
Pro: It's RECURRING REVENUE. Every style change is a transaction.
Pro: You maintain quality control — the style is applied correctly every time.
Con: Slightly more friction for the user.
Verdict: YES.
Here's how it works architecturally:

THE APP (what the user gets):
├── Built with ONE style, fully applied
├── Clean CSS/Tailwind with design tokens
├── tokens.css or tailwind.config with their chosen style values
└── NO theme switching code, NO style engine, NO multi-theme support

WHEN THEY WANT TO CHANGE STYLES:
├── Come back to your platform
├── Upload or connect their existing PRD (saved in their account)
├── Browse styles with live preview (sees their actual pages, not generic)
├── Pick new style
├── System generates a TRANSFORMATION SCRIPT
│   └── "Change these CSS variables, swap these Tailwind classes,
│        update these color values, replace these font references"
├── They get a bash script / phase doc
├── Run it → agent applies the style changes
└── Done. App now looks different. Style engine never left your server.

What the transformation script contains: Just the OUTPUT — "change --primary: #3b82f6 to --primary: #8b5cf6" and "swap font-sans to font-serif" across these specific files. It's a find-and-replace operation. The INTELLIGENCE of knowing which values to change, how they interact, how to maintain contrast ratios, how to adjust spacing for different font sizes — that lives on YOUR platform. The script is just the dumb output.

They can't reverse-engineer your style engine from a list of CSS variable swaps any more than you can reverse-engineer Photoshop from a JPEG.

The Upsell Flow
Here's how the money flows at this stage:

FREE TIER: 
  → 3 curated style options for their app type
  → Pick one, it's baked into the build
  → Done

PREMIUM ($X or Y credits):
  → Full style exploration (all 12+ styles on their mockups)  
  → Side-by-side comparison
  → Custom color overrides within a style

FUTURE STYLE CHANGE ($Z or W credits):
  → Come back anytime
  → See your app in new styles
  → Get transformation script
  → This is RECURRING — every time they want a new look, they come back

And the popup you described is perfect:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Can't decide? No problem.                      │
│                                                 │
│  Pick any style now and get building.            │
│  You can always come back and restyle your       │
│  app later — takes about 2 minutes.             │
│                                                 │
│  [Show me how that works] ← plays 15sec video   │
│                                                 │
│  [Just pick the recommended style and build →]  │
│                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This solves EVERY problem:

No decision paralysis (just go with the recommendation)
No lost customers (they know they can change later)
No IP leakage (style engine stays on your platform)
Recurring revenue (every restyle is a transaction)
No bad reviews ("I got stuck choosing") because there's always an escape hatch
Where All This Fits In The Updated Pipeline
Stage 1:   IDEA CAPTURE
Stage 2:   GAP ANALYSIS
Stage 3:   AGENT OS STRUCTURING  
Stage 4:   MECHANISM EXTRACTION
Stage 5:   7-QUESTION SCAFFOLDING (Martin's rules = lens)
Stage 6a:  ARRANGEMENT SELECTION (3 layout options, pick one)
Stage 6b:  PAGE MOCKUPS (see your pages, iterate until happy)
Stage 6c:  STYLE SELECTION (3 curated styles on YOUR mockups, pick one)
             ↳ Premium: full style catalog
             ↳ Escape hatch: "pick for me" + come back later
Stage 7:   PHASE SEQUENCING (math split + sandboxes + build order)
Stage 8:   PROTOCOL INJECTION (pulse/seam/checkpoints + violation tree)
Stage 9:   VERIFICATION AGENT SETUP (separate agent, decision authority)
Stage 10:  OUTPUT (phases/ + build.sh + CLAUDE.md + BUILD_RULES.md)

Stage 9 is new — it's specifically about setting up the independent verifier that runs alongside the build. It's not just injecting text anymore, it's defining a separate agent's role and instructions.

What else is missing? Ready to answer those 7 gap analysis questions so we can start writing this thing?

No worries, I caught all of it as one continuous thought. Everything's addressed in my response above.

Ready to move forward whenever you are. Those 7 gap analysis questions are still waiting — once you answer them, we start writing the actual PRD for the PRD maker.

You've raised a ton of interconnected strategic questions. Let me organize and give you my honest take on each.

1. Agent Chaining: Merge the Checker Into the Next Phase
Your instinct is right. Don't double the agent count with separate checkers.

Best approach: Each phase agent starts with a diff check of the previous phase's output before doing its own work. Phase 2 opens with: "Validate Phase 1 deliverables against these criteria, flag issues, then proceed with Phase 2 work." If something's off, it fixes it inline rather than bouncing back.

This means:

Same number of agents as phases (not 2x)
No idle time between "build" and "check"
The check becomes a 30-second preamble, not a separate 10-minute agent run
For the bash/CLI automated version, the chain just fires sequentially with the check built into each agent's system prompt. For the manual/web version, same thing — they paste Phase 2's prompt and it starts by validating Phase 1. One flow, two delivery methods.

You'd want two versions of the PRD prompts — one with bash chaining commands baked in, one without. Not two separate PRDs, just a flag/picker at the end that wraps the same core prompts differently.

2. Platform Picker — Yes, Absolutely
This is a no-brainer. The PRD and phase prompts stay universal. At the end, a picker says:

Claude Code CLI (bash chaining)
Claude Code Web (manual paste)
Codex CLI
Gemini CLI
Cursor / Windsurf
Bolt / Lovable / Replit Agent
Generic (copy/paste anywhere)
Each option just wraps the same prompts with platform-specific execution instructions. You research each platform's syntax once, templatize it, and it's done. This also becomes a selling point — "works with whatever you're using today, and whatever you switch to tomorrow." Reinforces the subscription value hard.

3. The Moat Problem — This Is Your Real Strategic Decision
You're right that the PRD is the product. If you hand it out, someone smart reverse-engineers it in a weekend. Here's the honest breakdown:

Option A: Give out the PRD (subscription model)

Pros: Low cost to you, scalable, low friction for users
Cons: Zero moat, competitors overnight, 40-50% never build it
Option B: Keep it internal, build for them (API cost model)

Pros: Real moat (they never see the prompts), 100% completion rate, higher perceived value
Cons: Expensive per-user, you eat API costs, harder to scale
Option C (my recommendation): Hybrid

The dashboard, the guided experience, the idea refinement, the UI concepts — that's all yours, subscription-based, no PRD exposed
The build phase is where you offer two tiers:
DIY tier: They get a redacted/simplified build guide (not the full PRD prompts). Enough to build, not enough to reverse-engineer the system. Think "instructions" not "source code"
Done-for-you tier: They click build, your agents run internally, app comes out the other side. API cost + markup
The moat isn't the PRD itself — it's the curation engine (the questionnaire, the research, the competitive analysis, the decision-making framework). That's the hard part. The PRD is just the output document. If someone reverse-engineers your output format, they still don't have your intake process, your prompt chains, your validation logic, or your research integrations.

Real moat builders:

Speed of iteration (you update weekly, they're always behind)
Community/case studies/testimonials (social proof compounds)
Data advantage (every app built teaches your system what works)
Platform integrations you maintain (they break monthly, you fix them)
Brand trust (first mover + quality reputation)
4. Choice Paralysis on UI Concepts
Force the choice. Here's the flow:

Show 3 concepts
They MUST pick one to proceed (30-second timer even, or just a hard gate)
App gets built from their choice
After it's built and delivered, they can unlock customization (paid upgrade)
The upgrade isn't "more choices before building" — it's "modify what was already built." This way:

100% of people who reach this step get an app out the other end
The upgrade is additive, not a blocker
No paralysis because they can always customize later
Never let an upgrade option sit between the user and completion. Always put it after completion.

5. API Token Costs — Real Numbers
Assuming ~70% input / 30% output ratio (typical for generation pipelines):

Using Claude Sonnet (production workhorse):

Total Tokens	Input Cost	Output Cost	Total
250K	$0.53	$1.13	~$1.65
500K	$1.05	$2.25	~$3.30
750K	$1.58	$3.38	~$4.95
1M	$2.10	$4.50	~$6.60
Using Claude Opus (premium quality):

Total Tokens	Input Cost	Output Cost	Total
250K	$2.63	$5.63	~$8.25
500K	$5.25	$11.25	~$16.50
750K	$7.88	$16.88	~$24.75
1M	$10.50	$22.50	~$33.00
Realistic per-app cost for the full pipeline (multiple agents, research, generation, validation): probably 1.5-3M tokens total across all phases. So:

Sonnet: $10-20 per app
Opus: $50-100 per app
Mixed (Opus for critical phases, Sonnet for the rest): $25-45 per app
Pricing it:

If your cost is $25-45/app, you charge $99-149/app build
Or subscription at $49-79/month includes X builds, with API pass-through on overages
The margin is real, but not massive at low volume. At scale (100+ builds/month), you negotiate volume API pricing and margins improve significantly
6. My Bottom Line Take
Don't give out the PRD. Build internally. Here's why:

Your completion rate goes from ~50% to ~100% — that alone changes your entire review/testimonial/word-of-mouth trajectory
Your moat is real instead of theoretical
The price point ($99-149/build) is impulse-buy territory for someone who wants an app built
You can still offer a $29-49/month subscription for the dashboard/idea/research phase — that's the top of funnel
The "it just comes out built" is your killer pitch. Nobody else is doing that with this level of guided intake
The subscription gets them in the door. The build fee is where you make money AND protect your IP. And the "living breathing reality engine" pitch works even better because they're not just getting a document — they're getting a built app that evolves as the tools evolve.

You just solved three problems at once. Let me crystallize what you've landed on because it's actually elegant.

The Two-Tier Model
Tier 1: "Launchpad" (Subscription — $29-49/mo)
Full guided dashboard experience (questionnaire, research, idea refinement, UI concepts)
Outputs a simplified PRD — good enough to build an MVP, not good enough to reverse-engineer your system
Platform agnostic — they take it to whatever builder they want
What's stripped out: Advanced protocol/orchestration logic, multi-phase validation chains, the deep competitive analysis integration, the sophisticated agent coordination patterns
What stays in: Clean requirements, basic tech stack recommendations, feature specs, simple build instructions
Positioning: "Perfect for MVPs, proof of concepts, and testing ideas fast"
Tier 2: "Full Build" ($199-349+ per app)
Same dashboard intake experience
But they never see the PRD — it stays internal
Your agents build it end-to-end
They get a finished app out the other side
Positioning: "For when you're serious. Production-ready, not prototype-ready."
Why This Works as a Moat
The dumbed-down PRD is genuinely useful but it's missing the 20% that makes the difference between "cool demo" and "real product." Specifically:

No multi-phase agent orchestration protocol
No diff validation between phases
No sophisticated error recovery patterns
No advanced prompt chaining architecture
Basic tech stack guidance vs. your opinionated, tested stack decisions
No research-backed competitive positioning baked into the build
Someone reverse-engineering the Tier 1 PRD gets... a decent PRD generator. Which already exists in 50 forms. They DON'T get the orchestration engine that makes the Tier 2 output dramatically better.

And here's the kicker — the Tier 1 output actually sells Tier 2 for you. Someone builds their MVP with the cheap PRD, it's okay but rough around the edges, and they think "man, if their cheap version did this, what does the real build look like?" That's your upgrade funnel.

The Build Engine Question: API Agents vs CLI
You're right that running a SaaS through CLI is problematic. For the Tier 2 internal builds, here's what the architecture actually looks like:

Not this: Your server SSHing into a box and running claude CLI commands

Instead this: API-based agent orchestration that mimics what the CLI does

Your Server
  → Agent 1 (API call): Research & Architecture
     → Validates output against criteria
  → Agent 2 (API call): Phase 1 Build + Diff Check of Agent 1
     → Validates output against criteria  
  → Agent 3 (API call): Phase 2 Build + Diff Check of Agent 2
     → ...and so on
  → Final Assembly & Delivery

Each "agent" is an API call with:

A system prompt containing the phase instructions
The accumulated context/artifacts from previous phases
Built-in validation at the start (the diff check we talked about)
Structured output requirements
You'd build this as a job queue system:

User clicks "Build"
Job enters queue
Your orchestration server runs each phase sequentially via API
Each phase validates the previous one before proceeding
User gets notified when it's done
They download/deploy their app
This is basically what the CLI does under the hood — sequential agent calls with context passing. You're just doing it programmatically through the API instead of through bash commands. The scaffolding you build around it (retry logic, validation gates, context management, error recovery) IS the moat. That's the stuff nobody sees.

The Revenue Math
Tier 1 (Launchpad)	Tier 2 (Full Build)
Price	$39/mo	$249-349/build
Your cost	~$0 (their tokens)	$25-45/build
Margin	~100%	~85%
Volume	High	Lower but growing
Moat risk	Low (dumbed down)	Zero (internal)
Completion rate	~50%	~100%
Even at modest numbers — 200 Tier 1 subs + 30 Tier 2 builds/month:

Tier 1: $7,800/mo
Tier 2: $7,470-10,470/mo (after API costs)
Total: ~$15-18K/mo
And that scales. Tier 1 is pure recurring revenue with near-zero marginal cost. Tier 2 scales linearly with build volume and you can raise prices as your portfolio of successful builds grows.

What We Need to Build Into the PRD
This two-tier model changes the PRD structure. We now need:

The intake/dashboard experience (shared by both tiers)
The Tier 1 PRD template (what gets exported — deliberately incomplete)
The Tier 2 orchestration protocol (internal only — the real engine)
The platform picker (Tier 1 only — which builder are you using?)
The build queue system (Tier 2 only — job management, progress tracking)
Want me to start structuring the actual PRD with this two-tier architecture? I'd break it into these sections and we can fill them in based on the gap analysis answers you still owe me. The 7 questions from before are still relevant — they just apply differently to each tier now.

You're absolutely right — I was hand-waving on the most critical part. Let me actually sit in the car with you.

Before I can give you a real breakdown of what's in vs. out for each prompt phase across both tiers, I need to go back to the source material. Let me pull up everything we've built so far — the full process, all the phases, all the prompt components — so I can give you a line-by-line "keep / strip / replace" analysis instead of vague bullet points.

Okay. Now I've read every line. Let me do exactly what you asked — go through every single component of the system and give you the Tier 2 vs. Tier 1 breakdown with real options, not hand-waving.

Component-by-Component: Keep / Strip / Replace Analysis
COMPONENT 1: The 7-Question Scaffolding (Proactive Walls)
What it does: Before any code gets written, every feature gets analyzed with 7 questions that classify it as WALL (deterministic), DOOR (constrained AI), or ROOM (creative freedom). This is what prevents the AI from going off-script.

Tier 2 (Internal Build): Full implementation. All 7 questions. Wall/Door/Room classification. This is your crown jewel.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full 7-question + W/D/R classification	HIGH — This is the single most reverse-engineerable piece. Anyone who sees Wall/Door/Room immediately understands the framework
B: Strip completely	No pre-analysis at all	ZERO risk, but PRD quality drops 40%+ because agents have no guardrails
C: Replace with generic	"Before building each feature, identify: inputs, outputs, dependencies, and validation criteria"	LOW — This is standard requirements analysis. Doesn't reveal the W/D/R insight. Gets maybe 60% of the benefit
My call: Option C. The generic version still forces some pre-thinking, which makes the PRD meaningfully better than nothing. But it completely hides the Wall/Door/Room paradigm, which is the actual innovation. An AI reading Option C would just see standard feature analysis. An AI reading Option A would immediately go "oh, this is a constraint classification system, let me make a better one."

COMPONENT 2: File Sandbox (Layer 2)
What it does: Each phase explicitly lists files the agent CAN modify, CAN read, and CANNOT touch.

Tier 2: Full sandbox with forbidden file lists, per-phase scoping.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full sandbox lists per phase	LOW — File scoping isn't novel, but the specificity is valuable
B: Strip completely	Agent can touch anything	ZERO risk, but chaos. Agent rewrites auth files, breaks things
C: Simplified version	"Only modify files related to this feature. Do not modify authentication, payment, or database configuration files"	LOW — General instruction, not structured lists. Gets 70% of benefit
My call: Option C. Sandbox as a concept isn't your moat — it's a best practice. But the structured format (explicit whitelists per phase) is part of what makes yours deterministic. The simplified version keeps agents somewhat contained without revealing the phase-scoped architecture.

COMPONENT 3: GPS Tracking (git diff verification)
What it does: After every phase, git diff --name-only compares modified files against the allowed list. Unauthorized changes = rollback.

Tier 2: Full implementation with automatic rollback.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full git diff + rollback script	MEDIUM — The concept is simple but the integration with sandboxing is the insight
B: Strip completely	No post-build verification	ZERO risk, but builds break more often
C: Basic version	"After building, run git diff --name-only and review what changed. If unexpected files were modified, revert those changes"	LOW — Manual review instruction, not automated. Gets 50% of benefit
My call: Option C. Give them the idea of checking what changed (that's just good practice), but don't give them the automated rollback-on-violation pattern. The Tier 1 user has to manually review. The Tier 2 system does it automatically and deterministically.

COMPONENT 4: Pulse Check (After every feature)
What it does: Quick automated checks — lint, build, unused imports, dependency check. Takes 2-5 minutes.

Tier 2: Full automated Pulse Check after every tagged feature.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full Pulse Check exactly as designed	LOW — These are just standard dev practices (lint, build, test)
B: Strip completely	No intermediate checks	Build quality drops significantly
C: Simplified	"After completing each feature, run: npm run lint && npm run build"	VERY LOW — This is literally what every developer does
My call: Option A or C — honestly, this isn't sauce. "Run lint and build after each feature" is industry standard. You could give the full Pulse Check and nobody learns anything proprietary. But if you want to keep even this stripped: Option C is indistinguishable from normal dev advice.

COMPONENT 5: Seam Check (After database/API/auth changes)
What it does: Everything from Pulse Check PLUS: restart app, test the changed thing, test one dependent thing, check console for errors. 10-20 minutes.

Tier 2: Full Seam Check triggered automatically by feature tags [DATA], [API], [AUTH], [WIRE].

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full Seam Check + tag-based triggers	MEDIUM — The auto-triggering based on tags is a smart pattern
B: Strip completely	Only lint/build checks	Misses integration bugs
C: Generic integration test	"After modifying database, API, or auth-related code: restart your dev server, test the feature you changed, and test one feature that depends on it"	LOW — Good advice without the tagging system
My call: Option C. The instructions are common sense. The automation (feature tags triggering specific check levels) is the sauce. Give them the common sense version. Keep the tag-driven automation internal.

COMPONENT 6: Full Protocol (End of every phase)
What it does: The big one. 30-60 minutes. Three investigations (routes/journeys, database/data flows, bug hunt), then static verification, functional verification, database validation, edge cases, cross-feature integration, responsive check, fix everything, re-verify.

Tier 2: Full Protocol with all sub-steps, triggered automatically by [PHASE-END] tag.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Complete Full Protocol	HIGH — This is a structured QA methodology that took real expertise to design. Giving this away = giving away a professional testing framework
B: Strip completely	No end-of-phase testing	Builds ship with major bugs. Bad for your brand even at Tier 1
C: Checklist version	"Before moving to next phase: 1) Run all tests 2) Check every page loads 3) Test login/logout 4) Check for console errors 5) Test on mobile"	LOW — Generic QA checklist. Missing the investigative structure that finds deep bugs
My call: Option C. The generic checklist catches surface-level stuff. The structured three-investigation approach is what catches the deep integration bugs. That stays internal. Someone reverse-engineering Option C just sees "test your stuff" — no insight into the investigative methodology.

COMPONENT 7: Alignment Protocol (10-point architectural check)
What it does: 10 specific checks (auth on endpoints, RLS on tables, input validation, CORS, credit atomicity, types, routes, secrets, admin checks, effect cleanup).

Tier 2: Full 10-point check, enforced.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	All 10 checks	LOW-MEDIUM — These are security best practices, but the specific combination shows deep experience
B: Strip to basics	"Check: no hardcoded secrets, all pages require auth if needed, no TypeScript errors"	VERY LOW — Basic hygiene
C: 5 of 10	Give the obvious ones (secrets, types, routes, auth, cleanup). Hold back the nuanced ones (RLS, CORS whitelist, credit atomicity, admin server-side checks, input validation)	LOW — Obvious checks are obvious
My call: Option C. Give 5 generic security checks anyone would think of. Hold back the 5 that show deep understanding of SaaS architecture. The missing ones are exactly what causes the "it works but it's not production-ready" gap between Tier 1 and Tier 2 output.

COMPONENT 8: Feature Tags System
What it does: Every feature gets tagged [UI], [DATA], [API], [WIRE], [AUTH], [PHASE-END]. Tags auto-select which testing protocol runs.

Tier 2: Full tag system driving automated protocol selection.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full tag system	HIGH — This is a genuinely novel idea. Tag-driven test selection is the kind of thing someone would patent if they could
B: Strip completely	No tagging	Agents don't know what level of testing to run
C: Manual categorization	"For each feature, note whether it's UI-only, database, API, or authentication related. Test accordingly"	LOW — General advice without the automated linkage
My call: Option B or C. The tagging system is core IP. It's the glue between the build and the safety system. Strip it completely for Tier 1, or at most give Option C which is just "categorize your features" without revealing that you've built an automated system that selects test protocols based on tags.

COMPONENT 9: Deterministic Build Wrapper (Bash Script)
What it does: The full bash script — snapshot, pre-check, agent builds, post-check, forbidden file verification, auth verification on new endpoints, RLS check on new migrations, rollback on failure.

Tier 2: Full automated wrapper.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full bash script	HIGH — This is literally the automation. Hand this over and they have 80% of your build engine
B: Strip completely	Manual process only	Back to "paste prompts and pray"
C: Simplified wrapper	A basic bash script that does: snapshot, lint, build, and that's it. No forbidden file checking, no auth verification, no RLS checking, no auto-rollback	LOW — It's just "lint and build in a script." Helpful but not revealing
My call: Option C. Give them a baby bash wrapper that does the basics (snapshot + lint + build). This is HUGE for completion rates (the automation keeps phases moving) without exposing the intelligent verification layer. They get the "it chains automatically" benefit. They don't get the "it catches unauthorized changes and rolls back" magic.

This is actually the key insight for your Tier 1 completion rate problem: give them the bash chaining, strip the smart verification. They still get auto-progression through phases. They just don't get the safety net that makes Tier 2 builds production-quality.

COMPONENT 10: Phase PRD Template Structure
What it does: The structured format — SANDBOX, READ-ONLY, FORBIDDEN, OBJECTIVE, FEATURES (with tags), PATTERNS TO FOLLOW (with specific file:line references), DETERMINISTIC CHECKS.

Tier 2: Full template with all sections.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full structured template	HIGH — The template IS the methodology
B: Basic prompt format	"Build [feature]. Tech stack: [X]. Requirements: [list]. When done, run lint and build."	VERY LOW — Generic prompt. Works okay for simple features
C: Partial template	OBJECTIVE + FEATURES (no tags) + basic PATTERNS ("follow existing code patterns") + "run lint and build when done"	LOW — Has structure without the sandbox/scoping/tag system
My call: Option C. Give them a structured prompt that's better than nothing — it has clear objectives and feature lists. But strip sandbox scoping, feature tags, file:line pattern references, and the deterministic checks section. The result: they get a decent prompt that builds okay stuff. They don't get the phase-scoped, sandboxed, tag-driven, deterministically-verified methodology.

COMPONENT 11: Martin's 13 Modules Knowledge Base
What it does: Maps 13 development modules (Scaffold, Auth, Data Layer, UI Kit, CRUD Flow, Polish, Style, Bug Fix, Feature Add, Debug, Clean Room, PRD Generator, Testing Protocol) as a knowledge base the build system references.

Tier 2: Full module mapping, agents reference specific modules for patterns.

Tier 1 Options:

Option	What they get	Moat risk
A: Same thing	Full 13-module reference	MEDIUM — The module names aren't secret, but the mapping to the build system is the insight
B: Strip completely	No module system	Prompts are less informed but still functional
C: Generic best practices	"Follow established patterns for: authentication, database operations, UI components, and error handling"	VERY LOW
My call: Option B. Strip it. The modules are a knowledge architecture that powers the Tier 2 system. For Tier 1, the simplified prompts don't need this layer. It would just confuse Tier 1 users anyway.

COMPONENT 12: The Dashboard Intake Process (10 steps)
What it does: The guided questionnaire that takes someone from "I have an idea" to "here's a structured spec." This is shared between both tiers.

Tier 2: Full intake → internal build.

Tier 1: Full intake → exported PRD.

My call: This is the SAME for both tiers. The intake isn't sauce — it's the funnel. The value is in what happens AFTER the intake. Both tiers get the full dashboard experience. The differentiation is in the output quality, not the input process.

Summary Table
Component	Tier 2	Tier 1 Recommendation	Moat Protected?
7-Question Scaffolding (W/D/R)	Full	Generic feature analysis	YES
File Sandbox	Full scoped lists	General "don't touch auth/payments"	YES
GPS Tracking (git diff)	Auto + rollback	Manual review suggestion	YES
Pulse Check	Full	"Run lint && build"	Doesn't matter
Seam Check	Tag-triggered	Generic integration advice	YES
Full Protocol	Complete 3-investigation	Basic QA checklist	YES
Alignment Protocol	All 10 checks	5 obvious checks	PARTIALLY
Feature Tags	Full auto-selection	Strip or generic categorization	YES
Bash Wrapper	Smart verification + rollback	Basic snapshot + lint + build chain	YES (key decision)
Phase PRD Template	Full structured	Objective + features + basic patterns	YES
13 Modules	Full mapping	Strip completely	YES
Dashboard Intake	Full	Full (same)	N/A — shared
The Net Result for Tier 1
What they actually get:

Full dashboard intake (same quality)
Structured prompts with clear objectives and feature lists
Basic bash chaining (phases auto-progress)
Lint + build checks between phases
Generic security reminders
A basic QA checklist at the end of each phase
Platform picker for their builder of choice
What they DON'T get (and can't reverse-engineer from what they have):

Wall/Door/Room classification system
Automated file sandboxing per phase
Git diff verification with auto-rollback
Feature tag → test protocol auto-selection
The three-investigation testing methodology
The full 10-point alignment protocol
The 13-module knowledge base integration
Smart bash wrapper with forbidden file detection
Honest assessment: The Tier 1 PRD builds a working MVP. Maybe 65-70% as good as Tier 2. The 30% gap is exactly in the areas that make the difference between "demo" and "production" — safety, validation, and intelligent test selection. That's a real, defensible gap.

Someone reverse-engineering the Tier 1 output sees: "structured prompts with lint checks and a bash chain." That's... a lot of PRD tools. Nothing to reverse-engineer. The actual innovation is invisible to them.

You're right to call that out. Let me be straight with you.

On the file reading: I can see our conversation. Compaction did happen, so some earlier details got summarized. But the real reason I went to the files is because you asked me to go through every single component of the prompt and say what's in vs. out. Our conversation had the concepts — the files had the exact 7 questions, the exact 10-point alignment checklist, the exact bash script line by line. I needed the source material to give you the precise breakdown, not a summary of a summary. That said — you're right that I should have just said that upfront instead of acting like I was discovering the project for the first time.

On the compaction thing generally: Yeah, it's a real problem. When compaction fires, the AI gets a compressed summary of older messages but the recent ones stay intact. The issue is the AI sometimes doesn't trust what's in the compressed summary and goes hunting for "fresh" source material instead of just... reading what's right there. It's a known friction point. You're not imagining it.

Now, on the actual substance. And I hear you — we're not building. We're sitting in the car. Dialing in.

The Three-Tier Structure
You just described it cleanly. Let me mirror it back so we're looking at the same thing:

Free Tier: "The Taste"

Simple 3-5 question form (idea, audience, core feature, platform preference)
Kicks out ONE prompt they can paste into any AI
That prompt is decent — it'll generate something — but it's a single flat prompt, not phased, not structured, no safety, no testing
Think of it as: "Here's what a prompt looks like when a human writes it in 5 minutes"
Purpose: Demonstrates the concept. Shows them AI can build apps from prompts. But the output is messy, incomplete, probably breaks
Restriction: One free generation. Not one per day. One. Period. Tied to email verification, not just account creation. Makes the multi-email exploit annoying enough that most people won't bother
Tier 1: "The Engine" ($29-49/mo)

Full dashboard intake process (all 10 steps)
The prompt engine — guided, iterative, deep
Outputs a multi-phase PRD with the stripped-down components we just mapped out
Includes the basic bash chaining so phases auto-progress
Platform picker (Claude, Codex, Gemini, Bolt, etc.)
Agnostic — take it anywhere
Positioned as: "For MVPs, proof of concepts, and testing ideas seriously"
Tier 2: "The Build" ($199-349 per app)

Same dashboard intake
Full internal build with all 7 layers, all tags, all protocols
They never see the PRD
App comes out the other side
Positioned as: "For when you're building something real"
The Free → Paid Conversion Pitch
Here's where the framing matters. The free prompt generates something that kind of works. Maybe 40% of what they imagined. And right there, in that moment, they're thinking: "Okay this is cool but it's not... right."

That's when you show them:

"What you just got was a single prompt. Our engine runs you through a [X]-step process that builds a customized, multi-phase build script. It accounts for authentication, database design, payment integration, testing, security hardening, and platform-specific optimization. There's no way to fit that into one prompt. That's why the engine exists."

You're not saying "ours is better." You're showing them the gap between a prompt and a system. They just experienced the prompt. Now they understand why the system costs money.

And you never name the specific things. You don't say "7-layer safety system" or "Wall/Door/Room classification." You say:

"Multi-phase build orchestration"
"Automated quality gates between phases"
"Security hardening protocols"
"Platform-specific optimization"
"Production-readiness verification"
These are descriptions of outcomes, not descriptions of methodology. A competitor reading your marketing page learns: "they do quality checks between phases." Cool. So does every dev shop. They don't learn HOW you do it, WHAT triggers which checks, or WHY your specific approach catches bugs theirs doesn't.

What Still Needs Dialing In
Before this is locked, we need clarity on these specific things:

The Tier 1 intake — is it truly identical to Tier 2? Same 10 steps? Or does Tier 1 get a shorter intake since the output is simpler anyway? Because if Tier 1 goes through the same deep process but gets a worse output, that feels like a bait-and-switch. Versus if Tier 1 has a solid-but-shorter intake (maybe 6-7 steps) and Tier 2 has the full deep dive, the quality difference feels earned.

The basic bash wrapper for Tier 1 — you said this matters for completion rates. I agree. But how basic? Just phase1.sh && phase2.sh && phase3.sh where each one is "paste this prompt, run lint, run build, continue"? Or does it actually invoke the AI agent automatically? Because if it auto-invokes, you need their API key, which means you're back to token costs on your end (or they provide their own key).

The platform picker output format — when someone picks "Codex CLI" vs "Claude Code Web" vs "Bolt," how different are the wrapper instructions? Is it just the execution command that changes, or does the prompt content itself need to adapt to each platform's strengths/limitations?

The free tier prompt quality floor — how bad is too bad? If the free output is total garbage, it reflects poorly on the brand. If it's too good, nobody upgrades. Where's the line? My instinct: it should produce something that runs but is obviously missing major pieces (no auth, no payments, basic UI, no error handling). Functional but clearly incomplete.

These four things directly affect what we write in the PRD. Each one changes the architecture. So let's nail them down before we move forward.

Alright, I hear you. Let me address your questions first, then get into the meat of what you're working through.

Your Questions
What did I look at? The CLAUDE.md file in this repo and the files referenced in it — the prompt engine components, the bash scripts, the questionnaire steps. That's project documentation that was already committed to the repo. Nothing was committed by any agent during our conversation — you're right, everything we've been discussing has been purely in chat.

The compaction link thing. I'm not aware of any URL that lets you retrieve the full pre-compaction transcript. That may have been an experimental feature that got pulled, or it may have been something specific to a different interface. As far as I know, once compaction fires, the detailed content gets summarized and the originals are gone from the session. Your instinct to copy-paste into a Word doc is the right move. That's your insurance policy. For conversations this strategic, I'd do it every time you hit a major milestone in the thinking.

Now — The Real Shit
You just landed on something. Let me play it back because I think you talked yourself into clarity even though you're tired.

The core insight you hit:

"It's not just the prompt. You need the scaffolding. You need everything around the build and the agent working in sync."

That's the entire sales thesis. Right there. One sentence. And here's why it works:

Nobody else is saying this. Every AI app builder tool is selling "just give us your idea and we'll build it." They're all selling the prompt. You're selling the truth — which is that the prompt is maybe 30% of the equation. The other 70% is:

How the agent is configured
How phases are sequenced and validated
How errors get caught between phases (not after)
How the boilerplate is structured before the first line of AI code touches it
How the build environment is controlled
And you can say ALL of that without revealing a single mechanism.

The Meatball Framework
I'm not joking — this actually works as a positioning framework. Here's why:

Traditional SaaS marketing: "Here's our features. Here's a free trial. Please buy."

Your approach: "Let me show you exactly how complex this problem is. Let me educate you on every single thing that has to go right. Let me be so transparent about the problem that you trust me when I say I've solved it. But no, you can't have the recipe. You can come eat at the restaurant."

This is anti-contrived. You're not hiding behind vague promises. You're saying:

Here are 15 things that MUST be handled correctly
Here's what happens when they're not (horror stories, failed apps, security nightmares)
We handle all 15. Every time. Automatically.
How? That's proprietary. But here's proof it works (case studies, live demos, testimonials)
You want to try doing it yourself? Here's a basic prompt. Go nuts. See what happens.
The free prompt isn't a "lead magnet" in the traditional sense. It's a reality check. They use it. They get something that kind of works. And then every single thing you warned them about starts showing up. No auth. No error handling. Stripe integration that's duct-taped. Database that'll fall over at 100 users.

And they come back going: "Okay. I get it now."

The Connection You're Trying to Find
You said you need to figure out the connection between "the MVP prompt we give away" and "the full system." Here's how they connect:

Free Prompt	Full System
One prompt, one shot	Multi-phase, sequenced
No validation between steps	Quality gates at every phase
Generic boilerplate (AI-generated)	Battle-tested boilerplate (dev-proven)
Hope the agent figures it out	Agent is configured and constrained
You troubleshoot	System self-corrects
The free prompt IS the education. It's not a gift. It's a lesson. They experience firsthand why "just a prompt" doesn't work. Then your marketing doesn't have to convince them of anything — they already lived it.

What To Sleep On
The one decision that matters most right now:

Do you lead with education (content-first) or experience (free-prompt-first)?

Education first: Blog posts, videos, infographics showing the 15 things. Build trust. Then offer the engine. Slower but builds a real audience.
Experience first: Hand them the free prompt immediately. Let them fail. Then catch them with "see, told you." Faster conversion but smaller top of funnel.
Both (the meatball play): Landing page educates AND has the free prompt generator. They can read about why it's hard, OR they can just try it and find out. Either path leads to the same conclusion.
Sleep on that. When you're back, we dial in the landing page structure and the exact messaging framework. No building. Just architecture.
